docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
LIBER*M is a comprehensive, multi-platform framework designed to facilitate the migration of legacy applications to cloud-native Java based environments. It provides a robust set of tools and libraries to streamline the transition process, ensuring minimal disruption and maximum efficiency.

LIBER*M is composed of the following components:

  • LIBER*M Commons: A collection of common packages and utilities for the converted programs. This packages include functionalities such as:
    • program management
    • storage management
    • file i/o (sequential and indexed)
    • E/SQL
    • scripting
    • utilities
  • LIBER*TP: An online framework for migrating CICS and IMS applications.
  • LIBER*Batch: A job execution framework for distributing and orchestrating batch workloads.

LIBER*M components as well as the converted applications support any deployment architecture:

  • on-prem or in private, hybrid, or public cloud (Azure, AWS, GCP, etc.)
  • IaaS, PaaS & SaaS
  • traditional infrastracture (bare-metal, VMs and IaaS).
  • containers (Docker, K8S, etc, EKS, etc.)
  • serveless (Azure Functions, AWS Lambdas, GCP Cloud Functions)

docs.title: Compatibility matrix
docs.text: LIBER*M supports the following components.

docs.title: JDK/JRE
docs.text:
LIBER*M is works with any JDK 21. In particular, it has been certified with:

  • OpenJDK JDK 21
  • Amazon Corretto 21 (recommended for production)

docs.title: Programming Languages
docs.text:
LIBER*M is certified for:

  • Java 21
  • Groovy 4
  • Veryant ISCOBOL 2025-R1

Other JVM languages (i.e. Kotlin or Scala) are supposed to work with LIBER*M but they have never been tested so far.

docs.title: Spring
docs.text:
LIBERTP and LIBERBatch rely on:

  • Spring Boot 3.3
  • Spring Security 6.3
  • Spring Cloud 2023

docs.title: Transaction management
docs.text: LIBER*TP rely on Atomikos(tm) 6.0.

docs.title: Logging
docs.text: LIBER*M logs using SLF4J 2.0. By default, it depends on Logback 1.6.

docs.title: REDIS
docs.text: LIBER*M accesses REDIS using Redission(tm) version 3.48.

docs.title: Relational Databases
docs.text:
LIBERM access relational databases using JDBC drivers. Therefore, any compliant JDBC driver is supposed to work with LIBERM.

LIBER*M has been tested with the following JDBC drivers:

Database JDBC Driver Version IBM DB2 11.5.9.0 SQL Server 12.10.0.jre11 Oracle Database 23.8.0.25.04 PostgreSQL 42.7.6 MySQL 9.3.0

Of course, the selected database must support the JDBC driver used.

docs.title: MQ/Series
docs.text: LIBER*M supports the MQ JMS driver version 3.3.5.

docs.title: Containers
docs.text:
LIBER*M can be hosted in any container that runs a supported JDK.

LIBER*M default container images are based on Amazon Corretto 21 ( public.ecr.aws/amazoncorretto/amazoncorretto:21).

docs.title: Cloud and Containerization technologies
docs.text:
LIBER*M is a 100% Java application therefore it can work with all the most popular containerization and cloud services.

The list of tools and services is huge and includes, for example:

  • On-prem Docker, Kubernetes and OpenShift
  • AWS Lambdas, ECS, EKS, EC2, ElastiCache, AuroraDB and many others
  • GCP GKE, VMs, Cloud Functions, AlloyDB and many others
  • Azure VMs, AKS, Functions, AzureSQL and many others

docs.title: Maven packages
docs.text:
LIBER*M packages are available at https://maven.pkg.github.com/nib-labs/nib-java. In order to use them, you need to add the repository to your pom.xml:

XML
<repositories>
    <repository>
        <id>github</id>
        <url>https://maven.pkg.github.com/nib-labs/nib-java</url>
    </repository>
</repositories>

The repository is hosten on GitHub.com and even if the repository is public, GitHub requires a valid user to download packages. Therefore, you need to add your GitHub credentials to your maven settings.xml file:

XML
<servers>
    <server>
        <id>github</id>
        <username>GITHUB_USERNAME</username>
        <password>GITHUB_PERSONAL_ACCESS_TOKEN</password>
    </server>
</servers>

docs.title: Packages
docs.text:
The following packages are available in the group com.nib:

Package Description nib-commons LIBER*M Common packages and utilities for the converted programs. nib-commons-pgsql Extensions for PostgreSQL database access. nib-commons-mssql Extensions for MS SQL Server database access. nib-commons-mysql Extensions for MySQL database access. nib-commons-db2 Extensions for IBM DB2 database access. nib-commons-oracle Extensions for Oracle Database database access. nib-commons-redis Extensions for REDIS shared memory emulation. nib-commons-cobol Extensions for Veryant isCOBOL. nib-groovy-batch Groovy extensions for job programming. nib-motorhead LIBER*Batch core components nib-motorhead-container LIBER*Batch container nib-supernaut LIBER*TP core components nib-supernaut-redis LIBER*TP REDIS Backbone nib-supernaut-hazelcast LIBER*TP Hazelcast Backbone (experimental) nib-supernaut-keycloak LIBER*TP Keycloak extensions nib-supernaut-container LIBER*TP container nib-supernaut-cobol LIBER*TP extensions and precompiler for Veryant isCOBOL nib-tn3270 TN3270 Gateway core components nib-tn3270-container TN3270 Gateway container nib-utilities Batch utilities

docs.title: Working with Veryant isCOBOL
docs.text:
LIBER*M is a 100% Java framework, therefore it works with any JVM compliant programming language. In the context of a mainframe migration, you may decide not to convert your existing COBOL code to Java but rather retain it using, indeed, a COBOL compiler for the JVM.

LIBER*M provide a set of features to integrate Veryant isCOBOL.

!> isCOBOL is a Veryant product: MLOGICA is not responsible for any defect or malfunction caused by the isCOBOL compiler or runtime. For support on the Veryant product, please refer to your veryant license subscription.

docs.title: Building COBOL programs
docs.text: When compiling cobol programs, it is important to specify as few options to the isCOBOL compiler so to ensure correct cooperation between the generated code and LIBER*M.

docs.title: Batch programs
docs.text:
For batch programs, you need to specify:

Compiler option Description Mandatory -cax Use external file handler yes -cv IBM Compatibility flag yes -cva IBM arithmetic compatibility yes -d Include basic debug information debug -dcii Use IBM sign encoding and IBM COMP sizes yes -dv=0 Initialize to WS to low-values yes -jc Generate the ‘.class’ file yes -jj Generate the ‘.java‘ file debug -jo=-g Passes '-g' to the java compiler debug -m1 Put all of WORKING-STORAGE into a contiguous block of memory. yes -smat Allows the mixing of source files and copybooks formats no

Example:

Bash
-c=iscobol.batch.properties -cax -ce=cpy -cv -cva -d -dcii -dv=0 -jc -jj -lo=list -m1 -od=target/classes -smat -sp=src/main/cobol/cpy

Also, the iscobol.properties passed to the compiler must include:

Properties
iscobol.compiler.outdd=SYSOUT

docs.title: Online programs
docs.text:
For batch programs, you need to specify:

Compiler option Description Mandatory -cv IBM Compatibility flag yes -cva IBM arithmetic compatibility yes -d Include basic debug information debug -dcii Use IBM sign encoding and IBM COMP sizes yes -dv=0 Initialize to WS to low-values yes -jc Generate the ‘.class’ file yes -jj Generate the ‘.java‘ file debug -jo=-g Passes '-g' to the java compiler debug -m1 Put all of WORKING-STORAGE into a contiguous block of memory. yes -smat Allows the mixing of source files and copybooks formats no

Also, the iscobol.properties passed to the compiler must include: Example:

Bash
-c=iscobol.cics.properties -ce=cpy -cv -cva -d -dcii -dv=0 -eo=errs -esme=100 -jc -jj -lo=list -m1 -od=target/classes -smat -sp=src/main/cobol/cpy
Properties
iscobol.compiler.outdd=SYSOUT
iscobol.compiler.custompreproc=com.nib.supernaut.cobol.precompiler.CicsPrecompiler

docs.title: CICS Precompiler
docs.text:
From online COBOL programs you can invoke LIBER*TP API. To do this you leverage the isCOBOL OO COBOL syntaxes.

However, this is not necessary, because LIBERTP provides a CICS precompilers that translates EXEC CICS verbs into the corresponding LIBERTP API.

For the list of CICS command supported by LIBER*TP, see here.

docs.title: E/SQL interface
docs.text:
The E/SQL interface is managed by the isCOBOL compiler, that translates the EXEC SQL statements into corresponding JDBC calls at compile time.

LIBERM components make sure that the isCOBOL generated JDBC commands are executed over the database connections of the running job or transaction. Therefore, you don't have to care about connecting the database or managing the transaction because it is done by the LIBERM framework.

Please note that the isCOBOL precompiler do not alter or manipulate the SQL command you provided. This means that the SQL statement must conform the target database syntax. So, if are migrating a COBOL/DB2 application to Java with PostgreSQL, you must convert the DB2 SQL statements into PGSQL compatible ones. The same applies to SQLCODEs, that are not translated by the isCOBOL runtime.

docs.title: Online Debug
docs.text: 1) Start the run region node with the JVM option -Discobol.rundebug=2 2) Connect the terminal and start the first transaction. COBOL runtime should write the message Debugger listening on port 9999 ... in the log. 3) Attach with the desired debugger (Eclipse or isCOBOL), as described below.

docs.title: isCOBOL debugger
docs.text:
Execute the following command in the isCOBOL Evolve Shell to start the debug session:

Text Only
iscrun -d -r localhost 9999

To debug a specific program:

1) set the breakpoint on the desired program name, thur the menu Breakpoint > Set, select Program and type in the program name. 2) resume the execution with the Run > Continue menu command. 3) use the application normally to reach the desired program: the debugger will stop at the breakpoint.

docs.title: Eclipse
docs.text:
Define a Debug configuration in Eclipse with the following parameters:

  • Type: iSCOBOL remote application
  • Host: localhost
  • Port: 9999

To debug a specific program:

1) connect the remote debugger: when the debug starts and the first program is shown in the eclipse debugger, resume execution with Run > Resume 2) navigate the app as desired and just before entering the transaction than triggers the desired program, suspend the debugger with Run > Suspend 3) now enter the necessary PFKEY to start the next transaction and the debugger will stop at the next COBOL program

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
LIBERM Commons are the core of the LIBERM framework. They provide the basic building blocks for the LIBER*M framework.

Module Description Programs Foundation classes for the program management and configuration Storage In-memory data storage classes IO File Catalog, Sequential and VSAM Files classe ESql E/SQL classes for database access

docs.title: Command line utilities
docs.text:
LIBER*M provides the following command line utilities:

  • nibvsam: file management
  • mh: LIBER*Batch control utility

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
The configuration of the LIBER*M Commons components is done through a properties file to be made available at runtime.

By default, the configuration file is named nib-commons.config and is searched:

  • in the current directory
  • in the root of the classpath

The default can be overridden by setting the environment variable NIB_COMMONS_CONFIG or the system property com.nib.commons.config to the desired file name. If the value is prefixed by file: the configuration file is expected to a file path. If the value is prefixed by classpath: the configuration file is expected to be in the classpath.

For example:

Bash
# java -Dcom.nib.commons.config=classpath:com.acme.my-config.properties -jar my-app.jar
# java -Dcom.nib.commons.config=file:/config/my-config.properties -jar my-app.jar

The following properties are available:

Property Description Default data.root.path The default base path for all sequential data files ${env:DATA_ROOT:-.} spool.root.path The default base path for all spool files ${env:SPOOL_ROOT:-.} physical.lock.enabled If true, sequential files are locked physically false storage.charset The charset used to encode and decode strings ISO-8859-1 storage.zoned.sign.ebcdic If true, the sign of zoned decimal fields is encoded in EBCDIC true storage.ebcdic If true, the storage encodes in EBCDIC by default false storage.longer-redefines Defines the behavior when encountering longer redefines. Options: Ignore, Throw, Allocate Ignore esql.date.format The format used to parse and format DATE columns none esql.timestamp.format The format used to parse and format TIMESTAMP columns none esql.time.format The format used to parse and format TIME columns none pds.ascii.extension When working in EBCDIC mode, PDS members with the specified extension are treated as ASCII .txt cache.mode The cache mode to use. Possible values are NONE, CACHED, MEMORY MEMORY cache.path The path to the cache file. This is only used when cache.mode is CACHED or NONE ./vsam-cache

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
Properties
#
# LIBER*M Commons configuration
#
data.root.path=/data
spool.root.path=/spool
storage.charset=ISO-8859-1
storage.zoned.sign.ebcdic=true
esql.date.format=dd/MM/yyyy
esql.timestamp.format=yyyy-MM-dd-HH.mm.ss.SSSSSS
esql.time.format=HH:mm:ss

docs.title: Programs
docs.text: LIBER*M Commons provides a set of classes to ease the transformation or mainframe procedure logic into pure object-oriented, structured java classes.

docs.title: AbstractProgram
docs.text:
Application programs should extend AbstractProgram to leverage LIBER*M Commons programming facilities. It exposes the field context that provides access to the Context object.

AbstractProgram has an abstract method procedure that application classes extending must implement. This is the program entry point when called.

Java
public class MYPGM01 extends AbstractProgram {

    public MYPGM01(Context rt) {
        super(rt);
    }

    @Override
    protected int procedure(AbstractNField... parms) throws ContextException {
        sysout.display("THIS IS MY MAIN");
        return RETURN_CODE;
    }
}

docs.title: Annotations
docs.text:
AbstractProgram can be annotated with the following annotation:

Annotation Target Purpose @ProgramStorage member the method allocate of the member is allocated when the program object is constructed @ProgramInit method the method is invoked before the procedure method is invoked @RequiresNib classes requires a specific version of LIBER*M @NFieldAliases classes generates a field alias helper class

Example:

Java
@RequiresNib(version = "1.4.0")
@NFieldAliases
public class MYPGM01 extends AbstractProgram {
    SequentialFile outFile;
    NChar outRecord = new NChar(80);

    public MYPGM01(Context rt) {
        super(rt);
    }

    class WorkingStorage extends NGroup {
        public NInteger i = new NInt32();
        public NChar FS = new NChar(2);
        public NChar sysinLine = new NChar(80);
        public class Record01 extends NGroup {
            public NChar text3 = new NChar(80).initial("FIELD 2 / TEXT 3");
            public NChar text4 = new NChar(80).initial("FIELD 2 / TEXT 4");
            public NChar text5 = new NChar(80).initial("FIELD 2 / TEXT 5");

            public class SubField extends NGroup {
                public NChar text10 = new NChar(80).initial("TEXT10");
            }

            SubFiel subfield = new SubField();
        }

        Field2 field2 = new Field2();
    }

    @ProgramStorage
    final WorkingStorage WS = new WorkingStorage();         // auto allocated

    @ProgramInit
    protected void initialize() {                   // post construct method
        inFile = new SequentialFile(context, "INFILE")
                .recordIs(inRecord)
                .fileStatusIs(WS.FS);
    }

    MYPGM01NFieldAliases aliases = new MYPGM01NFieldAliases(this);

    @Override
    protected int procedure(AbstractNField... parms) throws ContextException {
        sysout.display("THIS IS MY MAIN");
        sysout.display("THIS IS TEXT10: ", aliases.text10);
        return RETURN_CODE;
    }
}

docs.title: Context
docs.text:
The Context object exposed methods to:

  • control the program flow (call, load, cancel, goback and stopRun)
  • access the execution environment
  • interface the job context

The AbstractProgram class provides access to the Context object that has been assigned to it. This is done through a field named context.

Java
@Override
protected int procedure(AbstractNField... parms) throws ContextException {
    context.call("PROGRAM2", WS.a, WS.b);
    return RETURN_CODE;
}

docs.title: SYSIN, SYSOUT and SYSLOG
docs.text:
AbstractProgram provides the following fields to access the standard I/O objects:

Field Object type Facility sysin ISysin The SYSIN sysout ISysOut The SYSOUT syslog ISysLog The SYSLOG Java
@Override
protected int procedure(AbstractNField... parms) throws ContextException {
    sysout.display("TEST PROGRAM STARTED");
    parm.setAddress(parms[0].getAddress());
    sysout.display("PARM LEN=", parm.parmLen.getValue());
    sysout.display("PARM TXT=", parm.parmTxt.getString(0, parm.parmLen.getInt()));
    RETURN_CODE = 0;
}

docs.title: Exceptions
docs.text:
LIBER*M Commons provides the following exception to control the program flow:

Exception Purpose AbendException Raises an abend UserAbendException Raises a user abend ExitProgramException Exit the current program and returns to the caller StopRunException Stops the run unit and returns to top level

Those exceptions can be thrown in the application program to exit from the current method/program and let the LIBER*M Commons context handle it properly.

Java
void fileError(String op) {
    String s = String.format("ERROR DETECTED ON [%s] FS=%s", op, WS.FS);
    sysout.display(s);
    throw new UserAbendException(12, false, s);     // will raise an abend
}

docs.title: Entries
docs.text:
A program can register an entry using the registerEntry method of the Context object. Once registered, the entry may be called by any program in the run unit.

Entry methods must match the following interface:

Java
public interface IProcedureDelegate {
    int call(AbstractNField... args);
}

Example: Java

public class ENPGM01 extends AbstractProgram {
    public ENPGM01(Context rt) {
        super(rt);
    }

    @ProgramInit
    protected void initialize() {
        context.registerEntry(this, "MYENTRY", this::myentry);          // registering my entry
    }

    @Override
    protected int procedure(AbstractNField... args) throws ContextException {
        sysout.display("I AM ENPGM01");
        context.call("ENPGM02");
        return RETURN_CODE;
    }

    public int myentry(AbstractNField... args) {
        sysout.display("I AM MYENTRY IN ENPGM01");
        return 0;
    }
}

public class ENPGM02 extends AbstractProgram {
    public ENPGM02(Context rt) {
        super(rt);
    }

    @Override
    protected int procedure(AbstractNField... args) throws ContextException {
        sysout.display("I AM ENPGM02");
        context.call("MYENTRY");        // using the entry registered by my caller
        sysout.display("I AM ENPGM02 AGAIN");
        return RETURN_CODE;
    }

}

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
LIBER*M Commons provide a set of I/O API that replicates mainframe file access mechanisms:

  • File catalog
  • Sequential files
  • Partitioned DataSets (PDS)
  • Generation Data Groups (GDG)
  • VSAM files

The file catalog is meant to store files characteristic for batch and online applications to use.

The sequential files classes reproduce the behavior of QSAM FB abd VB files on top of plain sequential files, so that applications might preserve the existing programming patterns.

The VSAM files classes provide VSAM like file objects, replicating KSDS, RRDS and ESDS files on top of database systems.

Both sequential and VSAM files I/O layers can be customized to alter default behaviors.

docs.title: File Catalog
docs.text:
LIBER*M Commons provides a file catalog meant to store files characteristic. The catalog, per-se, is a database object that contains all the necessary details of a file. The framework allows a seamleass access to the information using convenient API and replicating mainframe features such as cataloguing, dispositions and GDGs.

Currently, the catalog API is implemented on a relational database and is available for:

  • MySQL
  • Oracle
  • PostgreSQL
  • DB2
  • SQL Server

In the future, more databases (SQL and NoSQL) might be supported.

docs.title: Catalog table
docs.text:
The catalog table, NIB_FILE_CATALOG contains the following columns:

Column Description DSN Dataset name CATALOG Catalog name ORG File organization (PS, PO or VSAM) RECFMT Record format (F, FB, VB, LSEQ, etc.) VARFMT Variable header format (IBM or MF) MINRECLEN Minimum record length MAXRECLEN Maximum record length VSAMORG VSAM organisation (KSDS, RRDS or ESDS) KEYOFF Key offset (for KSDS) KEYLEN Key length (for KSDS) RELATED Related DSN (for PATH or AIX) PHYSICAL Physical file GDGLIMIT GDGLIMIT for a Generation Data Group ATTRIBUTES Additional file attributes (as JSON object)

The catalog table is created automatically by the LIBER*M Commons if it does not exist.

docs.title: Catalog management
docs.text:
Catalog can be accessed and modified using:

  • Command line catalog utility nibvsam
  • Groovy catalog utility
  • NIBCAMS utility
  • Catalog API

docs.title: Groovy catalog utility
docs.text:
nibvsam can be invoked directly from a groovy script using the commodity API NibVsamCommandLine.invoke() and providing all the command line parameters supported by nibvsam.

Groovy
def PDS = "AWS.M2.CARDDEMO.CNTL"
def pdsDir = "${System.env['DATA_ROOT']}/${PDS}"
def path = new File(pdsDir);
NibVsamCommandLine.invoke(new String[]{"delete", PDS})
NibVsamCommandLine.invoke(new String[]{"define", "nonvsam", "--recfmt=LSEQ", "--lrecl=80,80", "--pds", "--physical=${pdsDir}", PDS})

docs.title: Catalog API
docs.text:
The Catalog API are exposed by the class com.nib.commons.io.catalog.Catalog. With this class, you can inquire and update the file catalog.

Java
@Override
protected int procedure(AbstractNField... args) throws ContextException {
    var cat = new Catalog(context);               // getting the context catalog API

    var fd = new FileDescriptor()                 // defining a file descriptor for a VSAM file
            .dsn("MY.VSAM.KSDS01")
            .org(FileOrganization.VSAM)
            .recordFormat(RecordFormat.Fixed)
            .recordLength(16, 80)
            .vsam(VsamOrganization.KSDS)
            .primaryKey(0, 8);

    cat.delete(fd.getDsn(), fd.getCatalog());       // delete the file

    cat.define(fd);                                 // create the file

    return 0;
}

A catalog API object can be created passing the current context of the AbstractProgram class, to access the catalog used by the current program/step/script.

Alternatively, you can access another catalog, creating the Catalog object providing a com.nib.commons.esql.DatabaseConnection object representing the connection to the database hosting the catalog you want to access. For example:

Java
String url = "jdbc:postgresql://localhost/postgres?user=postgres&password=postgres&ssl=false&currentSchema=vsam";
DatabaseConnection vsam = new DatabaseConnection("VSAM", DriverManager.getConnection(url));
Catalog catalog = new Catalog(vsam);

docs.title: Application use
docs.text:
Application programs can access the catalog just using the I/O routines:

  • Online: applications can use the dataset (or file) parameter of file I/O commands. LIBER*TP will take care of mapping the dataset name to the corresponding entry in the catalog using the definitions in the FCT
  • Batch: using dd methods in the groovy scripts you can link an entry in the catalog with the internal name used by the application programs, exactly like JCLs do on mainframe.

docs.title: Customization
docs.text:
Sequential and VSAM files can be customized to override default behavior as needed.

Specifically, you can:

  • Specify the storage path for a sequential file
  • Override the target schema for a VSAM file table
  • Define a VSAM catalog entry that references another VSAM table
  • Associate a specific VSAM I/O implementation class with a file

docs.title: Customizing Sequential Files
docs.text:
Sequential datasets can be customized by adding a customization section to the datasets section in the scripting configuration file.

The customization section consists of a list of rules that define:

  • File selection criteria, based on:

    • Dataset name (DSN)
    • Catalog name
    • Record format
    • File organization

    • Customization properties, such as:

    • Physical file path

When a file is created by a batch script and matches the specified criteria, the defined customization properties are applied.

All criteria are evaluated as regular expressions, if provided.

The physical property specifies the target file path. It can include interpolated placeholders (special registers) evaluated at runtime. Supported registers include:

  • DSN: the dataset name of the matched file

docs.title: Example
Customizing VSAM Files
docs.text: For details on customizing VSAM files, refer to the VSAM Files Customization section.

docs.title: File Status
docs.text:
LIBER*M Commons file classes support the same file status values like COBOL:

Condition File Status value SUCCESS 0 NON_UNIQUE_ALTERNATE_INDEX_DUPLICATE_KEY_FOUND 02 INVALID_FIXED_LENGTH_RECORD 04 OPTIONAL_FILE_IS_NOT_PRESENT 05 EOF 10 READ_OUTSIDE_FILE_BOUNDARY 14 KEY_SEQUENCE_ERROR 21 PRIMARY_DUPLICATE_KEY_FOUND 22 RECORD_NOT_FOUND 23 KEY_OUTSIDE_BOUNDARY 24 PERMANENT_IO_ERROR 30 RECORD_OUTSIDE_FILE_BOUNDARY 34 FILE_NOT_PRESENT 35 OPEN_FILE_WITH_WRONG_MODE 37 TRIED_TO_OPEN_A_LOCKED_FILE 38 CONFLICTING_FILE_ATTRIBUTES 39 FILE_ALREADY_OPEN 41 FILE_ALREADY_CLOSED 42 REWRITE_TRIED_BEFORE_A_READ 43 RECORD_HAS_A_DIFFERENT_LENGTH 44 READ_BEYOND_END_OF_FILE 46 FILE_NOT_OPEN_R 47 FILE_NOT_OPEN_W 48 FILE_NOT_OPEN_RW 49 PASSWORD_OR_AUTHORIZATION_FAILED 91 LOGIC_ERROR 92 RECORD_LOCKED 99 ERROR 99

docs.title: Generation Data Groups
docs.text:
LIBER*M Commons supports Generatiion Data Groups (PDS).

GDG can be defined using nibvsam or using the NIBCAMS utility.

They can be accessed passing them on the DD statement, using the parameter gdg to indicate, indeed, the desired generation number.

Groovy
def dsn = "GROOVY.GDG.FB80"

NibVsamCommandLine.invoke(new String[]{"delete", dsn})
NibVsamCommandLine.invoke(new String[]{"define", "gdg", "--limit=10", dsn})

exec "STEP0001", pgm: "TESTHELP", parm: "WRITE", {
    dd "OUTFILE1", dsn: dsn, gdg: 1, lrecl: 80, disp: [NEW, CATLG, DELETE]
}
exec "STEP0002", pgm: "TESTHELP", parm: "WRITE", {
    dd "OUTFILE1", dsn: dsn, gdg: "1", lrecl: 80, disp: [MOD, CATLG, DELETE]
}
exec "STEP0003", pgm: "TESTHELP", parm: "WRITE", {
    dd "OUTFILE1", dsn: dsn, gdg: 2, lrecl: 80, disp: [NEW, CATLG, DELETE]
}

The generation cataloguing system matched the z/OS one and is performed at the end of the job.

docs.title: nibvsam
docs.text: LIBER*M Commons includes a command line tool nibvsam that allows to manage files.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
Bash
Usage: nibvsam [-C=<catalog-url>] [-V=<vsam-url>] [COMMAND]
  -C, --cat-db=<catalog-url> Catalog JDBC URL
  -V, --vsam-db=<vsam-url>   VSAM JDBC URL
Commands:
  define  Defines an entry in the catalog
  delete  Delete a file from catalog
  erase   Erase VSAM file content
  list    List catalog content
  copy    Copies file content
  help    Display help information about the specified command.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: The tool requires LIBER*M Commons configuration file. It can be provided as described in configuration.

docs.title: Setup
docs.text:
To use the nibvsam command line tool, set the following environment variables:

Linux:

Bash
export PATH=$NIB_JAVA_HOME/bin:$PATH
export NIB_COMMONS_CONFIG=/my/config/nib-commons.config

Dos:

Text Only
set PATH=%NIB_JAVA_HOME%\bin;%PATH%
set NIB_COMMONS_CONFIG=/my/config/nib-commons.config

docs.title: Groovy commodity API
docs.text:
nibvsam can be invoked directly from a groovy script using the commodity API NibVsamCommandLine.invoke() and providing all the command line parameters supported by nibvsam.

Groovy
def PDS = "AWS.M2.CARDDEMO.CNTL"
def pdsDir = "${System.env['DATA_ROOT']}/${PDS}"
def vsam = "AWS.M2.USRSEC.KSDS01.CMDLINE"

NibVsamCommandLine.invoke(new String[]{"delete", PDS})
NibVsamCommandLine.invoke(new String[]{"define", "nonvsam", "--recfmt=LSEQ", "--lrecl=80,80", "--pds", "--physical=${pdsDir}", PDS})
NibVsamCommandLine.invoke(new String[]{"define", "cluster", "--io-class=com.nib.regtest.programs.CardDemoUserIOClone", "--ksds", "--lrecl=80,80", "--key=0,8", vsam})

docs.title: Partitioned Datasets
docs.text:
LIBER*M Commons supports partitioned datasets (PDS) and maps them to file system directories, which children files are mapped to PDS members. The children file name must match the member name, without extension.

For example, a PDS named ACME.PDSFILE.LIB and containing the members FILETXT1, COPY0001 and COPY0003, matches the following directory structure:

Text Only
$DATA_ROOT
    |
    + ACME.PDSFILE.LIB
            |
            +- FILETXT1
            +- COPY0001
            +- COPY0003

PDS can be defined using nibvsam. They can be accessed passing them on the DD statement, using the parameter member to indicate, indeed, the desired PDS member.

Example:

Groovy
def PDS = "TEST.PDS.FB80"
def pdsDir = "${System.env['DATA_ROOT']}/${PDS}"            // physical path interpolated for file/dir operation hereafter 
def pdsPhysical = "\${env:DATA_ROOT:-.}/${PDS}"             // physical path correctly escaped for the catalog
def path = new File(pdsDir)

NibVsamCommandLine.invoke(new String[]{"delete", PDS})
NibVsamCommandLine.invoke(new String[]{"define", "nonvsam", "--recfmt=LSEQ", "--lrecl=80,80", "--pds", "--physical=${pdsPhysical}", PDS})
path.mkdir()

exec "STPWRITE", pgm: "WRITFILE", parm: "WRITE", {
    dd "OUTFILE1", dsn: PDS, member: "MEMBER01", disp: [NEW, KEEP, KEEP]
}

exec "STPREAD1", pgm: "READFILE", parm: "READ", {
    dd "INFILE1", dsn: PDS, member: "MEMBER01", disp: [OLD, KEEP, KEEP]
}

docs.title: EBCDIC storage
docs.text:
PDSes are normally used to store data cards, scripts and other "code". When LIBER*M Commons is configured to store data in EBCDIC mode, also PDS are supposed to be stored in EBCDIC. This may be unhandy in all those cases mentioned before, when users and/or developers need to edit the PDS members.

In this case, when referencing a PDS member in a DD, LIBER*M Commons looks first for a file with a special extension (txt by default):

  • if found, it converts the files from ASCII to EBCDIC before passing to the program
  • otherwise, it passes the files to the program AS-IS

This means you can mix EBCDIC and ASCII members at your convenience:

  • ASCII files with the ascii extension
  • EBCDIC files, with no extension

The ascii extension is .txt by default but can be configured in the LIBER*M Commons configuration file.

docs.title: Sequential files
docs.text:
LIBER*M Commons provide the class SequentialFile for sequential file I/O. This class relies on plain O.S. sequential files and supports both PS and PO type datasets.

It supports:

  • fixed length records (FB)
  • variable length records (VB, in both IBM and MicroFocus formats)
  • line sequential records (LSEQ)

docs.title: File Definition
docs.text:
SequentialFile provides fluent API to create and define a file object. This increases the readability and helps former mainframe programmers to familiarize with the target environments.

Like in COBOL, when you declare a sequential file, you need to indicate:

  • the external file name (like on the COBOL ASSIGN) that must match a DD name
  • one or more file records
  • an optional file status field

Example

Java
void files() {
    final NChar type1Record = new NChar(80);
    final NChar type2Record = new NChar(80);
    final NZoned fs = new NZoned(2);

    SequentialFile outFile = new SequentialFile(context, "FBFILE")
            .recordIs(type1Record)
            .recordIs(type2Record)
            .fileStatusIs(fs);
}

docs.title: File Access
docs.text:
Once the file is defined, it can be accessed using familiar I/O API such as open, readNext, write and close.

Please note that the match between internal name and DD name is performed at open time.

Java
void files() {
    final NChar inRec = new NChar(80);
    final NZoned fs = new NZoned(2);

    SequentialFile inFile = new SequentialFile(context, "FBFILE")
            .recordIs(inRec)
            .fileStatusIs(fs);

    outFile.open(OpenMode.Input);

    outFile.write();
    outFile.close();
    //...
    inFile.open(OpenMode.Input);
    inFile.readNext();
    inFile.close();
}

docs.title: Error management
docs.text: SequentialFile offers different mechanisms to check and manage I/O responses. These mechanisms can be combined.

docs.title: File Status
docs.text:
This is the mechanism that mimics the COBOL file management. If the file is declared with a 2 bytes file status field, the latter can be used after every I/O operation to check the status of the operation.

Java
void files() throws IOException {
    final NChar inRec = new NChar(80);
    final NZoned fs = new NZoned(2);

    SequentialFile inFile = new SequentialFile(context, "FBFILE")
            .recordIs(inRec)
            .fileStatusIs(fs);

    inFile.open(OpenMode.Input);
    if (!fs.isZero()) {
        throw new RuntimeException("Error opening file");
    }
    while (true) {
        inFile.readNext();
        switch (fs.getInt()) {
            case 10:            // end of file detected
                inFile.close();
                return;
            case 0:             // record correctly read
                processRecord();
                break;
            default:            // error detected   
                throw new IOException("error reading file FS=" + fs);
        }
    }
}

LIBER*M Commons file classes support the same file status as COBOL. See File Status.

docs.title: I/O response
docs.text:
Every I/O returns a FileIOResponse object, which provides methods to verify the operation result.

Java
void files() throws IOException {
    var resp = inFile.open(OpenMode.Input);
    if (!resp.isSuccess()) {
        throw new IOException("Error opening file");
    } else {
        System.out.println("File successfully open");
    }
    while (resp.isSuccess()) {
        resp = inFile.readNext();
        if (resp.isEof()) break;
        if (resp.isSuccess()) {
            processRecord();
        } else {
            throw new IOException("error reading file FS=" + resp.getFs());
        }
    }
    inFile.close();
}

FileIOResponse also provide fluent methods activate callbacks on specific conditions, somehow like COBOL clauses such as AT END or INVALID KEY.

Java
void files() throws IOException {
    var resp = inFile.open(OpenMode.Input)
            .onSuccess(() -> {
                System.out.println("File successfully open");
            })
            .onFailure(() -> {
                throw new IOException("Error opening file");
            });
    while (resp.isSuccess()) {
        boolean eof = false;
        resp = inFile.readNext()
                .onSuccess(() -> {
                    processRecord();
                })
                .atEnd(() -> {
                    eof = true;
                })
                .onFailure(() -> {
                    if (!eof) throw new IOException("error reading file FS=" + fs);
                });
    }
}

docs.title: Exception handling
docs.text:
By default, file classes do not throw exceptions when an error is detected during an I/O operation. This feature can be turned at object creation time, turning on exceptions management with the method exceptionsEnabled().

Java
void files() throws IOException {
    SequentialFile inFile = new SequentialFile(context, "FBFILE")
            .recordIs(inRec)
            .exceptionEnabled()                 // turning on exceptions throwing
            .fileStatusIs(fs);
    try {
        inFile.open(OpenMode.Input);
        while (true) {
            inFile.readNext();
            processRecord();
        }
    } catch (EndOfFileException eof) {
        // EOF, nothing to do
    } catch (FileIOException e) {
        throw new IOException("error reading file FS=" + e.getStatus());
    } finally {
        if (inFile.isOpen()) {
            inFile.close();
        }
    }
}

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
LIBER*M Commons provides file classes that reproduce the behavior of VSAM on top of a database, supporting:

  • KSDS (CLUSTERs and AIXes)
  • ESDS
  • RRDS
  • PATH
VSAM organization File class KSDS (ClUSTER & AIX) KeySequentialFile ESDS EntrySequentialFile RRDS RecordRelativeFile

The following relational databases are supported:

  • PostgreSQL
  • IBM DB2
  • Oracle Databases
  • MySQL
  • Microsoft SQL Server
  • CocrouchDB

In future version, some NoSQL databases are planned to be supported too.

docs.title: VSAM File Customization
docs.text:
VSAM files can be customized to override default behavior and support more flexible deployment scenarios.

Specifically, you can:

  • Override the database schema in which a VSAM table is created
  • Create a catalog entry that links to an existing VSAM table
  • Associate a custom VSAM I/O class for specific files

docs.title: Schema Override
docs.text:
Schema override allows a VSAM table to be created in a schema different from the default associated with the VSAM connection used by the script.

This enables segregation of table ownership across users or applications. It is particularly useful in environments where multiple applications or users share the same database and VSAM catalog, but require access control and schema isolation.

Note: The script must be executed with a database connection that has permission to create objects in the target schema.

docs.title: File Linking
docs.text:
File linking is a mechanism to register only the catalog entry for a file, associating it with an existing VSAM table.

This supports scenarios where each application or user operates with its own database or schema, yet needs to reference shared data from another source.

Typical use cases include:

  • Referencing a table with a different name (e.g., two files with different names share the same underlying data)
  • Linking to a table located in a different schema within the same database
  • Linking to a synonym or foreign table (e.g., accessing VSAM data stored in a remote or external database)

docs.title: Custom I/O Classes
docs.text: Custom I/O classes allow you to define a specific I/O implementation for a VSAM file. For more information, refer to the I/O Class documentation.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
VSAM files are customized by adding a customization section under the vsam block in the scripting configuration file.

The customization list defines:

  • File selection criteria:

    • Dataset name (DSN)
    • Catalog name
    • Record format
    • File organization

    • Customization properties:

    • Physical name

    • Target schema
    • Link mode
    • Custom I/O class

When a file is created by a batch script and matches the selection criteria, the corresponding customization properties are applied.

Selection criteria are evaluated as regular expressions, if provided.

The physical name may include interpolated variables (special registers), evaluated at runtime. Supported registers include:

  • FLW: the first-level qualifier of the file's DSN

docs.title: Example
File Access
docs.text:
LIBER*M Commons provide the following classes for VSAM file I/O:

VSAM organization VSAM File class KSDS (ClUSTER & AIX) KeySequentialFile ESDS EntrySequentialFile RRDS RecordRelativeFile

These classes rely on the physical storage describe here. It supports:

docs.title: File Definition
docs.text:
VSAM file classes provides fluent API to create and define a file object. This increases the readability and helps former mainframe programmers to familiarize with the target environments.

Like in COBOL, when you declare a sequential file, you need to indicate:

  • the external file name (like on the COBOL ASSIGN) that must match a DD name
  • one or more file records
  • an optional file status field
  • an optional key definition, for KSDS files
  • an optional alternate key definition, for KSDS files

Example

Java
static class MyRecord extends NGroup {
    public NZoned primaryKey = new NChar(8);
    public NZoned altKey = new NInt32();
    public NChar recordData = new NChar(20);
}
void vsam() {
    final MyRecord rec = new MyRecord();
    KeySequentialFile f =  new KeySequentialFile(this, "VSAM01")
            .recordIs(rec)
            .recordKeyIs(rec.primaryKey)
            .alternateRecordKeyIs(rec.altKey);
}

docs.title: File Access
docs.text:
Once the file is defined, it can be accessed using familiar I/O API such as open, read, write and close.

Please note that the match between internal name and DD name is performed at open time.

Java
void files() {
    final MyRecord rec = new MyRecord();
    KeySequentialFile f =  new KeySequentialFile(vsam, vsamKsdsFile01)
            .recordIs(rec)
            .recordKeyIs(rec.primaryKey)
            .alternateRecordKeyIs(rec.altKey);

    FileIOResponse resp=f.open(OpenMode.Input);
    assertTrue(resp.isSuccess());

    rec.primaryKey.setValue("RECORDAA");
    f.start(rec.primaryKey, KeyIs.GTEQ).onFailure(() -> {
        throw new IOException();
    });

    f.readNext().onFailure(() -> {
        throw new IOException();
    });

    rec.altkey.setValue(123);

    f.start(rec.altkey, KeyIs.GTEQ).onFailure(() -> {
        throw new IOException();
    });
    f.readNext().onFailure(() -> {
        throw new IOException();
    });
}

docs.title: Error management
docs.text:
VSAM file classes offers the same mechanisms of SequentialFile to check and manage I/O responses.

See sequential files for further information.

docs.title: File Status
docs.text: File statuses are supported. See File Status.

docs.title: Customized VSAM structures
docs.text:
VSAM files can be mapped to custom user-defined storage structures, with full control over data management delegated to the user.

This approach allows storing the content of a single VSAM file across one or more database tables, potentially following a structured data model—for example, a table with individual columns representing each field of the original record layout.

To enable this functionality, the user must supply a custom I/O class, i.e., a Java class that implements the required low-level I/O operations.

The I/O class must be provided for the VSAM cluster definition. AIX and PATHs will use the same I/O class as the related cluster.

docs.title: Cluster definition
docs.text:
The I/O class must be specified in the cluster definition, either:

  • providing the IOCLASS parameter in the NIBCAMS1 DEFINE CLUSTER command, or
  • by using the --io-class option in the nibvsam command line tool.

Example with NIBCAMS1:

Groovy
exec "DEFCLUST", pgm: "IDCAMS", {
    dd "SYSPRINT", sysout: "*"
    // Instream content is expanded below
    dd "SYSIN", instream: [
            " DEFINE   CLUSTER (NAME(TEST.IOCLASS.USRSEC.KSDS01.KSDS)     -",
            "                    KEYS(8,0)                                 -",
            "                    RECORDSIZE(80,80)                         -",
            "                    REUSE                                     -",
            "                    INDEXED                                   -",
            "                    IOCLASS(org.sample.batch.CardDemoUserIO)  -",
            "                    TRACKS(45,15)                             -",
            "                    FREESPACE(10,15)                          -",
            "                    CISZ(8192))                               -",
            "          DATA    (NAME(TEST.IOCLASS.USRSEC.KSDS01.KSDS.DAT)) -",
            "          INDEX   (NAME(TEST.IOCLASS.USRSEC.KSDS01.KSDS.IDX))"
    ]
}

Example with nibvsam command line tool:

Bash
nibvsam define cluster --io-class=com.nib.regtest.programs.CardDemoUserIOClone \
        --ksds \
        --lrecl=80,80 \
        --key=0,8 \
        TEST.IOCLASS.USRSEC.KSDS01.KSDS

docs.title: Class loading
docs.text: The I/O class must be made available in the default classpath of the JVM running the LIBER*M framework.

docs.title: IVsamApi Interface Summary
docs.text:
All I/O classes must implement the com.nib.commons.io.vsam.IVsamApi interface.

As an interface, all methods defined in IVsamApi must be implemented, even if some are not invoked at runtime. For instance, an I/O class designed for KSDS files is required to implement the entire interface; however, only the KSDS-specific methods will be utilized in practice. Methods related to other formats—such as those using RRN—will not be called and can therefore be implemented as no-ops or with empty bodies

The table below provides a summary of the expected behavior for each method.

Method Description defineCluster(FileDescriptor fd) Defines a new VSAM cluster. defineAix(FileDescriptor fd, KsdsKey kf) Defines an Alternate Index (AIX). deleteCluster(FileDescriptor fd) Deletes a VSAM cluster. deleteAix(FileDescriptor fd) Deletes an Alternate Index. alterCluster(FileDescriptor ofd, FileDescriptor nfd) Alters an existing cluster with a new configuration. rebuildIndexes(FileDescriptor fd) Rebuilds indexes for a VSAM file. open(OpenMode openMode, LockMode lockMode) Opens the VSAM file with specific modes. close() Closes the file. count(FileDescriptor fd) Returns number of records in the file. getNextRbaToAppend() Retrieves the next available RBA. read(KsdsKey keyDef, byte[] keyValue, byte[] recordBuffer, KeyIs mode, boolean update) Reads a record by key (KSDS) read(Rrn rrn, byte[] recordBuffer, KeyIs mode, boolean update) Reads a record by RRN (RRDS) read(Rba rba, byte[] recordBuffer, KeyIs mode, boolean update) Reads a record by RBA (ESDS) write(byte[] record, int reclen) Writes a record. write(Rrn rrn, byte[] record, int reclen) Writes a record at a specific RRN. write(Rba rba, byte[] record, int reclen) Writes a record at a specific RBA. rewrite(KsdsKey keyDef, byte[] key, byte[] record, int reclen) Rewrites a record by key. rewrite(Rrn rrn, byte[] record, int reclen) Rewrites a record by RRN. rewrite(Rba rba, byte[] record, int reclen) Rewrites a record by RBA. delete(KsdsKey keyDef, byte[] keyValue, boolean generic) Deletes one or more records by key. void delete(Rrn rrn) Deletes a record by RRN. exportData(SequentialFile target, boolean append) Exports file content to a sequential file. importData(SequentialFile source, int batchSize) Imports records from a sequential file. retrieveLastRrnWritten() Returns the last written Relative Record Number (RRN).

docs.title: AbstractIOClass facility
docs.text:
To simplify the development of custom I/O classes and reduce boilerplate code, the framework provides the com.nib.commons.io.vsam.AbstractIOClass base class.

This abstract class offers default implementations for all methods defined in the IVsamApi interface, allowing developers to override only the methods relevant to their specific use case. This improves both readability and maintainability of I/O class implementations.

Each default implementation in AbstractIOClass throws an exception at runtime. This design ensures that if a method is unintentionally left unimplemented but invoked during execution, it will fail explicitly—helping developers quickly identify and address missing overrides.

Below is an example of a custom I/O class extending AbstractIOClass, focused on persisting VSAM records into a relational database.

Java
package com.nib.regtest.programs;

import com.nib.commons.io.*;
import com.nib.commons.io.Record;
import com.nib.commons.io.exceptions.EndOfFileException;
import com.nib.commons.io.exceptions.FileIOException;
import com.nib.commons.io.vsam.*;
import com.nib.commons.storage.Memory;
import com.nib.commons.utils.NotImplementedException;

import java.sql.*;
import java.util.Arrays;

public class CardDemoUserIO extends AbstractIOClass {

    public static final String SQL_TRUNCATE = "truncate table regtest_carddemo_users";
    public static final String SELECT_1_PRIMARY_KEY = "select * from regtest_carddemo_users where USER_ID %s ? order by USER_ID %s limit 1 %s";
    public static final String SELECT_1_AIX_1 = "select * from regtest_carddemo_users where LAST_NAME %s ? order by LAST_NAME %s limit 1 %s";

    public CardDemoUserIO(AbstractFile file, Connection conn) {
        super(file, conn);
    }

    static final String SQL_INSERT = "insert into regtest_carddemo_users values (?, ?, ?, ?, ?)";
    static final String SQL_CREATE_CLUSTER = """
            create table regtest_carddemo_users
            (
                user_id CHAR(8) not null primary key,
                first_name varchar(20),
                last_name varchar(20),
                pass char(8) not null,
                user_type char(1)
            );
            """;
    static final String SQL_CREATE_AIX = """
            create index regtest_carddemo_users_surname
                      on regtest_carddemo_users (last_name)
            """;
    static final String SQL_UPDATE = """
            update regtest_carddemo_users set 
                user_id = ?,
                first_name = ?,
                last_name = ?,
                pass = ?,
                user_type = ?
            where user_id = ?
            """;

    static final String SQL_DELETE_EQUAL = "delete from regtest_carddemo_users where user_id = ?";
    static final String SQL_DELETE_GENERIC = "delete from regtest_carddemo_users where user_id >= ?";
    static final String SQL_SELECT_ALL = "select * from regtest_carddemo_users order by user_id";

    @Override
    public void deleteCluster(FileDescriptor fd) throws FileIOException {
        try (Statement stm = connection.createStatement()) {
            String createSql = "drop table regtest_carddemo_users";
            stm.execute(createSql);
        } catch (SQLException sqle) {
            throw new FileIOException(fd, FileStatus.ERROR, "error deleting table: " + sqle.getMessage(), sqle);
        } catch (Exception e) {
            throw new FileIOException(fd, FileStatus.ERROR, "error deleting table: " + e.getMessage(), e);
        }
    }

    @Override
    public void rebuildIndexes(FileDescriptor fd) {
    }

    @Override
    public void defineCluster(FileDescriptor fd) throws FileIOException {
        try (Statement stm = connection.createStatement()) {
            stm.execute(SQL_CREATE_CLUSTER);
        } catch (Exception e) {
            throw new FileIOException(fd, FileStatus.ERROR, "Error creating table: " + e.getMessage(), e);
        }
    }

    @Override
    public void defineAix(FileDescriptor fd, KsdsKey keyDef) throws FileIOException {
        String idx = keyDef.getName();
        String sql = SQL_CREATE_AIX;
        try (Statement stm = connection.createStatement()) {
            stm.execute(sql);
        } catch (Exception e) {
            throw new FileIOException(fd, FileStatus.ERROR, "Error creating AIX: " + e.getMessage(), e);
        }
    }

    @Override
    public void open(OpenMode openMode, LockMode lockMode) throws FileIOException {
        if (!tableExists("regtest_carddemo_users")) {
            throw new TableNotFoundException(file);
        }
        if (openMode == OpenMode.Output) {
            truncate();
        }
    }

    boolean tableExists(String table) throws FileIOException {
        String sql = String.format("""
                        SELECT COUNT(*) FROM INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TABLES 
                            WHERE TABLE_SCHEMA=CURRENT_SCHEMA AND UPPER(TABLE_NAME)='%s'
                        """,
                table.toUpperCase());
        try (Statement stm = connection.createStatement()) {
            var q = stm.executeQuery(sql);
            if (q.next()) {
                return q.getInt(1) > 0;
            }
        } catch (SQLException e) {
            throw new FileIOException(file, FileStatus.ERROR, "Error checking table existance: " + e.getMessage(), e);
        }
        return false;
    }

    protected void truncate() {
        try (Statement stm = connection.createStatement()) {
            String sql = SQL_TRUNCATE;
            log.debug(sql);
            stm.execute(sql);
        } catch (Exception e) {
            throw new FileIOException(file, FileStatus.ERROR, "Error truncating table: " + e.getMessage(), e);
        }
    }

    @Override
    public int read(KsdsKey keyDef, byte[] keyValue, byte[] recordBuffer, KeyIs mode, boolean update) {
        String sqlBase = switch (keyDef.getOffset()) {
            case 0 -> SELECT_1_PRIMARY_KEY;
            case 28 -> SELECT_1_AIX_1;
            default -> throw new FileIOException(file, FileStatus.ERROR, "Key not supported");
        };
        String order = switch (mode) {
            case LTEQ, LESS -> "DESC";
            default -> "ASC";
        };
        String sql = String.format(sqlBase,
                mode.operator,
                order,
                update ? "FOR UPDATE" : ""
        );
        String key = new String(keyValue);
        try (PreparedStatement statement = connection.prepareStatement(sql)) {
            statement.setString(1, key.trim());
            try (ResultSet rs = statement.executeQuery()) {
                if (rs.next()) {
                    CardDemoUser user = new CardDemoUser();
                    user.allocate();
                    // filling record with DB values
                    user.secUsrId.setValue(rs.getString("user_id"));
                    user.secUsrFname.setValue(rs.getString("first_name"));
                    user.secUsrLname.setValue(rs.getString("last_name"));
                    user.secUsrPwd.setValue(rs.getString("pass"));
                    user.secUsrType.setValue(rs.getString("user_type"));
                    user.secUsrFiller.lowValues();
                    // preparing last key read
                    Arrays.fill(keyValue, (byte) 0);
                    byte[] kv = switch (keyDef.getOffset()) {
                        case 0 -> user.secUsrId.getBytes();
                        case 28 -> user.secUsrLname.getBytes();
                        default -> throw new NotImplementedException();
                    };
                    System.arraycopy(kv, 0, keyValue, 0, user.secUsrId.length());
                    // preparing record
                    Arrays.fill(recordBuffer, (byte) 0);
                    System.arraycopy(user.getBytes(), 0, recordBuffer, 0, user.length());
                    return user.length();
                } else {
                    throw new KeyNotFoundException(file);
                }
            }
        } catch (FileIOException fioe) {
            throw fioe;
        } catch (Exception e) {
            log.error("IOCLASS READ", e);
            throw new FileIOException(file, FileStatus.ERROR, e.getMessage(), e);
        }
    }

    @Override
    public void write(byte[] record, int reclen) throws FileIOException {
        try (PreparedStatement statement = connection.prepareStatement(SQL_INSERT)) {
            CardDemoUser user = new CardDemoUser();
            if (record.length < user.length() || reclen < user.length()) {
                throw new FileIOException(file, FileStatus.ERROR, "Illegal input record length");
            }
            user.allocate(new Memory(record));
            statement.setString(1, user.secUsrId.getValue());
            statement.setString(2, user.secUsrFname.getValue().trim());
            statement.setString(3, user.secUsrLname.getValue().trim());
            statement.setString(4, user.secUsrPwd.getValue());
            statement.setString(5, user.secUsrType.getValue());
            int rowsInserted = statement.executeUpdate();
            if (rowsInserted != 1) {
                throw new FileIOException(file, FileStatus.ERROR, "Cannot insert into io table regtest_carddemo_users");
            }
        } catch (SQLException sqle) {
            checkVsamSqlException(IOOperation.WRITE, sqle);
        } catch (FileIOException fioe) {
            throw fioe;
        } catch (Exception e) {
            throw new FileIOException(file, FileStatus.ERROR, "Error writing storage: " + e.getMessage(), e);
        }
    }

    @Override
    public void rewrite(KsdsKey keyDef, byte[] keyValue, byte[] record, int reclen) {
        String keyName = keyDef.getName();
        String sql = SQL_UPDATE;
        try (PreparedStatement statement = connection.prepareStatement(sql)) {
            CardDemoUser user = new CardDemoUser();
            if (record.length < user.length() || reclen < user.length()) {
                throw new FileIOException(file, FileStatus.ERROR, "Illegal input record length");
            }
            user.allocate(new Memory(record));
            statement.setString(1, user.secUsrId.getValue());
            statement.setString(2, user.secUsrFname.getValue().trim());
            statement.setString(3, user.secUsrLname.getValue().trim());
            statement.setString(4, user.secUsrPwd.getValue());
            statement.setString(5, user.secUsrType.getValue());
            statement.setString(6, user.secUsrId.getValue());
            int rowsUpdated = statement.executeUpdate();
            switch (rowsUpdated) {
                case 0 -> throw new KeyNotFoundException(file);
                case 1 -> {
                }
                default -> throw new FileIOException(file, FileStatus.ERROR, "multiple rows selected");
            }
        } catch (SQLException sqle) {
            checkVsamSqlException(IOOperation.REWRITE, sqle);
        } catch (FileIOException fioe) {
            throw fioe;
        } catch (Exception e) {
            throw new FileIOException(file, FileStatus.ERROR, "Error rewriting storage: " + e.getMessage(), e);
        }
    }

    public int delete(KsdsKey keyDef, byte[] keyValue, boolean generic) throws FileIOException {
        String keyName = keyDef.getName();
        String sql = generic ? SQL_DELETE_GENERIC : SQL_DELETE_EQUAL;
        try (PreparedStatement statement = connection.prepareStatement(sql)) {
            CardDemoUser user = new CardDemoUser();
            String userid = new String(keyValue).trim();
            statement.setString(1, userid);
            int rows = statement.executeUpdate();
            if (rows == 0) {
                throw new KeyNotFoundException(file);
            }
            return rows;
        } catch (FileIOException fioe) {
            throw fioe;
        } catch (Exception e) {
            throw new FileIOException(file, FileStatus.ERROR, "Error deleting storage: " + e.getMessage(), e);
        }
    }

    @Override
    public void close() throws FileIOException {
    }

    @Override
    public long importData(SequentialFile infile, int batchSize) throws FileIOException {
        long count = 0;
        boolean ee = infile.isExceptionsEnabled();
        boolean ac = false;
        try {
            ac = connection.getAutoCommit();
            connection.setAutoCommit(false);
        } catch (SQLException e) {
            throw new RuntimeException(e);
        }
        infile.setExceptionsEnabled(true);
        infile.open(OpenMode.Input);

        byte[] rec = infile.getRecord().getBytes();
        CardDemoUser user = new CardDemoUser();
        if (rec.length < user.length()) {
            throw new FileIOException(file, FileStatus.ERROR, "Illegal input record length");
        }
        user.allocate(new Memory(rec));
        try (PreparedStatement statement = connection.prepareStatement(SQL_INSERT)) {
            try {
                while (true) {
                    infile.read();
                    statement.setString(1, user.secUsrId.getValue());
                    statement.setString(2, user.secUsrFname.getValue().trim());
                    statement.setString(3, user.secUsrLname.getValue().trim());
                    statement.setString(4, user.secUsrPwd.getValue());
                    statement.setString(5, user.secUsrType.getValue());
                    statement.addBatch();
                    if (++count % batchSize == 0) { // Execute batch every 10K rows
                        statement.executeBatch();
                        connection.commit(); // Commit every batch
                    }
                }
            } catch (EndOfFileException eof) {
                statement.executeBatch();
                connection.commit();
            }
            return count;
        } catch (FileIOException fioe) {
            throw fioe;
        } catch (Exception e) {
            throw new FileIOException(file, FileStatus.ERROR, "Error importing data: " + e.getMessage(), e);
        } finally {
            if (infile.isOpen()) infile.close();
            infile.setExceptionsEnabled(ee);
            try {
                connection.setAutoCommit(ac);
            } catch (SQLException e) {
                throw new RuntimeException(e);
            }
        }
    }

    @Override
    public long exportData(SequentialFile outfile, boolean append) throws FileIOException {
        long count = 0;
        Record outrec = outfile.getRecord();
        byte[] recordBuffer = outfile.getRecord().getBytes();
        outfile.open(append ? OpenMode.Append : OpenMode.Output);
        try (PreparedStatement statement = connection.prepareStatement(SQL_SELECT_ALL)) {
            try (ResultSet rs = statement.executeQuery()) {
                while (rs.next()) {
                    CardDemoUser user = new CardDemoUser();
                    user.allocate();
                    // filling record with DB values
                    user.secUsrId.setValue(rs.getString("user_id"));
                    user.secUsrFname.setValue(rs.getString("first_name"));
                    user.secUsrLname.setValue(rs.getString("last_name"));
                    user.secUsrPwd.setValue(rs.getString("pass"));
                    user.secUsrType.setValue(rs.getString("user_type"));
                    user.secUsrFiller.spaces(); // <-- I want the export file to be padded with spaces.
                    // preparing record
                    Arrays.fill(recordBuffer, (byte) 0);
                    System.arraycopy(user.getBytes(), 0, recordBuffer, 0, user.length());
                    outrec.setLength(user.length());
                    outfile.write();
                    count++;
                }
            }
        } catch (FileIOException fioe) {
            throw fioe;
        } catch (Exception e) {
            throw new FileIOException(file, FileStatus.ERROR, "Error exporting data", e);
        } finally {
            outfile.close();
        }
        return count;
    }

}

docs.title: Physical Storage
docs.text:
File classes store VSAM file in a database: each file is stored in a table. These tables are structured as follows:

Column KSDS RRDS ESDS VSAM_KEY primary key relative record number Virtual RBA VSAM_DATA record data record data record data VSAM_AIXooooolllll* AIX key - -

(*) zero or more.

The columns are created using the following datatypes

Column DB2 PostgreSQL SQL Server MySQL Oracle Record data VARCHAR FOR BIT DATA BYTEA VARBINARY VARBINARY BLOB KSDS primary and aix keys CHAR FOR BIT DATA BYTEA BINARY BINARY RAW ESDS key INT INT INT INT NUMBER RRDS key INT GENERATED ALWAYS AS IDENTITY INT GENERATED ALWAYS AS IDENTITY INT IDENTITY INT AUTOINCREMENT NUMBER GENERATED ALWAYS AS IDENTITY

Every key column (primary or aix) is backed up by an index.

docs.title: KSDS Key columns
docs.text:
Key columns for KSDS files are kept up to date by triggers that take care of storing the right information (i.e. the KEY data) in the key column. Therefore, for every key column (primary or AIX) one or more triggers are automatically created to automatically maintain the keys.

With this approach, the actual data column can be directly manipulated even by applications not using the LIBER*M file classes, and keys would be kept aligned.

Unfortunately, there are some database engines (i.e. Cocrouch DB or M2) that do not support triggers: in this case, the file classes take care of updating the key columns. That means that if the data is directly modified, the keys go out of sync.

PostgreSQL, IBM DB2, Oracle Databases, MySQL and Microsoft SQL Server fully support triggers.

docs.title: Alternate Indexes (AIX)
docs.text: UNIQUE and NONUNIQUE AIXes are supported. Each AIX corresponds to an additional key columns in the file table and an index.

docs.title: Paths
docs.text: PATHs are supported. They are "just" an entry in the file catalog used by the vsam file classes to map the related cluster or alternate index

docs.title: Server database
docs.text:
The VSAM tables (as well as the catalog table) require a database connection to operate. Therefore, to make use of VSAM (and catalog), you must provide:

  • a database system, with sufficient storage to hold the VSAM data
  • a valid user and/or schema, with create, drop, read and write permissions on the database

The connection to the database is identified by a JDBC connection string that must match the specs of the selected database drivers and can be provided:

  • in the LIBER*M Commons configuration file, for the batch processes
  • in the LIBER*TP configuration files, for the online transactions

docs.title: I/O Classes
docs.text:
As of now, VSAM files are always managed as described above. In future version, a pluggable I/O classes mechanism will be made available to customize the physical VSAM storage.

Stay tuned.

docs.title: VSAM Cache
docs.text:
VSAM file classes translate file I/O operations into database direct accesses. Because of their very nature, the latter is less performant than mainframe VSAM file access. Their performance is influenced by the topology of modern architecture, in particular by the network latency.

So, massive file browsing very performant on mainframe systems might become critical in a distributed environment.

LIBER*M Commons provides a read-only cache mechanism for the VSAM files. The cache loads an in-memory read-only copy of the VSAM file the first time the file is open. The in-memory copy is then used by the application read operations, improving batch programs performances up to 10 times.

The VSAM cache is persistent in the JVM and available for all the threads. This means that, when scripts run under LIBER*Batch control, the cache can be activated by one job and afterward re-used by others, leading to a higher degree of performance gain.

The VSAM cache can be controlled using the com.nib.commons.io.vsam.cache.CacheManager class.

The cache for a file can be enabled in different ways:

  • at file declaration
  • at script level, on the file DD
  • using CacheManager

docs.title: Enabling cache at file declaration
docs.text:
The cache can be enabled when declaring a file.

Java
    var inFile = new KeySequentialFile(context, "READFILE")
        .recordIs(outRecord)
        .fileStatusIs(WS.FS)
        .exceptionsEnabled()
        .cached()                           // enabling cache for this file
        .recordKeyIs(outRecord.key);

docs.title: Enabling cache on the DD
docs.text:
The cache can be enabled in the script, when defining the file DD, passing the parameter cached: true.

Groovy
    exec "MYSTEP01", pgm: "VSAMREAD", {
    dd "VSAMFI00", dsn: "ACME.VSAM.KSDS", disp: [OLD, KEEP, KEEP], cached: true
    dd "SYSPRINT", sysout: "*"
}

docs.title: Managing cache with the cache manager
docs.text:
The CacheManager provides methods to control the cache:

  • request caching for a files
  • evict a file from the cache
  • list the cache content

CacheManager can be used also in Groovy script, making easy to create cache control steps in your batch processes.

Example:

Groovy
CacheManager.instance().cache("MYVSAM.FILE.CLUSTER")
CacheManager.instance().cache("OTHERVSAM.FILE.CLUSTER")
CacheManager.instance().evict("PREVIOUSLY.CACHED.VSAM")
CacheManager.instance().report()

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
LIBER*M Commons provides a set of classes to ease the migration of COBOL/PLI/ASM embedded SQL commands. These classes allow managing host variables and indicators in an "EXEC" like fashion, so to retain the original logic of converted applications, while using standard JDBC drivers.

The main classes of the E/SQL API are Sqlca and NSqlCommand.

docs.title: NSqlCommand
docs.text:
NSqlCommand is the actual "EXEC" interface and exposes methods to provide input and output host variables.

Example:

Java
void slqtest() {
    NChar timestamp = new NChar(26).allocate();
    try (NSqlCommand sql = new NSqlCommand(sqlca)) {
        sql.setCommand("select current timestamp from sysibm.sysdummy1");
        sql.into.add(timestamp);
        sql.execute();
    }
    assertTrue(sqlca.sqlcode.isZero());

    try (NSqlCommand sql = new NSqlCommand(sqlca)) {
        sql.setCommand("select ID, DATE1, TIME1 from TESTTABLE where DATE=?");
        where.setValue("11/12/2018");
        sql.where.add(where, Types.DATE);
        sql.into.add(t.id);
        sql.into.add(t.d1);
        sql.into.add(t.ts1);
        sql.execute();
    }
}

docs.title: SQL Syntax
docs.text:
The LIBER*M Commons E/SQL API do not alter or manipulate the SQL command you provided. This means that the SQL statement must conform to the target database syntax.

In other words, if you are migrating a COBOL/DB2 application to Java with PostgreSQL, you must convert the DB2 SQL statements into PGSQL compatible ones.

Java
void slqtest(String db) {
    var cmd = switch (db) {
        case "db2" -> "select current timestamp from sysibm.sysdummy1";
        case "oracle" -> "select to_char(current_timestamp, 'yyyy-mm-dd-hh24.mi.ss.ff') from dual";
        case "mysql", "mssql", "pgsql" -> "select current_timestamp";
        default -> throw new IllegalStateException("Unexpected value: " + db);
    };
    Sqlca sqlca = new Sqlca(vsam);
    try (NSqlCommand sql = new NSqlCommand(sqlca)) {
        sql.setCommand(cmd);
        sql.into.add(hTs);
        sql.execute();
    }
}

docs.title: Sqlca
docs.text: Sqlca, essentially, represents the connection details, expose control fields (i.e. SQLCODE) and provides "WHENEVER" style callbacks.

docs.title: SQLCODEs
docs.text: The Sqlca class, exposes diagnostic fields such as sqlcode and sqlerrmc. Please note that the LIBER*M Commons E/SQL API does not alter the database error codes and messages. Therefore, the migrated application must manage the target database error code and messages.

docs.title: Callbacks
docs.text:
You can use Sqlca to replicate trigger callbacks for specific error conditions.

Java
protected void procedure() {
    // COB(336): EXEC SQL WHENEVER SQLERROR   GOTO DBERROR END-EXEC.
    sqlca.whenever(SqlCondition.Error, this::handleSqlDberror);
    // COB(337): EXEC SQL WHENEVER SQLWARNING GOTO DBERROR END-EXEC.
    sqlca.whenever(SqlCondition.Warning, this::handleSqlDberror);
    // COB(338): EXEC SQL WHENEVER NOT FOUND  CONTINUE     END-EXEC.
    sqlca.whenever(SqlCondition.NotFound);
    progStart();
}

docs.title: DATE/TIME/TIMESTAMP format
docs.text:
When host variables assigned to DATE, TIME and TIMESTAMP columns are read or written to the JDBC driver, the LIBER*M Commons E/SQL API convert the format of such data types.

The format can be defined in the LIBER*M Commons configuration file. If no configuration is provided, the default format of the driver are used.

Example:

Properties
#
# LIBER*M Commons configuration
#
esql.date.format=dd/MM/yyyy
esql.timestamp.format=yyyy-MM-dd-HH.mm.ss.SSSSSS
esql.time.format=HH:mm:ss

docs.title: E/SQL Cursors
docs.text: LIBER*M Commons E/SQL API supports SQL cursors using the class NSqlCursor. This class leverages a standard java ResultSet to provide the E/SQL cursor functionalities.

docs.title: Browse
docs.text:
In this example, the program opens a cursor and fetches the rows one by one. The program stops fetching when there are no more rows to fetch.

Java
    NSqlCursor cursor = new NSqlCursor(sqlca);
    cursor.setCommand("select k, a, b from regtest_cursors where k between 'KEY00010' and 'KEY00020'");
    cursor.open();
    if (sqlca.sqlcode.getInt() != 0) {
        throw new RuntimeException("ERROR: " + sqlca.sqlerrmc);
    }
    while (sqlca.sqlcode.isZero()) {
        cursor.fetch(WS.hKey, WS.hA, WS.hB);
        switch (sqlca.sqlcode.getInt()) {
            case 0:
                System.out.println("CURSOR: " + WS.hKey + " - " + WS.hA + " - " + WS.hB);
                break;
            case 100:
                break;
            default:
                throw new RuntimeException("ERROR: " + sqlca.sqlerrmc);
        }
    }
    cursor.close();

docs.title: Update CURRENT OF using SQL update
docs.text:
The most efficient way to update columns just fetched by a cursor, is to perform a direct update.

To perform a direct update it is required a row identifier, therefore: - the cursor SELECT must be complemented with the row identifier (i.e. CTID, ROWID, etc.) - the update must use the selected row identifier in the WHERE clause

Java
    NSqlCursor cursor = new NSqlCursor(sqlca);
    cursor.setCommand("select ctid, k, a, b from regtest_cursors where k between 'KEY00070' and 'KEY00080'");
    cursor.open();
    if (sqlca.sqlcode.getInt() != 0) {
        throw new RuntimeException("ERROR: " + sqlca.sqlerrmc);
    }
    while (sqlca.sqlcode.isZero()) {
        NChar ctid = new NChar(32).allocate();
        cursor.fetch(ctid, WS.hKey, WS.hA, WS.hB);
        switch (sqlca.sqlcode.getInt()) {
            case 0:
                System.out.println("CURSOR: " + WS.hKey + " - " + WS.hA + " - " + WS.hB + " CTID=" + ctid);
                if (WS.hKey.equals("KEY00075")) {
                    WS.newA.setValue("Campo A 75 - UPDATED");
                    WS.newB.setValue("Campo B 75 - UPDATED");
                    // COB(802): 003469      END-EXEC
                    try (NSqlCommand sql = new NSqlCommand(sqlca)) {
                        sql.setCommand("UPDATE regtest_cursors set a=?, b=? where ctid = ?::tid");
                        sql.set
                                .add(WS.newA) //
                                .add(WS.newB);
                        sql.where
                                .add(ctid);
                        sql.execute();
                    }
                }
                break;
            case 100:
                break;
            default:
                throw new RuntimeException("ERROR: " + sqlca.sqlerrmc);
        }
    }
    cursor.close();

docs.title: Update CURRENT OF using Java Resultset
docs.text:
LIBER*M Commons cursors support Java native resultset update.

WARNING: using Java native resultset update has performance implications. It is recommended to use direct updates when massive updates are required.

The following example shows how to update a row using NSqlCursor which uses the updateRow method of the ResultSet object:

Java
    NSqlCursor cursor = new NSqlCursor(sqlca);
    cursor.setCommand("select k, a, b from regtest_cursors where k between 'KEY00010' and 'KEY00020'");
    cursor.setResultSetType(ResultSet.TYPE_SCROLL_SENSITIVE);
    cursor.setResultSetConcurrency(ResultSet.CONCUR_UPDATABLE);
    cursor.open();
    if (sqlca.sqlcode.getInt() != 0) {
        throw new RuntimeException("ERROR: " + sqlca.sqlerrmc);
    }
    while (sqlca.sqlcode.isZero()) {
        cursor.fetch(WS.hKey, WS.hA, WS.hB);
        switch (sqlca.sqlcode.getInt()) {
            case 0:
                System.out.println("CURSOR: " + WS.hKey + " - " + WS.hA + " - " + WS.hB);
                if (WS.hKey.equals("KEY00015")) {
                    ResultSet rs=cursor.getResultset();
                    try {
                        rs.updateString("a", "Campo A 15 - UPDATED");
                        rs.updateString("b", "Campo B 15 - UPDATED");
                        rs.updateRow();
                    } catch (SQLException e) {
                        throw new RuntimeException(e);
                    }
                }
                break;
            case 100:
                break;
            default:
                throw new RuntimeException("ERROR: " + sqlca.sqlerrmc);
        }
    }
    cursor.close();

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
LIBER*M Commons provide a set of classes that mimic the mainframe datatypes, so to ease the migration of applications coming from the mainframe word. These type of class can be referred as "fields".

These "fields" classes use fully managed API and are contained in the java package com.nib.commons.storage and extend the base class AbstractNField.

The following datatypes are provided:

Datatype COBOL datatype LIBER*M Commons class(es) ZONED PIC 9(n) NZoned BINARY UNSIGNED PIC 9(n) COMP NUInt16, NUInt32, NUInt64 BINARY SIGNED PIC S9(n) COMP NInt16, NInt32, NInt64 PACKED PIC S9(n) COMP-3 NPacked CHAR PIC X(n) NChar 32 bits FLOATING POINT COMP-1 NFloat 64 bits FLOATING POINT COMP-2 NDouble POINTER USAGE IS POINTER NPointer

Structures (field groups) can be reproduced using classes that extend NGroup.

docs.title: The Storage object
docs.text:
All the data storage classes allocate and store their data in a Storage object. Such a type of objects can be shared among many "fields" so to mimic, essentially, a memory storage area. The underlying java storage is a simple byte[], making the data accesses safe and managed.

For example, in a converted program, a single Storage object can be used to store the entire "WORKING-STORAGE" of the program, so to allow programming patterns widespread in mainframe programs.

docs.title: EBCDIC storage
docs.text:
By default, LIBER*M Commons store data in ASCII format. But the classess can be configured to store data in EBCDIC.

This means that, for example, setting a char field to the value 123, the underlaying memory is set to 0xF1F2F3.

Fields can be set operate in EBCDIC mode either individually or globally.

To configure individual fields to operate in EBCDIC mode, just use the method ebcdic() at instantiation. Example:

Java
void ebcdic() {
    NChar asciiField = new NChar(8)
            .initial("12345678")
            .allocate();
    NChar ebcdicField = new NChar(8)
            .initial("12345678")
            .ebcdic()
            .allocate();

    assertEquals("12345678", asciiField.toString());                // both fields are initialized with "12345678"
    assertEquals("12345678", ebcdicField.toString());
    assertEquals("3132333435363738", asciiField.toHexString());     // ASCII file in memory is 313233...  
    assertEquals("f1f2f3f4f5f6f7f8", ebcdicField.toHexString());    // EBCDIC is f1f2f3...
}

To configure LIBER*M Commons to work in EBCDIC mode globally, you need to set the parameter storage.ebcdic in the LIBER*M Commons configuration file.

Example:

Properties
#
# LIBER*M Commons configuration
#
storage.charset=ISO-8859-1
storage.ebcdic=true                                 # setting whole runtime to EBCDIC
esql.date.format=dd/MM/yyyy
esql.timestamp.format=yyyy-MM-dd-HH.mm.ss.SSSSSS
esql.time.format=HH:mm:ss

docs.title: Elementary Fields
docs.text: LIBER*M Commons supports the following elementary field types.

docs.title: Zoned fields
docs.text: Zoned fields are reproduced by the class NZoned. It can be defined as signed or unsigned at instantiation time.

docs.title: Packed fields
