docs.text: Packed fields are reproduced by the class NPacked. It can be defined as signed or unsigned at instantiation time.

docs.title: Binary fields
docs.text:
Binary, signed and unsigned, fields are reproduced by the class NInteger. It can be defined as signed or unsigned at instantiation time.

But for the sake of declaration and readability, a set of classes that extends NInteger are provided to construct signed/unsigned fields of 2, 4 and 8 bytes length.

Integer class Bits Bytes Sign COBOL equivalent NInt16 16 2 true PIC S9(4) COMP NUInt16 16 2 false PIC 9(4) COMP NInt32 32 4 true PIC S9(8) COMP NUInt32 32 4 false PIC 9(8) COMP NInt64 64 8 true PIC S9(16) COMP NUInt64 64 8 false PIC 9(16) COMP

All the classes extending NInteger, may be configured to store integers with big or little endianness. By default, they store data in big endian mode, like 390 CPUs.

docs.title: Floating Point fields
docs.text:
Floating Point fields are reproduced by the classes NFloat and NDouble. The store floating point data using IEEE754 notation (Java native).

Integer class Bits Bytes Sign COBOL equivalent NFloat 32 2 true COMP-1 NDouble 64 4 true COMP-2

docs.title: Char fields
docs.text: Char fields, the generic PIC X, are reproduced by the class NChar.

docs.title: Field Groups
docs.text:
Mainframe programs often use complex data structures, field groups. This feature can be reproduced creating a class that extends he class NGroup.

Your new class can declare fields in the same order they are in the original structure, and the base methods will make sure fields are allocated exactly where they are meant to be.

NGroup based classed can be nested, so to managed structure sub-levels.

For example, the following COBOL record:

COBOL
01  FD-TRAN-CAT-RECORD.
    05  FD-TRAN-CAT-KEY.
        10  FD-TRAN-TYPE-CD                         PIC X(02).
        10  FD-TRAN-CAT-CD                          PIC 9(04).
    05  FD-TRAN-CAT-DATA                            PIC X(54).

becomes:

Java
public FdTranCatRecord fdTranCatRecord = new FdTranCatRecord();

public static class FdTranCatRecord extends NGroup {
    public static class FdTranCatKey extends NGroup {
        public NChar fdTranTypeCd = new NChar(2);
        public NZoned fdTranCatCd = new NZoned(4, false);
    }

    public FdTranCatKey fdTranCatKey = new FdTranCatKey();
    public NChar fdTranCatData = new NChar(54);
}

Some relevant notes:

  • Allocating the group object, automatically, all the children "fields" are allocated. you don't need to allocate fields one by one;
  • Redefines are allowed only inside the same group (like in COBOL);

docs.title: Initial values
docs.text:
All the "fields" classes may have an initial value, like COBOL clause VALUE or PL/I INIT. It is assigned using the initial() method.

Java
void initMe() {
    NChar c=new NChar(8).initial("IT'S ME");
    NInt32 i= new NInt32().initial(-10);
}

docs.title: Pointers
docs.text:
Pointers are reproduced by the class NPointer. This class represents the storage address in the Storage object used by objects extending AbstractNField.

The NPointer allows also pointer arithmetic, meaning you can actually manipulate the pointers and "move" area definitions:

Java
/// data areas
class PointersSample extends NGroup {
    final NChar textArea = new NChar(8).initial("12345678");
    final NPointer pointer = new NPointer();
}

void sample() {
    PointerSample ps = new PointerSample();         // declaring the group instance;
    NChar addressableArea = new NChar(4);           // declaring an area to address later on
    ps.allocate();                                  // allocating memory for the data group
    ps.pointer.setTo(ps.getAddress());              // setting the pointer to the address of field textArea
    ps.pointer.add(1);                              // increasing the pointer
    addressableArea.setAddress(ps.pointer);         // setting address of the test are to the pointer 
    assertEquals(addressableArea.getValue(), "2345");       // the addressable are now points to textArea+1
}

docs.title: Redefines
docs.text:
"Fields" objects can "redefine" another data area, like the COBOL clause REDEFINES.

Invoking the method redefines() before they (or enclosing group) are actually allocated, the field "redefines" the specified area.

Example:

Java
class RedefinesSample extends NGroup {
    final NChar charField = new NChar(4);                           // 4 bytes area
    final NInt32 intField = new NInt32().redefines(charField);      // 4 bytes integer
}

void sample() {
    RedefinesSample rs = new RedefinesSample();
    rs.allocate();
    rs.charField.lowValues();
    assertEquals("00000000", rs.charField.toHexString());
    assertTrue(rs.intField.isZero());                    // checking binary fields as numeric

    rs.intField.setValue(0x1010);
    assertEquals("00001010", rs.charField.toHexString());
}

docs.title: LIBER*Batch image
docs.text:
LIBERBatch is the LIBERM batch job coordinator that mimics the behavior of the IBM z/OS Job Entry Subsystem (JES).

This image sets up a container environment for running a LIBER*Batch node using Amazon Corretto 17.

docs.title: Node configuration
docs.text:
The LIBER*Batch configuration files are read from the directory /config.

Please refer to the LIBER*Batch configuration for further details on how to configure a LIBER*Batch cluster and nodes.

Don't forget that every connection to other components (i.e. REDIS, log aggregators, etc.) must be configured in the LIBER*Batch configuration files loaded from /config .

So if you want to configure, for example, how logs are shipped to your log aggregation system, you must edit the main LIBER*Batch configuration file application.yaml and/or your logging system configuration (i.e. logback.xml).

docs.title: License keys
docs.text: The LIBER*Batch node requires a license key to run. The license key must be provided as an environment variable NIB_JAVA_LICENSE_KEY.

docs.title: 3P Components license keys
docs.text: License keys (or files) for 3P tools or libraries must be provided according to the respective vendor indications.

docs.title: Veryant isCOBOL license
docs.text:
IsCOBOL license can be passed to the container either copying or mounting the iscobol.properties file containing the license key in the /root directory of the container.

Example with Docker Compose:

YAML
  motorhead:
    # ... other parameters ...
    volumes:
      - ${USERPROFILE}/iscobol.properties:/home/motorhead/iscobol.properties

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text:
This image contains the required dependencies to run a LIBER*Batch node plus some commodity packages:

  • Amazon Corretto 21
  • Spring Boot
  • Spring Cloud
  • Redis client
  • JDBC drivers for DB2, PostgreSQL, MySQL and SQL Server
  • Logback
  • Loki logback appender

In case you need to add more dependencies, you can extend this image and add them to the Dockerfile in the directory /motorhead/jars.

The LIBER*Batch startup script will add all jars in this directory to the classpath.

IMPORTANT: The LIBER*Batch dependencies are available to the custom apps and scripts too.

docs.title: How to use via docker
docs.text:
To run the Docker container, use the following command:

Bash
docker run -d -p 8080:8080 \ 
  -e NIB_JAVA_LICENSE_KEY="<license-key>" \
  -m ${APP_ROOT}/config:/config \
  -m ${DATA_ROOT}:/data \
  -m ${APP_ROOT}/target/classes:/classes \
  --name motorhead-node \
  niblabs/motorhead

This command runs the container in detached mode and maps port 8080 of the host to port 8080 of the container.

docs.title: How to use with docker compose
docs.text:
Example compose service for LIBER*Batch:

YAML
  motorhead-a:
    image: "nib/motorhead"
    deploy:
      mode: replicated
      replicas: 2
    #    ports:
    #        - "8080:8080"
    depends_on:
      redis:
        condition: service_started
      loki:
        condition: service_started
      rdbms:
        condition: service_healthy
        restart: true
      crdb:
        condition: service_healthy
        restart: true
    environment:
      - SPRING_PROFILES_ACTIVE=NodeA
      - NIB_JAVA_LICENSE_KEY=${NIB_JAVA_LICENSE_KEY}
    volumes:
      - ./motorhead:/config
      - ${NIB_JAVA_HOME}:/opt/nib
      - ${DATA_ROOT}:/data

docs.text: Spring, IBM, CICS, JES, REDIS, and any other names or brands referenced herein are trademarks or registered trademarks of their respective owners. All rights to these trademarks are fully acknowledged and respected, and their inclusion in this context does not imply any affiliation with or endorsement by the trademark holders.

docs.title: LIBER*TP image
docs.text:
LIBER*TP is a Spring based framework designed to facilitate the migration of mainframe online programs (CICS & IMS) to modern environments.

This image sets up a container environment for running a supernaut node using Amazon Corretto 17.

docs.title: Node configuration
docs.text:
The LIBER*TP configuration files are read from the directory /config.

Please refer to the LIBER*TP configuration for further details on how to configure a LIBER*TP region or node.

Don't forget that every connection to other components (i.e. RDBMS, REDIS, log aggregators, etc.) must be configured in the LIBER*TP configuration files loaded from /config.

So if you want to configure, for example, how logs are shipped to your log aggregation system, you must edit the main LIBER*TP configuration file application.yaml and/or your logging system configuration (i.e. logback.xml).

docs.title: 3P Components license keys
docs.text: License keys (or files) for 3P tools or libraries must be provided according to the respective vendor indications.

docs.title: Veryant isCOBOL license
docs.text:
IsCOBOL license can be passed to the container either copying or mounting the iscobol.properties file containing the license key in the /root directory of the container.

Example with Docker Compose:

YAML
  supernaut:
    # ... other parameters ...
    volumes:
      - ${USERPROFILE}/iscobol.properties:/home/supernaut/iscobol.properties

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text:
This image contains the required dependencies to run a LIBER*TP node plus some commodity packages:

  • Amazon Corretto 21
  • Spring Boot
  • Spring Cloud
  • Redis client
  • JDBC drivers for DB2, PostgreSQL, MySQL and SQL Server
  • Logback
  • Loki logback appender
  • Atomikos (TransactionEssentials)

In case you need to add more dependencies, you can extend this image and add them to the Dockerfile in the directory /supernaut/jars.

The LIBER*TP startup script will add all jars in this directory to the classpath.

docs.title: Atomikos logs
docs.text:
By default, atomikos is configured with the following options:

Property Value com.atomikos.icatch.log_base_dir /tmlogs com.atomikos.icatch.log_base_name ${uname}-tmlog com.atomikos.icatch.output_dir /tmlogs

To override those values, you can pass the environment variable ATOMIKOS_OPTS with all the new values when the container is started.

Bash
ATOMIKOS_OPTS="-Dcom.atomikos.icatch.log_base_dir=/my-path -Dcom.atomikos.icatch.log_base_name=my-tmlog"

docs.title: How to use via docker
docs.text:
To run the Docker container, use the following command:

Bash
docker run -d -p 8080:8080 \ 
  -e NIB_JAVA_LICENSE_KEY="<license-key>" \
  -m ${APP_ROOT}/config:/config \
  -m ${APP_ROOT}/src/bms:/bms \
  -m ${APP_ROOT}/target/classes:/classes \
  --name supernaut-node \
  niblabs/supernaut

This command runs the container in detached mode and maps port 8080 of the host to port 8080 of the container.

docs.title: How to use with docker compose
docs.text:
Example compose service for LIBER*TP:

YAML
  supernaut:
    image: "niblabs/supernaut"
    deploy:
      mode: replicated
      replicas: 3
    depends_on:
      redis:
        condition: service_started
      rdbms:
        condition: service_healthy
        restart: true
      crdb:
        condition: service_healthy
        restart: true
    environment:
      - NIB_JAVA_LICENSE_KEY=${NIB_JAVA_LICENSE_KEY}
    volumes:
      - ./supernaut:/config
      - ${NIB_JAVA_HOME}/samples/aws-carddemo-java/target/classes/bms:/bms
      - ${NIB_JAVA_HOME}/samples/aws-carddemo-java/target/classes:/classes

docs.title: Trademarks
docs.text: Spring, IBM, CICS, JES, REDIS, and any other names or brands referenced herein are trademarks or registered trademarks of their respective owners. All rights to these trademarks are fully acknowledged and respected, and their inclusion in this context does not imply any affiliation with or endorsement by the trademark holders.

docs.title: TN3270 image
docs.text:
TN3270 is a gateway service that allows to connect TN3270 terminals to a Supernaut region.

This image sets up a container environment for running a TN3270 service.

docs.title: Node configuration
docs.text:
The TN3270 configuration files are read from the directory /config.

Please refer to the TN3270 configuration for further details on how to configure a TN3270 service node.

Remember that, when using the TN3270 container, the correct host names and address must be used to:

  • expose correctly the service for TN3270 terminals
  • connect the Supernaut region at the correct endpoint

Depending on the deployment architecture, those addresses may vary.

docs.title: License key
docs.text: The TN3270 node requires a license key to run. The license key must be provided as an environment variable NIB_JAVA_LICENSE_KEY.

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text:
This image contains the required dependencies to run a TN3270 node plus some commodity packages:

  • Amazon Corretto 21
  • Spring Boot
  • Spring Cloud
  • Logback
  • Loki logback appender

In case you need to add more dependencies, you can extend this image and add them to the Dockerfile in the directory /TN3270/jars.

The TN3270 startup script will add all jars in this directory to the classpath.

docs.title: How to use via docker
docs.text:
To run the Docker container, use the following command:

Bash
docker run -d -p 3270:3270 \ 
  -e NIB_JAVA_LICENSE_KEY="<license-key>" \
  -v /local/path/tn3270.yaml:/config/tn3270.yaml:ro
  --name tn3270-node \
  nib/tn3270

This command runs the container in detached mode and maps port 3270 of the host to port 3270 of the container.

docs.title: How to use with docker compose
docs.text:
The 3270 container can be used with any container orchestration software.

This is an example with Docker Compose:

YAML
    tn3270:
    image: "nib/tn3270"
    depends_on:
      - supernaut
    ports:
      - "3271:3270"
    environment:
      - NIB_JAVA_LICENSE_KEY=${NIB_JAVA_LICENSE_KEY}
    volumes:
      - ./tn3270:/config

docs.title: TN3270 image for the development environment
docs.text:
The tn3270 container can be used in a development environment to connect to a Supernaut region in the host system.

That's the typical use case when developing and testing applications that require a TN3270 connection.

The default configuration embedded in the docker image is fit for purpose.

So all you have to do is to run the container with the following command:

Bash
docker run -p 3270:3270 --name nib-tn3270 -e NIB_JAVA_LICENSE_KEY=%NIB_JAVA_LICENSE_KEY% nib/tn3270

docs.title: Trademarks
docs.text: Spring, IBM, CICS, JES, REDIS, and any other names or brands referenced herein are trademarks or registered trademarks of their respective owners. All rights to these trademarks are fully acknowledged and respected, and their inclusion in this context does not imply any affiliation with or endorsement by the trademark holders.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Migrating from a mainframe environment to open systems is a complex and high-impact initiative that touches nearly every aspect of an organization’s IT landscape. This transition typically aims to modernize legacy systems, reduce operating costs, and improve agility by leveraging more flexible and scalable open system architectures. However, the process involves significant technical and organizational challenges, particularly in the areas of application code and data migration.

Code migration requires converting legacy applications written in COBOL, PL/I, or Assembler into Java or C# code to run in modern environments such as Java, .NET, or cloud-native platforms. This includes addressing differences in system architecture, batch processing models, middleware, and job scheduling tools, while preserving core business logic and functionality.

Data migration involves transferring large volumes of structured and unstructured data, frequently stored in hierarchical or proprietary mainframe formats such as VSAM or DB2, into relational databases or modern data platforms. Ensuring data integrity, consistency, and security during the migration process is critical, as is addressing issues such as data encoding, record structure conversion, and the handling of historical or archived datasets.

Therefore, while planning a migration, at least the following areas must be analysed and addressed:

  • Target System Architecture
  • Code migration
    • Application programs conversion
      • Language syntaxes
      • E/SQL
      • Collating sequence
    • Application JCL and scripts conversion
      • JCL formalism
      • JCL utilities
      • Restart logic
  • Data migration
    • File migration
    • Database migration
  • Scheduling
  • Security
  • Monitoring/Logging
  • Printing
    • Document composition
    • Output management
  • Operations
    • Operational JCL and scripts replacement
    • Backups
    • Tapes and cartridges management
  • Interfaces
    • Internal interfaces
    • External interfaces and partner systems
  • Development
    • Existing tooling and procedures
    • Target DevOps
  • Testing
    • Functional test
    • Performance & stress test
    • Other Non-functional test (DR, DevOps, etc.)
  • Management of Change

docs.title: File migration
docs.text: Mainframe systems often rely heavily on sequential, VSAM and partitioned datasets (PDS) for online and batch processing. Therefore, a key aspect of the data migration process is the file migration to convert the datasets from EBCDIC to ASCII.

docs.title: Data conversion
docs.text:
All files need to be converted from EBCDIC to ASCII respecting their data structure. In fact, one cannot just apply a straightforward EBCDIC to ASCII conversion, like an FTP would do.

Mainframe files are often structured in one or more record layouts with different datatypes that must be carefully converted to ASCII.

We recommend using specialized file conversion tools to be configured to use the correct file layouts, often defined in a COBOL COPYBOOK or equivalent artefact.

docs.title: Catalog migration
docs.text:
Once data is converted from EBCDIC to ASCII, the files catalog entries must be created in the catalog. LIBER*M provides different tools to manage the catalog.

We suggest to extract the file characteristics from the mainframe LISTCAT or LISTDS use it as input for scripts invoking nibvsam to create the necessary catalog entries.

docs.title: VSAM migration
docs.text:
VSAM migration requires an additional step. Once the file is converted from EBCDIC to ASCII, you need to import that file into the actual VSAM table.

You can use nibvsam or NIBCAMS1.

docs.title: File Migration Workflow
docs.text:
The following diagram represents a simplified file migration workflow. Of course, data conversion tools and cataloging scripts must be created as part of the data migration process.

---
title: File migration workflow
---
flowchart TD

    subgraph MF[Mainframe]
        IDLIS[[IDCAMS LISTCAT/LISTDS]] --> LISTCAT@{ shape: docs, label: "LISTCAT/LISTDS" } 
        QSAM[(EBCDIC QSAM)] 
        VSAM[(EBCDIC VSAM)] --> REPRO[[IDCAMS REPRO]] 
        REPRO--> VSAM_SEQ[(EBCDIC VSAM<br/>export)]
    end     

    COPY@{ shape: docs, label: "Record Layouts" } --> TK 

    subgraph TGT[Conversion system]
        QSAM --> |FTP| EBCQ[(EBCDIC QSAM)]
        VSAM_SEQ --> |FTP| EBCV[(EBCDIC VSAM)]
        EBCQ --> TK[[Data Conversion Tools]]
        EBCV --> TK
        TK --> ASCV[(ASCII VSAM export)]
        ASCV --> NIBCAMS1[[NIBCAMS1 repro]] 
        LISTCAT --> NIBVSAM[[Cataloging Scripts]]
    end


    subgraph DB[Target System]
        TK --> ASCQ@{shape: lin-cyl, label: "ASCII QSAM" } 
        NIBVSAM --> CATALOG[(LIBER*M Catalog)] 
        NIBCAMS1 --> TABLE[(ASCII VSAM table)] 
    end

docs.title: EBCDIC data storage
docs.text:
LIBERM allows to store data in EBCDIC. By configuring the LIBERM Commons configuration file, you can force the entire framework to work in EBCDIC.

But this has a number of side effects, such as:

  • all your files will be stored in EBCDIC, therefore, on open systems, they will be harder to edit with regular ASCII editors
  • in case you application uses a database too (i.e. DB2), you will have a mixed environment where files are EBCDIC but your database is ASCII, resulting in uncoherent collating order

We suggest to work in EBCDIC mode ONLY in projects of limited scale and do not make originally use of a database.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
LIBERBatch is the LIBERM batch Job coordinator. It mimics the behavior of the IBM z/OS Job Entry Subsystem (JES) and is used to submit and manage batch jobs on the LIBER*M platforms.

A LIBER*Batch cluster is composed by:

  • one or more job execution nodes, running the batch jobs
  • zero or more proxy nodes, to manage the job submission and monitoring
  • zero or more on-demand one-off nodes
  • the backbone, an in-memory database that manages the shared resources
---
title: LIBER*Batch Architecture
---
flowchart TD
    NLB((🔀NLB)) --> MOTORHEAD
    CMD[🧑‍💻Command Line] -->|REST API| NLB
    CURL[📦HTTP Request] -->|REST API| NLB

    subgraph MOTORHEAD[LIBER*Batch Cluster]
        N1[⚙️Initiator #1] --> Job1[Job]
        N2[⚙️Initiator #2] --> Job2[Job]
        NX[⚙️Initiator ...] --> JobX[Job]
        PROXY["⚙️Proxy (optional)"]
    end

    MOTORHEAD <--> In-MemoryDB[(⚡REDIS)]
    MOTORHEAD <--> DB[(🛢🛢🛢DB2/PGSQL/MYSQL/ORACLE/MSSQL)]

LIBER*Batch used the concept of "initiator". The initiator is essentially a thread that executes the jobs. Each initiator belongs to a class.

Nodes can have one or more initiators, each one belonging to a different class. Multiple nodes can run initiators of the same class.

So you can configure the cluster in many different ways, depending on the workload and the resources available.

docs.title: The Backbone
docs.text:
The backbone represents the cluster shared memory. Indeed, the backbone is an in-memory storage system, where the LIBER*Batch nodes store:

  • Nodes information
  • Input job queues
  • Active jobs data
  • Ouput job queue (optionally)
  • Locks and synchronization structures

Currently, LIBER*Batch supports only the REDIS backbone.

docs.title: REDIS backbone
docs.text:
The resources are shared on a REDIS database. REDIS can be provided with any deployment (standalone nodes, redis clusters or even SaaS, i.e. AWS ElastiCache).

The access to Redis is guaranteed by the Redisson Spring Boot Starter, so the REDIS connection configuration is managed using standard Redisson configuration file and any setup supported by Redisson is compatible with LIBER*Batch.

For further information on the Redis database used by the backbone, see here.

docs.title: The Long Term Storage
docs.text:
The job output queue is stored using the long term storage provider. The backbone is the default long term storage provider, but for production systems, it is better to have a dedicate storage system for the output queue, so to avoid storing past jobs info in the in-memory DB.

LIBER*Batch ships with an RDBMS based long term storage provider, that works with PGSQL, DB2, Oracle, SQL Server and MySQL.

It is also possible to create custom long term storage provider, implementing the ILongTermStorage interface.

For further info, see Long Term Storage.

docs.title: The Job Execution nodes
docs.text:
Job execution nodes are the nodes that execute the jobs. These are the default node type.

Every job execution node has one or more threads, one each assigned class, that monitors input queue, pool and process the job execution requests, executing the jobs.

docs.title: The Proxy nodes
docs.text:
Each node is able to respond to administration and monitoring requests for any of the nodes.

In clustered environments with flexible load balancers (i.e. k8s) the proxy node is not necessary.

But in a clustered environment with simple load balancers and with nodes that might be instantiated or removed on demand (i.e. docker compose), it might be not convenient to use one of the execution nodes to request administrative and monitoring services.

The proxy node is just a node with no initiators. It is used to access administrative and monitoring APIs in such scenarios.

More proxy node can be allocated in the cluster to guarantee the availability of the administrative and monitoring services.

docs.title: One-off nodes
docs.text:
Certain nodes can be configured to operate in a "one-off" mode, where they start, execute a single job, and then shut down. This configuration is particularly useful when specific jobs must run on designated resources.

For instance, in a Kubernetes environment, affinities can be used to execute regular job execution nodes on general-purpose machines, while resource-intensive jobs are executed on high-performance machines in a one-off manner.

Alternatively, daily jobs can be executed on regular job execution nodes running within managed Kubernetes services ( such as Amazon EKS or Google GKE), while more demanding weekly or monthly workloads are offloaded to on-demand managed batch computing services (e.g., AWS Batch or Google Cloud Batch) running one-off nodes.

docs.title: Command Line
docs.text: LIBER*Batch has a command line tool mh that allows to interact with the motorhead cluster.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
Bash
Usage: mh [-j] [-M=<URL>] [-P=<PASSWD>] [-U=<USER>] [COMMAND]
LIBER*Batch Admin Utility
  -j, --json                JSON output
  -M, --motorhead=<URL>     LIBER*Batch API URL
  -P, --password=<PASSWD>   LIBER*Batch API password
  -T, --jwt-token=<TOKEN>   JWT authentication token
  -U, --user=<USER>         LIBER*Batch API user
Commands:
  class    Manage job classes
  cluster  Manage cluster
  node     Manage node
  job      Manage job
  help     Display help information about the specified command.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
The tool requires the URL of the LIBER*Batch API endpoint. It can be provided either as a command line argument or as an environment variable.

To provide the URL as an environment variable, set the MOTORHEAD_URL variable to the LIBER*Batch API URL.

docs.title: Setup
docs.text:
To use the mh command line tool, set the following environment variables:

Linux:

Bash
export PATH=$NIB_JAVA_HOME/bin:$PATH
export MOTORHEAD_URL=http://localhost:8080
export MOTORHEAD_USER=admin
export MOTORHEAD_PASSWORD=password

Dos:

Text Only
set PATH=%NIB_JAVA_HOME%\bin;%PATH%
set MOTORHEAD_URL=http://localhost:8080

docs.title: Submit a job
docs.text:
Text Only
D:\Nib\nib-java>mh job submit READCARD.groovy
job submitted jobnum=46 UUID=d8eac78b-b2e0-4afb-a26e-3f8cd59d8be4

docs.title: Submit a job with restart
docs.text:
Text Only
D:\Nib\nib-java>mh job submit --restart-step=STEP03 JRESTART.groovy
job submitted jobnum=48 UUID=78aaac78b-b2e0-4afb-a26e-3f8cd59d8be4

docs.title: Disable all classes
docs.text:
Text Only
D:\Nib\nib-java>mh class disable "*"
class * disabled.

docs.title: Show class status
docs.text:
Text Only
D:\Nib\nib-java>mh class status
name description          status   jobs
---- -------------------- -------- -----
A                         disabled     0
B                         disabled     0
C                         disabled     0
D                         disabled     0
E                         disabled     0

docs.title: Use JWT authentication
docs.text:
Bash
ACCESS_TOKEN=$(curl -s --location --request POST 'http://localhost:9010/realms/dev/protocol/openid-connect/token' \
        --header 'Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded' \
        --data-urlencode 'client_id=dev-client' \
        --data-urlencode 'username=my-user' \
        --data-urlencode 'password=my-secret-password' \
        --data-urlencode 'grant_type=password' |
        jq -r '.access_token')
mh -T $ACCESS_TOKEN cluster status

docs.title: Monitoring
docs.text:
LIBER*Batch exposes observability metrics compatible with standard monitoring technologies, such as Prometheus, Grafana, etc.

LIBER*Batch for Java implements observability using Micrometer. It exposes the following observation points:

Metric Description motorhead.job.elapsed The elapsed time of a job in milliseconds. motorhead.jobs.active The number of currently active jobs. motorhead.jobs.waiting The number of jobs waiting for a resource. motorhead.jobs.hold The number of jobs on hold. motorhead.jobs.pending The number of jobs waiting on queue to be executed.

docs.title: Scripting interface
docs.text: LIBER*Batch allows executing any Groovy script and provides some facilities to ease the integration with scripts.

docs.title: Job class
docs.text:
Job class can be specified when submitting the job, but also in the job itself, using @MotorheadClass annotation:

Groovy
@BaseScript NibJob Job123
@Field @MotorheadClass('B') def JOBCLASS

job "JOB123"

docs.title: External Variables
docs.text:
External variables can be passed to the script when submitting it. Both REST API and command line tool allow to specify key/value pairs to be passed to the script:

Bash
mh job submit -V COMPANY=ACME -V ZONE=EU Job123.groovy

In the script, the variables can be retrieved accessing the groovy binding object:

Groovy
@BaseScript NibJob Job123
@Field @MotorheadClass('B') def JOBCLASS

job "JOB123"

def company = binding.getVariable("COMPANY")
def zone = binding.getVariable("ZONE")

log.info("COMPANY: ${company} & ZONE: ${zone}")

External variables are key handling, for example, input coming from a scheduler, that can specify the variables on the submit command, for the job to use it afterward.

docs.title: Job Restart
docs.text: Job restart can be forced, even without any change in the script. Just provide the restart data in the request (API or command line), and the framework will trigger the restart (if enabled by the scripting configuration.

docs.title: Security
docs.text:
LIBER*Batch manages security using Spring's standard authentication and authorization mechanisms. It supports four modes of operation:

  • disabled: no authentication or authorization is performed (default).
  • basic: basic HTTP authentication and RBAC authorization handled using an internal user store defined in a JSON file.
  • jwt-auth: authentication is performed using JSON Web Tokens (JWT), typically issued by an external identity provider. No RBAC authorization is applied.
  • jwt-rbac: authentication is performed using JWTs from an external identity provider, and RBAC authorization is enforced based on the roles present in the JWT.
  • custom: the LIBER*Batch web security component is not activated, for the user to provide their own Web Security component

docs.title: Disabled
docs.text:
When security mode is set to disabled, no authentication or authorization is performed. This is the default mode of operation.

Example configuration

YAML
motorhead:
  # ...
  security:
    mode: disabled

docs.title: Basic
docs.text:
In basic mode, LIBER*Batch uses HTTP basic authentication and manages RBAC authorization using a user store.

Users and their roles are specified in a JSON file, whose path is set with the motorhead.security.users configuration property.

Each user in the JSON file is defined as a JSON object, where the key is the username and the value is an object containing:

  • password: the user's password, encoded with bcrypt and prefixed by {bcrypt}.
  • email: the user's email address.
  • roles: an array listing the roles assigned to the user.

Example configuration

YAML
motorhead:
  # ...
  security:
    mode: basic
    users: 'file:./users.json'

Example users file

JSON
{
  "admin": {
    "password": "{bcrypt}$2a$12$ogEqKNEJYP4xHwv7BWyHpeGuizx4.s7tf2VYnv4.hkHqLAu99HQmm",
    "email": "hand.of.doom@nib-labs.io",
    "roles": [
      "view",
      "submit",
      "alter"
    ]
  },
  "scheduler": {
    "password": "{bcrypt}$2a$12$fDBhxESlLC5.ebgRB0kQNuo0RQEtqIg1YrTlFl3T5X70JvL4GpwZO",
    "email": "herr.professor@nib-labs.io",
    "roles": [
      "submit"
    ]
  },
  "query": {
    "password": "{bcrypt}$2a$12$fDBhxESlLC5.ebgRB0kQNuo0RQEtqIg1YrTlFl3T5X70JvL4GpwZO",
    "email": "hand.of.doom@nib-labs.io",
    "roles": [
      "view"
    ]
  }
}

docs.title: OAUTH2/JWT authentication only
docs.text:
In jwt-auth security mode, LIBER*Batch authenticates users using JSON Web Tokens (JWT) issued by an external identity provider. In this mode, RBAC authorization is not applied.

OAUTH2/JWT authentication is set up using standard Spring configuration properties. At a minimum, you must specify the JWK Set URI so that LIBER*Batch can validate JWT tokens.

Example configuration

YAML
spring:
  # ...
  security:
    oauth2:
      resourceserver:
        jwt:
          issuer-uri: http://localhost:9010/realms/demo

motorhead:
  # ...
  security:
    mode: jwt-auth

docs.title: OAUTH2/JWT with RBAC
docs.text:
In jwt-rbac security mode, LIBER*Batch authenticates users using JSON Web Tokens (JWT) typically issued by an external identity provider. RBAC authorization is then enforced based on the roles included in the JWT.

OAUTH2/JWT authentication is configured using standard Spring properties. At a minimum, you must specify the JWK Set URI so that LIBER*Batch can validate JWT tokens.

Example configuration

YAML
spring:
  # ...
  security:
    oauth2:
      resourceserver:
        jwt:
          issuer-uri: http://localhost:9010/realms/demo

motorhead:
  # ...
  security:
    mode: jwt-rbac
    jwt:
      client-id: testjes-client     # required if roles are namespaced per client

docs.title: Custom
docs.text:
In custom security mode, LIBER*Batch uses a custom provided security component, that must define how users are authenticated and authorized.

The custom provided component must be available on LIBER*Batch classpath and it package must be included in the component-scan. The component must provide a SecurityFilterChain type bean.

Example configuration

YAML

motorhead:
  component-scan: org.sample.mhplugins
  # ...
  security:
    mode: custom
Example security component

Java
@Configuration
@EnableWebSecurity
public class CustomWebSecurity {
    private static Logger log = LoggerFactory.getLogger(CustomWebSecurity.class);
    @Autowired
    private MotorheadConfig config;

    @Bean
    public SecurityFilterChain securityFilterChain(HttpSecurity http) throws Exception {
        log.debug("Custom web security active");
        return http
                .csrf(csrf -> csrf.disable())
                .authorizeHttpRequests((authorize) -> authorize
                        .anyRequest().permitAll()
                )
                .httpBasic(Customizer.withDefaults())
                .build();

    }
}

docs.title: Roles
docs.text:
Roles are identified by a name and have associated permissions that determine access to commands and REST API endpoints.

Their actual names can be defined in the configuration file via the motorhead.security.roles property.

The available roles are:

Role Property Default name Description Allowed APIs View view view Allows querying node/cluster status, classes, and job queues. Every APIs of type GET Alter alter alter Allows modifying node/cluster status, classes, and job queues. Every APIs of type PUT or POST Submit submit submit Allows submitting jobs. Only /job/submit

The following example shows configuration for OAUTH2/JWT authentication with roles names definition:

YAML
spring:
  # ...
  security:
    oauth2:
      resourceserver:
        jwt:
          issuer-uri: http://localhost:9010/realms/demo

motorhead:
  # ...
  security:
    mode: jwt-rbac
    roles:
      view: "batch-operator-view"
      alter: "batch-operator-alter"
      submit: "batch-operators-submit"

The above configuration example customizes the role names used by LIBER*Batch to match those assigned by the identity provider. That means the users must have such roles assigned in the identity management software.

docs.title: Example setup with Keycloak
docs.text:
This section provides a brief example of how to set up LIBER*Batch security using Keycloak as the identity provider.

  1. Install and run Keycloak (refer to the Keycloak documentation for instructions). You can use docker to run Keycloak quickly:

Bash

docker run -p 9010:8080 -e KEYCLOAK_ADMIN=admin -e KEYCLOAK_ADMIN_PASSWORD=admin quay.io/keycloak/keycloak:26.2 start-dev
2. Access the Keycloak admin console at http://localhost:9010 and log in with the admin credentials. 3. Create a new realm (e.g., dev). 4. Create a new client (e.g., dev-client) with the following settings: - Client Protocol: openid-connect - Access Type: public - Valid Redirect URIs: * - Direct Access Grants Enabled: ON 5. Create roles corresponding to LIBERBatch roles (e.g., operator-view, operator-alter, operator-submit). 6. Create users. Make sure all necessary user info (email, name and surname) are provided and that the users have passwords set. For the example purposes, set "Email Verified" to "ON" for each user and make sure no "Required User Actions" are pending. 7. Assign the created roles to the users as needed. 8. Configure LIBERBatch to use the Keycloak instance you just configured for authentication and authorization by setting the following properties in the configuration file:

YAML
  spring:
    # ...
    security:
      oauth2:
        resourceserver:
          jwt:
            issuer-uri: http://localhost:9010/realms/dev          # Check keycloak realm name here

  motorhead:
    security:
      mode: jwt-auth
      roles:
        view: "operator-view"
        alter: "operator-alter"
        submit: "operator-submit"
  1. Test the setup by obtaining a JWT token from Keycloak and using it to access LIBER*Batch APIs. You can obtain a token using the following curl command:
Bash
   ACCESS_TOKEN=$(curl -s --location --request POST 'http://localhost:9010/realms/dev/protocol/openid-connect/token' \
        --header 'Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded' \
        --data-urlencode 'client_id=dev-client' \
        --data-urlencode 'username=john-doe' \
        --data-urlencode 'password=test' \
        --data-urlencode 'grant_type=password' |
        jq -r '.access_token')
  curl -X GET http://localhost:8081/cluster/status \
     -H "Authorization: Bearer $ACCESS_TOKEN"

If you see the JSON response of the API, the setup is successful!

docs.title: REST API Documentation
docs.text:
LIBER*Batch can be controlled using REST web services API. The following APIs are available:

URL Method Description / GET Gets the status of the node. /cluster/status GET Gets the status of the cluster. /cluster/reload PUT Requests to all nodes in the cluster to reload the initiators. /cluster/shutdown PUT Requests to all nodes in the cluster to shutdown and close the cluster. /node/shutdown POST Requests to the nodes in the cluster to shutdown and exit with provided exit code /node/restart/{node} PUT Requests to all or selected nodes in the cluster to shutdown and exit with restart code /job/submit POST Submits a job with the provided script, arguments, job class, and variables. /job/status GET Retrieves the status of a job /job/hold/{jobnum} PUT Puts the specified job on hold. /job/kill/{jobnum} PUT Kills the specified job. /job/release/{jobnum} PUT Releases the specified job from hold. /job/cancel/{jobnum} PUT Cancels the specified job. /job/ps GET Lists all active jobs. /job/list GET Lists completed jobs filtered by class and/or name. /job/backlog GET Lists pending jobs filtered by class. /job/alter POST Alters the state of a job (Hold, Release, Cancel, Kill). /job/purge POST Purges executed jobs data from the long term storage /class/status GET Gets the status of all classes. /class/status/{cls} GET Gets the status of a specific class. /class/alter POST Enables or disables specified classes. /class/enable/{cls} PUT Enables the specified class. /class/disable/{cls} PUT Disables the specified class.

Detailed API docs can be found here:

  • Node control
    • Node Status
    • Node Shutdown
  • Cluster control
    • Cluster Status
    • Cluster Reload
  • Job control
    • Submit
    • Status
    • Hold
    • Kill
    • Release
    • Cancel
    • List
    • Backlog
    • Alter
    • Purge
  • Class control
    • Status
    • Alter
    • Class
    • Class

docs.title: Swagger
docs.text:
LIBER*Batch also provides a Springdoc Swagger UI and API Docs for exploring and testing the REST API. You can access the Swagger UI at the path /swagger-ui.html of every node. For example, if your node is running at http://localhost:8080, you can access the Swagger UI at http://localhost:8080/swagger-ui.html.

swagger can be configured using its own properties prefixed with springdoc.swagger-ui in the LIBER*Batch configuration file.

For more information on configuring Swagger UI, refer to the Springdoc OpenAPI documentation.

docs.title: Disabling Swagger UI
docs.text:
Swagger UI is enabled by default. To disable it, set the following property in the LIBER*Batch configuration file:

YAML
springdoc:
  api-docs:
    enabled: false
  swagger-ui:
    enabled: false

docs.title: Endpoint
docs.text:
• URL: /job/purge
  • Method: POST

docs.title: Request Body
docs.text:
• Type: PurgeJobsRequest
    • Fields:
      • jobname (String): Filters jobs by job name. (Optional)
      • jobclass (String): Filters jobs by job class. (Optional)
      • status (JobStatus): Filters jobs by status. (Optional)
      • submittedBefore (LocalDateTime): Purges jobs submitted before the specified date and time. (Optional)
      • startedBefore (LocalDateTime): Purges jobs started before the specified date and time. (Optional)
      • completedBefore (LocalDateTime): Purges jobs completed before the specified date and time. (Optional)

docs.title: Response
docs.text: • Type: 200 OK

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Purges completed job data using the provided filters such as job name, job class, status, and date thresholds.

docs.title: Exceptions
docs.text: • MotorheadException - Returns 400 Bad Request

docs.title: Request
Response
docs.text:
Text Only
200 OK

docs.title: cURL Example
docs.text: ``bash curl -X POST "[http://yourapiurl.com/job/purge](http://yourapiurl.com/job/purge)" -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '{ "jobname": "JOBDIR01", "jobclass": "A", "status": "Completed", "completedBefore": "2025-01-01T00:00:00" }'

curl -X GET http://yourapiurl.com/job/status/0de183f0-359f-4d0a-9a4e-d05dd181ba15

docs.title: Endpoint
docs.text:
• URL: /job/submit
  • Method: POST

docs.title: Request Body
docs.text:
• Type: SubmitRequest

    • Fields:
      • script (String): The script to be executed.
      • args (String): Arguments for the script.
      • jobClass (String): The class of the job.
      • onHold (boolean): Whether the job should be on hold.
      • variables (Map\<String, String>): Variables for the job.
      • restart (RestartData): The job restart details. (Optional)

  • Type: RestartData

    • Fields:
      • step (String): The step to restart from.
      • jobid (String): The job id to restart from. (Optional)

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
• Type: SubmitResponse
    • Fields:
      • jobRequest (JobRequest): The job request details.
      • response (int): The response code.
      • message (String): The response message.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Submits a job with the provided script, arguments, job class, and variables.

docs.title: Exceptions
docs.text:
• JobNotFoundException - Returns 404 Not Found
  • MotorheadException - Returns 400 Bad Request

Response

curl -X POST http://yourapiurl.com/node/shutdown -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '{"rc": 123, "includeProxy": false}'

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: LIBER*Batch is configured using standard Spring Boot configuration properties.

docs.title: Application Properties
docs.text:
The configuration is contained in the Spring application properties.

The application configuration follows the general Spring Boot configuration structure and rules. Therefore, multiple configuration files can be used to define different profiles and configurations.

The configuration properties are prefixed with motorhead and are used to configure the LIBER*Batch cluster and nodes.

The configuration must include the following sets of properties:

  1. Spring properties to configure the Spring Boot application.
  2. Server properties to configure the server.
  3. Management properties to configure the management endpoints.
  4. LIBER*Batch properties to configure the LIBER*Batch subsystems and components.

docs.title: LIBER*Batch Application Properties
docs.text: Property Description Default motorhead.component-scan The packages to scan for Spring components. . none motorhead.cluster.name The name of the LIBER*Batch cluster. none motorhead.cluster.admin The URL of the main admin end-point. none motorhead.cluster.classes.enabled List of classes defined as enabled in the cluster. none motorhead.cluster.classes.disabled List of classes defined as disabled in the cluster. none motorhead.cluster.classes.default Default job class none motorhead.scripts.allowMultiple If true, multiple scripts can be executed in parallel. false motorhead.scripts.lockDatasets If true, the datasets are locked during the script execution. true motorhead.scripts.path The path where the Groovy scripts are stored. none motorhead.scripts.classpath The classpath for the Groovy scripts and Java programs. none motorhead.scripts.dependencies The path from where scripts and java JAR dependencies are loaded. none motorhead.scripts.config The LIBER*M batch configuration file. none motorhead.redis.config The Redis configuration file. none motorgad.redis.shardingStrategy The key sharding strategy none motorhead.security.mode Security mode (disabled, internal, jwt-auth, jwt-rbac, custom). disabled motorhead.security.users The users configuration file for internal security. none motorhead.security.jwt.client-id The JWT client-id from which extract client roles. motorhead-client motorhead.security.roles.view Role name for the view role. view motorhead.security.roles.alter Role name for the alter role. alter motorhead.security.roles.submit Role name for the submit role. submit motorhead.node.name The name of the node. none motorhead.node.classes List of classes handled by the node. none motorhead.node.type Node type: engine, proxy or one-off . engine motorhead.node.heartbeat The heartbeat interval in seconds. 60 motorhead.node.timeout The node timeout in seconds. 60 motorhead.node.exit.kill if true, running jobs are killed when the node is stopped (SIGTERM) false motorhead.long-term-storage.provider The class name of the long term storage provider none (backbone) motorhead.long-term-storage.config The configuration string of long term storage provider none

docs.title: Scripts paths
docs.text: It is important to understand the meaning of the different paths used in the LIBER*Batch and scripts configuration.

docs.title: LIBER*Batch classpath
docs.text:
Like any Java application, LIBER*Batch uses the classpath to load its classes and resources: its dependencies are defined by its POM file and deployed in the jars directory of the container.

This classpath is available also to the custom scripts and programs executed by LIBER*Batch (namely the application Groovy and Java artefacts).

docs.title: Scripts classpath
docs.text:
The scripts classpath is the classpath for the Java programs executed by LIBER*Batch.

In other words, this is the path containing the classes and resources of the custom application.

docs.title: Scripts path
docs.text:
The scripts path is the path where Groovy scripts are located.

In other words, this is the path containing the scripts of the custom application.

docs.title: Scripts dependencies
docs.text:
The scripts dependencies path is a list of directories containing JAR dependencies to be suffixed to the custom scripts (application) classpath. LIBER*Batch adds all the jars in the directories to the application classpath.

You can use this path to add additional dependencies to the custom application.

docs.title: Long Term Storage
docs.text: The long term storage provider is the mechanism to store the output job queue. For further details, see here.

docs.title: Security
docs.text: When the security is enabled, the users are defined in a JSON file. For further details, see Security.

docs.title: Example Configuration
docs.text:
YAML
spring:
  application:
    name: TESTJES1
  profiles:
    active: NodeA
  mvc:
    dispatch-options-request: true
  cloud:
    config:
      enabled: false
    function:
      scan:
        packages: "com.nib.motorhead"
management:
  endpoints:
    web:
      exposure:
        include: "*"
  metrics:
    enable:
      tomcat: false
      jvm: true
      process: true
      hikaricp: false
      system: true
      jdbc: true
      http: true
      logback: true
    tags:
      cluster: ${spring.application.name}
      environment: "test"
#server:
#  port: 7777
logging:
  config: file:./logback-motorhead.xml
  file:
    path: D:/tmp/motorhead

motorhead:
  cluster:
    name: ${spring.application.name}
    admin: http://localhost:8081
    classes:
      enabled: "A,B,C"
      disabled: "D,E"
      default: "A"
  #      pollTimeout: 1

  scripts:
    allowMultiple: false
    lockDatasets: true
    path: >
      ${NIB_JAVA_HOME}/samples/sample-groovy-batch/src/main/groovy
    classpath: >
      ${NIB_JAVA_HOME}/nib-commons/target/classes,
      ${NIB_JAVA_HOME}/nib-groovy-batch/target/classes,
      ${NIB_JAVA_HOME}/samples/sample-groovy-batch/target/classes
    dependencies:
      ${NIB_JAVA_HOME}\nib-groovy-batch\target\dependencies
    config: ${NIB_JAVA_HOME}/samples/sample-groovy-batch/nibbatch.json
  redis:
    config: 'file:./redisson-localhost.yaml'
  security:
    enabled: internal
    users: 'file:./users.json'
---
spring.config.activate.on-profile: NodeA
server.port: 8081
motorhead:
  node:
    name: NodeA
    classes: "A,B,D"
---
spring.config.activate.on-profile: NodeB
motorhead:
  node:
    name: NodeB
    classes: "A,B,D"
---
spring.config.activate.on-profile: NodeZ
motorhead:
  node:
    classes: "A,B,D"
---
spring.config.activate.on-profile: Proxy
motorhead:
  node:
    name: Proxy
    type: proxy
---
spring.config.activate.on-profile: OneOff
motorhead:
  node:
    name: OneOff
    type: one-off
    classes: "X"

docs.title: Long Term Storage
docs.text:
LIBER*Batch stores the job output queue using the long-term storage provider. The latter is a plug-in than implements the ILongTermStorage interface.

The backbone itself is the default long-term storage provider, but for production systems, it is better to have a dedicate storage system for the output queue, so to avoid storing past jobs info in the in-memory DB.

LIBER*Batch ships with both an RDBMS-based and a MongoDb-based long-term storage providers

docs.title: RDBMS-based long-term storage provider
docs.text:
The LIBER*Batch RDBMS-based long-term storage provider works with PGSQL, DB2, Oracle, SQL Server and MySQL. It can be configured by:

  • setting com.nib.motorhead.rdbms.RdbmsLongTermStorage as the long-term storage class
  • providing the JDBC connection string of the database that will host the job queue in the config property

Example:

YAML
motorhead:
  long-term-storage:
    provider: "com.nib.motorhead.rdbms.RdbmsLongTermStorage"
    config: "jdbc:postgresql://localhost/postgres?user=${PGSQL_USER}&password=${PGSQL_PASSWORD}&ssl=false&currentSchema=MHSCHEMA"

The provider creates a table named MH_JOB_OUTPUT_QUEUE in the database where the job information is stored. Each LIBER*Batch cluster should have its own dedicated database or schema, so that the table contains only the jobs of that cluster.

docs.title: MongoDb-based long-term storage provider
docs.text:
The LIBER*Batch MongoDb-based long-term storage provider works with MongoDB. It can be configured by:

  • setting com.nib.motorhead.mongodb.MongoDbLongTermStorage as the long-term storage class
  • providing the MongoDb connection URI in the config property
YAML
motorhead:
  long-term-storage:
    provider: "com.nib.motorhead.mongodb.MongoDbLongTermStorage"
    config: "mongodb://${MONGO_USER}:${MONGO_PASS}@localhost:27017"

The provider creates a database which name is the same as the cluster name and a collection named mh-job-output-queue in the database.

docs.title: Custom long-term storage provider
docs.text:
It is also possible to create custom long term storage provider, extending the ILongTermStorage interface. The classes implementing this interface must implement the following methods:

Method Purpose initialize initialize the long term storage provider store store the job request list to retrieve a list of stored job requests retrieve to retrieve a single job request

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
LIBER*Batch can be deployed in various environments, including traditional infrastructure, containers, PaaS, and serverless platforms.

In order to set up properly LIBER*Batch, it is key to understand the key configuration aspects that affect its functionality:

  • Class Loading The way the application scripts, classes and dependencies are loaded.
  • Connectivity: The way the LIBER*Batch node connects to the backbone and other components or exposes services.
  • Redis Backbone The Redis backbone.
  • Lifecycle: The lifecycle of the LIBER*Batch nodes.
  • Kubernetes: The deployment of LIBER*Batch in Kubernetes.

docs.title: Class Loading
docs.text:
LIBER*Batch loads application classes (also referred to as customer programs) from two distinct classpaths:

  • Primary Classpath: This is the standard Java classpath from which the LIBER*Batch node loads its own classes. It is defined at the node's startup.
  • Scripts path: A list of path entries (directories) specified in the LIBER*Batch configuration, from which application scripts are loaded.
  • Application Classpath: A list of classpath entries (directories or JARs) specified in the LIBER*Batch configuration, from which application classes are loaded.
  • Application Dependencies: A separate list of directories or JARs, also defined in the LIBER*Batch configuration, that contains dependencies required by customer applications.

The primary classpath is typically used to provide all the classes required by the LIBERBatch node itself, whereas the application classpath* is dedicated to loading customer programs.

docs.title: Primary Classpath
docs.text:
The primary classpath is defined at node startup and serves as the classpath for the LIBER*Batch node's core functionality. It must include all dependencies required by the node, such as JDBC drivers and configuration-related classes.

Additionally, it can include customer application dependencies that do not change frequently. For example, if a customer application relies on a specific package, its JAR files can be added to the primary classpath for efficiency.

The primary classpath is configured using either the CLASSPATH environment variable or the -cp option of the java command.

docs.title: Scripts path
docs.text:
The scripts path is specified in the LIBER*Batch configuration file and is used exclusively for loading customer scripts (groovy). It consists of a simple list of paths that define where application scripts are located.

Typically, this includes directories where application scripts are deployed.

docs.title: Application Classpath
docs.text:
The application classpath is specified in the LIBER*Batch configuration file and is used exclusively for loading customer programs. It consists of a simple list of paths that define where application classes are located.

Typically, this includes directories where application classes are compiled or deployed.

!> Remember that batch applications loading and running behavior is affected by the LIBER*M Commons configuration.

docs.title: Application Dependencies
docs.text:
The application dependencies list, also defined in the LIBER*Batch configuration file, is used to load auxiliary dependencies required by customer applications. It consists of paths that are scanned for JAR files, which are then added to the application classpath.

This is the recommended location for deploying additional packages required by application programs. For instance, if an application uses the JavaFaker library, its JAR files should be placed in this directory.

docs.title: Example Configuration
docs.text:
A sample LIBER*Batch configuration file:

YAML
motorhead:
  #...
  scripts:
    path: >
      /app/app1/src/main/groovy,
      /app/app2/src/main/groovy
    classpath: >
      /app/app1/target/classes,
      /app/app2/target/classes,
    dependencies:
      /app/common/target/dependencies
    config: /config/nibbatch.json

In this configuration, the LIBER*Batch node will:

  • Search for groovy scripts from the directories: /app/app1/src/main/groovy and /app/app2/src/main/groovy.
  • Load the program classes from the directories /app/app1/target/classes and /app/app2/target/classes.
  • Add all JAR files located in /app/common/target/dependencies to the application classpath.

The packages searched for applications programs are defined in the LIBER*M Commons configuration file, nibbatch.json.

docs.title: Configuring Classpaths in a Docker Container
Primary Classpath
docs.text:
The primary classpath can be modified by altering the contents of the /motorhead/jars directory.

The startup script used in the LIBERBatch Docker image automatically includes all JAR files located in /motorhead/jars in the primary classpath. To customize the environment, you can extend the base LIBERBatch Docker image and add your required JAR files to this directory. They will be automatically included in the primary classpath at startup.

docs.title: Application Classpath and Dependencies
docs.text:
Since the scripts path, application classpath and dependencies are configured in the LIBER*Batch configuration file, there are multiple ways to make application classes and JARs available in a containerized environment:

  1. Mounting External Directories:

  2. You can deploy application classes and dependencies at runtime by mounting host directories into the container. These directories should match the paths specified in the LIBER*Batch configuration file.

  3. Including Classes and JARs in the Image:

  4. Alternatively, you can build a custom Docker image that includes the necessary classes and JAR files. This involves creating a Dockerfile that copies the application files into the specified directories before building the image.

docs.title: Conclusion
docs.text: By leveraging these class loading strategies, you can effectively configure LIBER*Batch to load application classes within a Docker environment. Depending on your deployment requirements, you can either mount external dependencies dynamically or bundle them directly into your Docker image.

docs.title: Server Components
docs.text:
LIBER*Batch relies on various supporting components for proper functionality, including:

  • Backbone
  • Long Term Job Output Queue
  • Logging
  • Measuring and Monitoring

docs.title: Backbone
docs.text:
The LIBERBatch backbone is a core component that enables node interoperability and clustering within LIBERBatch.

The backbone is currently available only on Redis. The selected backbone is configured in the LIBER*Batch configuration file by specifying the Redis configuration file.

docs.title: Redis Backbone
docs.text:
LIBER*Batch use a Redis backbone by default(recommended), no setting is required. Just provide the appropriate Redis client configuration file.

YAML
motorhead:
  # ...
  redis:
    config: 'file:./redisson-localhost.yaml'```

LIBER*Batch utilizes the Redisson client (Redisson documentation), requiring configuration in the expected format. Example Redisson configuration:

YAML
singleServerConfig:
  address: "redis://127.0.0.1:6379"

Refer to the Redisson configuration guide for further details.

docs.title: Long Term Job Output Queue
docs.text:
LIBERBatch supports customizable long-term job output queue. By default, LIBERBatch stores the information of the jobs executed in the Redis backbone.

But it is possible to use a custom long term storage manager, by implementing a manager class that implements the interface com.nib.motorhead.ILongTermStorage and configuring it on the LIBER*Batch configuration file, specifying its class name and the configuration data:

YAML
motorhead:
  long-term-storage:
    class: "org.sample.MyLTSManager"
    config: "my config data"

LIBER*Batch includes by default a Rdbms long term storage that stores job information in relational databases such as PgSql, MySql, DB2, MSSQL and Oracle DB. To activate it, configure as follows:

YAML
motorhead:
  long-term-storage:
    class: "com.nib.motorhead.rdbms.RdbmsLongTermStorage"
    config: "jdbc:postgresql://localhost/postgres?user=postgres&password=postgres&ssl=false&currentSchema=darkside"

docs.title: Logging
docs.text:
In a production environment, logs should be shipped to an external aggregator. LIBERBatch leverages SLF4J, allowing integration with any Java logging framework. By default, LIBERBatch uses Logback.

To configure logging, specify the Logback configuration file in the logging section of the LIBER*Batch configuration file.

YAML
logging:
  config: file:./logback.xml
  level:
    com.myapp: INFO

Example Logback configuration for Loki:

XML
<configuration>
    <define name="hostname"
            class="ch.qos.logback.core.property.CanonicalHostNamePropertyDefiner"/>
    <appender name="STDOUT"
              class="ch.qos.logback.core.ConsoleAppender">
        <encoder>
            <pattern>[%date{ISO8601}][%-24t][%-5level] %msg%n</pattern>
        </encoder>
    </appender>
    <appender name="SIFT" class="ch.qos.logback.classic.sift.SiftingAppender">
        <!-- in the absence of the class attribute, it is assumed that the
             desired discriminator type is
             ch.qos.logback.classic.sift.MDCBasedDiscriminator -->
        <discriminator>
            <key>logid</key>
            <defaultValue>motorhead-core</defaultValue>
        </discriminator>

        <sift>
            <appender name="FILE-${logid}" class="ch.qos.logback.core.FileAppender">
                <file>${LOG_PATH}/${logid}.log</file>
                <append>false</append>
                <encoder>
                    <!--                    <pattern>%d [%thread] %level %mdc %logger{35} -%kvp -%msg%n</pattern>-->
                    <pattern>[%date{ISO8601}][%-24t][%-5level] %msg%n</pattern>
                </encoder>
            </appender>
        </sift>
    </appender>
    <appender name="LOKI" class="com.github.loki4j.logback.Loki4jAppender">
        <http>
            <url>http://localhost:3100/loki/api/v1/push</url>
        </http>
        <format>
            <label>
                <pattern>
                    cluster=%mdc{cluster:-none},job=%mdc{jobname:-none},jobnum=%mdc{jobnum:-none},host=${hostname},node=%mdc{node:-none},level=%level,thread=%t
                </pattern>
            </label>
            <message>
                <pattern>%msg %ex</pattern>
            </message>
        </format>
    </appender>
    <root level="info">
        <appender-ref ref="STDOUT"/>
        <appender-ref ref="SIFT"/>
        <appender-ref ref="LOKI"/>
    </root>
</configuration>

Note: MDC values are used to label log messages, facilitating nodes and jobs correlation.

docs.title: Measuring and Monitoring
docs.text:
LIBER*Batch exposes metrics via Micrometer. To enable metrics, configure Spring to expose management endpoints.

YAML
management:
  endpoints:
    web:
      exposure:
        include: "*"
  metrics:
    enable:
      jvm: true
      process: true
      system: true
      jdbc: true
      http: true
      logback: true
    tags:
      region: ${spring.application.name}
      environment: "test"
  tracing:
    enabled: false
    sampling:
      probability: 1.0

Notes: - LIBER*Batch exposes additional custom metrics beyond standard Spring metrics. - Metrics are exposed through Spring Actuator endpoints—ensure proper security in production. - Enable tracing only when distributed tracing is required, as it impacts performance. - Batch application connectivity (i.e. JDBC for E/SQL commands) is configured through the application configuration file(s).

docs.title: Deploying on Kubernetes
docs.text:
LIBER*Batch can be obviously deployed on Kubernetes. The deployment requires the definition of:

  • A set of Deployment for the LIBER*Batch nodes, with different configurations for each node type ( essentially the class(es) that the node initiator(s) handle(s)).
  • A Service for the LIBER*Batch nodes, to expose the node ports and allow the nodes to expose the API. Either a Loadbalancer or a ClusterIP.
  • An Ingress to route requests to your service (in case you are using a ClusterIP).
---
title: Example LIBER*Batch k8s deployment
---
graph LR
    subgraph k8s[☁️Kubernetes Cluster]

      subgraph SN[LIBER*Batch Namespace]
        direction LR
        MH_SVC[⚖️LIBER*Batch LoadBalancer] --> DPL_MH;
        DPL_MH[LIBER*Batch Deployment] --> MOTORHEAD;

        subgraph MOTORHEAD[LIBER*Batch Replicaset]
            direction LR
            Pod1[📦LIBER*Batch Pod 1]
            Pod2[📦LIBER*Batch Pod 2]
            Pod3[📦LIBER*Batch Pod ...]
            PodN[📦LIBER*Batch Pod N]
        end
        Config[⚙️LIBER*Batch ConfigMap] --> MOTORHEAD
        Secret[🔒LIBER*Batch Secret] --> MOTORHEAD
      end
    end 

    MOTORHEAD <--> In-MemoryDB[("⚡In-Memory DB<br/>(REDIS or Hazelcast)")]
    MOTORHEAD <--> RDBMS[("🛢Relational Database<br/>(DB2, PGSQL, MYSQL<br/>ORACLE, MSSQL)")]
    %% MOTORHEAD <-.-> IAM["🔑IAM<br/>(Keycloak, GCP Firebase, AWS IAM, etc.)"] 
    MOTORHEAD -.-> Monitoring["📊Monitoring<br/>(Prometheus, Cloud Watch, etc.)"];  
    MOTORHEAD -.-> Logs["📜Log Aggregators<br/>(Loki, Cloud Logs, etc.)"];
        MOTORHEAD <--> Storage[("💾Persistent Storage")]

    subgraph ASC[Customer Network]
        API[🔗REST API] --> MH_SVC;
        SCHED[⏱️Scheduler] --> MH_SVC;
        CMD[⌨️Command Line] --> MH_SVC
    end

docs.title: Backbone
docs.text: As described in other documents, a LIBERBatch cluster requires a backbone. The Redis backbone can be provided either as a pod of the application or an external service. So you can either deploy a "self-contained" k8s application, that includes the backbone and the LIBERBatch nodes, or you can have your backbone provided by, i.e., as an external SaaS such as AWS ElastiCache or a Redis database running on bare metal.

docs.title: Node Deployment
docs.text:
As mentioned before, the LIBER*Batch nodes are deployed as Deployment.

The simplest set up, is to have all nodes configured with the same configuration, and use a single Deployment for all of them. This means that, essentially, all nodes will serve the same classes. A configuration like this is useful for testing purposes, but not for production, where you want a better control on the number of initiators assigned to each classes as well as the capability to scale in or out the nodes serving a specific class.

Therefore, the recommended approach is to have a Deployment for each node type, with the configuration of the node driven by the Spring profiles. This means you can assign a different number of replicas to each node type, and, ultimately, control the number of initiators assigned to each class.

Please note the Spring profile set in the following example. The NodeA profile is used to configure the node. Another profile will be used for other node types.

YAML
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: motorhead-a
  namespace: motorhead-demo
spec:
  replicas: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: motorhead
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: motorhead
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: motorhead
          image: nib/motorhead
          imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent
          ports:
            - containerPort: 8080
          env:
            - name: SPRING_PROFILES_ACTIVE
              value: NodeA
            - name: NIB_JAVA_LICENSE_KEY
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: nib-java-license
                  key: NIB_JAVA_LICENSE_KEY
          volumeMounts:
            - name: config-volume
              mountPath: /config
            - name: nib-volume
              mountPath: /opt/nib
            - name: data-volume
              mountPath: /data
            - name: logs-volume
              mountPath: /logs
          readinessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /actuator/health
              port: 8080
            initialDelaySeconds: 10
            periodSeconds: 5
            failureThreshold: 3
            timeoutSeconds:
      volumes:
        - name: config-volume
          configMap:
            name: motorhead-config
        - name: nib-volume
          hostPath:
            path: /run/desktop/mnt/host/d/nib/nib-java
        - name: data-volume
          hostPath:
            path: /run/desktop/mnt/host/d/nib/nib-java/samples/aws-carddemo-k8s/data
        - name: logs-volume
          hostPath:
            path: /run/desktop/mnt/host/d/nib/nib-java/samples/aws-carddemo-k8s/logs

Optionally, you can define a readiness probe to check if the node is up and running. The probe can check the /actuator/health endpoint, which is exposed by the Spring Boot actuator.

It goes without saying, that this can be automated with templating tools such as Helm.

docs.title: Node configuration
docs.text:
Node configuration can be done using the ConfigMap object. The ConfigMap is mounted in the pod as a volume, and the configuration is read from there.

You can include any the configuration files in the ConfigMap

  • Node configuration (application.yml)
  • Files referenced by the configuration (i.e. the redis config, logging config, etc.)
  • The application configuration files (i.e. nibbatch.json )
Text Only
kubectl create configmap motorhead-config --namespace motorhead-demo --from-file=./motorhead

docs.title: Secrets
docs.text:
Of course, Secrets are used to store sensitive information, such as the license key, database credentials, etc.

For example, I could have my sensitive info stored in a Secret:

Text Only
kubectl create secret generic my-secret --namespace my-namespace --from-literal=DB_PASSWORD="MySecretPassword"

Mount it in the pod as an environment variable:

YAML
          env:
            - name: DB_PASSWORD
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: my-secret
                  key: DB_PASSWORD

And then use it in the motorhead or application configurations:

JSON
{
  "vsam": {
    "url": "jdbc:postgresql://host.docker.internal/postgres?user=postgres&password=${env:DB_PASSWORD}:&ssl=false&currentSchema=vsam&autosave=always",
    "autocommit": true
  }
}

docs.title: Mount points
docs.text:
The mount points are used to store the application classes, the data files and the logs. Please make sure to mount all the required directories that will be used by the LIBER*Batch nodes as well as by the application programs.

For example:

  • the logs directory, in case you want to log on files
  • the data directory, in case your application stores data on sequential files

docs.title: Logging
docs.text:
LIBER*Batch logs via slf4j and is deployed by default with logback. Therefore, you have maximum flexibility in configuring how and where to log.

In fact, you may configure logs to:

  • ship logs to a log aggregator (i.e. ELK, Splunk, Loki, etc.)
  • ship logs to a managed log service (i.e. AWS CloudWatch Logs, GCP Cloud Logging, etc.)
  • write logs on files

For example, in this logback.xml file, we configure the application to log on files, on the mount point /logs:

XML
<configuration>
    <define name="hostname"
            class="ch.qos.logback.core.property.CanonicalHostNamePropertyDefiner"/>
    <appender name="STDOUT"
              class="ch.qos.logback.core.ConsoleAppender">
        <encoder>
            <pattern>[%date{ISO8601}][%-24t][%-5level] %msg%n</pattern>
        </encoder>
    </appender>
    <appender name="SIFT" class="ch.qos.logback.classic.sift.SiftingAppender">
        <discriminator>
            <key>logid</key>
            <defaultValue>${HOSTNAME:-default-log}</defaultValue>
        </discriminator>

        <sift>
            <appender name="FILE-${logid}" class="ch.qos.logback.core.FileAppender">
                <file>/logs/${logid}.log</file>
                <append>false</append>
                <encoder>
                    <!--                    <pattern>%d [%thread] %level %mdc %logger{35} -%kvp -%msg%n</pattern>-->
                    <pattern>[%date{ISO8601}][%-24t][%-5level] %msg%n</pattern>
                </encoder>
            </appender>
        </sift>
    </appender>
    <logger name="com.nib" level="debug"/>
    <root level="info">
        <appender-ref ref="STDOUT"/>
        <appender-ref ref="SIFT"/>
    </root>
</configuration>

docs.title: Service
docs.text:
The Service is used to expose the node ports and allow the nodes to expose the API. While in other orchestrator, i.e. docker compose, a "Proxy" type node is required to expose the API, in Kubernetes this is not necessary, as the Service can be used to inject incoming traffic to any pod that matches the selector.

So you don't need to define a "Proxy" node deployment. You just need to tag all the pods with the same labels used in the Service.

You can define the service as either LoadBalancer or ClusterIp. If you use the latter you need also an ingress.

YAML
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: motorhead
  namespace: motorhead-demo
  labels:
    app: motorhead
spec:
  selector:
    app: motorhead
  ports:
    - protocol: TCP
      port: 8881
      targetPort: 8080
  type: ClusterIP

docs.title: Ingress
docs.text:
If you are using a ClusterIP service, then you need an Ingress to route HTTP requests from the external world to the cluster IP.

YAML
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: motorhead-ingress
  namespace: motorhead-demo
  annotations:
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/rewrite-target: /
spec:
  ingressClassName: nginx
  rules:
    - host: motorhead.127.0.0.1.nip.io
      http:
        paths:
          - path: /
            pathType: Prefix
            backend:
              service:
                name: motorhead
                port:
                  number: 8881

This will handle all the request going to http://motorhead.127.0.0.1.nip.io/.

docs.title: Jobs
docs.text:
One-off nodes can be started as k8s jobs. You need to define a one-off profile and reference that profile in the k8s job definition.

YAML
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: Job
metadata:
  name: motorhead-class-x
  namespace: motorhead-demo
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: motorhead
          image: nib/motorhead
          imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent
          ports:
            - containerPort: 8080
          env:
            - name: SPRING_PROFILES_ACTIVE
              value: OneOff
            - name: NIB_JAVA_LICENSE_KEY
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: nib-java-license
                  key: NIB_JAVA_LICENSE_KEY
          volumeMounts:
            - name: config-volume
              mountPath: /config
            - name: nib-volume
              mountPath: /opt/nib
            - name: data-volume
              mountPath: /data
            - name: logs-volume
              mountPath: /logs
      restartPolicy: Never
      volumes:
        - name: config-volume
          configMap:
            name: motorhead-config
        - name: nib-volume
          hostPath:
            path: /run/desktop/mnt/host/d/nib/nib-java
        - name: data-volume
          hostPath:
            path: /run/desktop/mnt/host/d/nib/nib-java/samples/aws-carddemo-k8s/data
        - name: logs-volume
          hostPath:
            path: /run/desktop/mnt/host/d/nib/nib-java/samples/aws-carddemo-k8s/logs
  backoffLimit: 2

docs.title: Cluster lifecycle
docs.text:
The LIBER*Batch cluster can be in one of the following states:

  • Stopped: the LIBER*Batch cluster is not running.
  • Starting: the LIBER*Batch cluster is starting up.
  • Running: the LIBER*Batch cluster is running.
  • Stopping: the LIBER*Batch cluster is shutting down.

When a LIBER*Batch node starts:

  • If the backbone data structures are not defined or the cluster status is Stopped, it:
    • sets the cluster status to Starting.
    • initializes the cluster data in the backbone
    • sets the cluster status to Running.
  • if the cluster status is Starting or Running, it attaches to the cluster.
  • if the cluster status is Stopping, the node process is be terminated.

The cluster can be stopped using:

  • the /cluster/shutdown API, or
  • the command line command cluster shutdown.

When a cluster shutdown is requested the status will be set to Stopping and all nodes will be requested shutdown. The last node left, sets the cluster status to Stopped.

docs.title: Application reloading
docs.text:
An important aspect of the LIBER*Batch lifecycle is the ability to reload the application classes while the cluster is running.

LIBER*Batch can reload application classes in two ways:

  • Reloading the classloaders: in this case the application classloader of every initiator is reset so the new version of application classes might be reloaded.
  • Rolling restart: in this case the nodes are restarted one by one, so the application classes are obviously reloaded.

docs.title: ClassLoader refresh
docs.text:
The classloader refresh is the fastest way to reload the application classes. It is triggered by:

  • the /cluster/reload API, or
  • the command line command cluster reload.

When a classloader refresh is requested, every initiator waits for the current job to finish, if any, and then re-creates its application classloader.

docs.title: Rolling restart
docs.text:
The rolling restart is the safest way to reload the application classes. With this strategy, nodes are closed one by one and restarted.

LIBER*Batch manages the correct termination of the node and the exit with the desired exit code. And, of course, the actual restart of the node is done by the underlying service orchestrator (i.e. docker compose, k8s, etc.).

The rolling restart can be triggered by:

  • the /node/shutdown APIs, or
  • the command line commands node shutdown.

The node shutdown allows providing the value to use as exit code by the node process, so to be allow the underlying service orchestrator to decide whether to restart the node or not.

Be aware that, by default, proxy nodes are excluded from the shutdown request. To include them, you can use the --include-proxy option.

For example, in a docker compose environment, you could:

  • configure the node service with restart: on-failure policy to restart the node automatically when it exits with a non-zero exit code
  • use the node shutdown --exit-code 8 command to trigger the rolling restart
  • use the node shutdown with default values command to close nodes.
  • use the cluster shutdown to stop the whole cluster.
