CICSUSER supernaut.security.users.system The system LIBER*TP user. SYSUSER supernaut.security.web-security.auth The user authentication mode for REST API initiated tasks (disabled, jwt, custom) none supernaut.security.web-security.userid-claim-name The name of the JWT claim to be used as userid none supernaut.security.authoriziation.provider Full class names of the User authorization provider none supernaut.security.authoriziation.config The authorization provider configuration string. none supernaut.security.authentication.provider Full class names of the User authentication provider none supernaut.security.authentication.config The authentication provider configuration string. none supernaut.printers.provider Full class names of the printers emulation provider none supernaut.printers.config The printers emulation provider configuration string. com.nib.supernaut.spool.DummySpoolProvider supernaut.jms.ibm-mq If true, the IBM MQ JMS provider is enabled. false supernaut.jms.trigger-monitors The path to the MQ trigger monitors configuration file. none supernaut.datasources.rdbms The RDBMS JDBC configuration file (optional). See Datasources none supernaut.datasources.vsam The VSAM JDBC configuration file (mandatory). See Datasources none supernaut.datasources.catalog The Catalog JDBC configuration file (mandatory). See Datasources none supernaut.datasources.connection-pools.min-pool-size Minimum number of connections kept in the database pools. 2 supernaut.datasources.connection-pools.max-pool-size Maximum number of connections that can be allocated by the pools. 5 supernaut.datasources.connection-pools.borrow-connection-timeout Maximum time in seconds a thread waits for a connection before failing. 30 seconds supernaut.datasources.connection-pools.login-timeout Login timeout in seconds used when establishing new physical connections (zero = infinite). 0 supernaut.datasources.connection-pools.max-idle-time Maximum idle time in seconds before an unused connection can be closed. 60 seconds supernaut.datasources.connection-pools.maintenance-interval Interval in seconds between pool maintenance checks. 60 seconds supernaut.datasources.connection-pools.max-lifetime Maximum lifetime in seconds of a pooled connection (0 means unlimited). 0

docs.title: Example Configuration
docs.text:
YAML
spring:
  application:
    name: TESTCICS
  profiles:
    active: dev
  cloud:
    config:
      enabled: false
    function:
      scan:
        packages: "org.sample.supernaut,com.nib.supernaut"
  lifecycle:
    timeout-per-shutdown-phase: "10s"

server:
  shutdown: graceful

management:
  endpoints:
    web:
      exposure:
        include: "*"
  metrics:
    enable:
      tomcat: false
      jvm: true
      process: true
      hikaricp: false
      system: true
      jdbc: true
      http: true
      logback: true
    tags:
      region: ${spring.application.name}
      environment: "test"
  #    distribution:
  #      percentiles-histogram:
  #        http:
  #          server:
  #            requests=true:
  tracing:
    enabled: true
    sampling:
      probability=: 1.0

supernaut:
  applid: ${spring.application.name}
  sysid: "DARK"
  cwa: 512
  programs:
    packages: >
      org.sample.online.testapp,
      org.sample.online.testapp2
    classpath: >
      ${NIB_JAVA_HOME}/samples/sample-online/target/classes,
      ${NIB_JAVA_HOME}/nib-commons-cobol/target/classes,
    dependencies: >
      ${NIB_JAVA_HOME}/nib-commons-cobol/lib
  component-scan: >
    org.sample.online.servlets,
    org.sample.online.webservices
  transactions: 'file:./pct.json'
  files: 'file:./fct.json'
  tdqueues: 'file:./dct.json'
  terminals:
    tct: 'file:./tct.json'
    typeterm: 'file:./typeterm.json'
    timeout: 1
    # codepage: "classpath:/codepages/ENUS037.cpg"
  bms:
    path: "classpath:/mapsets"
    attributes:
      ascii: false
      convert_space: false
  doctemplate:
    config: 'file:./doctemplate.json'
    path: "classpath:/doctemplates"
  extensions:
    iscobol:
      enabled: false
      cobpath: "${NIB_JAVA_HOME}/samples/sample-iscobol/target/classes"
  security:
    users:
      default: "CICSUSER"
      system: "SYSTEM"
    authorization:
      provider: "com.nib.supernaut.security.RoleBasedAuthorizationProvider"
      config: "file:./roles.json"
    authentication:
      provider: "com.nib.supernaut.security.RdbmsAuthenticationProvider"
      config: "jdbc:postgresql://localhost/postgres?user=postgres&password=postgres&ssl=false&currentSchema=online_security"
  spool:
    provider: "com.nib.supernaut.spool.DummySpoolProvider"
    config: "jdbc:postgresql://localhost/postgres?user=postgres&password=postgres&ssl=false&currentSchema=online_security"
  jms:
    ibm-mq: false
    trigger-monitors: 'file:./mq-trigger-monitors.json'

logging:
  config: file:./logback.xml
  level:
    com.nib: DEBUG

ibm:
  mq:
    queueManager: "QM1"
    channel: "DEV.ADMIN.SVRCONN"
    connName: "localhost(1414)"
    user: "admin"
    password: "passw0rd"

---
spring:
  config:
    activate:
      on-profile: dev

supernaut:
  start-up: "warm"
  ati:
    enabled: true
  backbone:
    storage: redis
    config: 'file:./redisson-localhost.yaml'
  datasources:
    rdbms: 'file:./esql-pgsql-xa.json'
    vsam: 'file:./vsam-pgsql-non-xa.json'
    catalog: 'file:./vsam-pgsql-non-xa.json'

docs.title: Redis backbone client
docs.text:
The Redis backbone uses the Redisson client to connect and use Redis. Of course such file must be defined according to the Redis configuration. For further information on Redisson, please refer to the Redisson documentation.

For example, the following configuration matches a simple Redis setup with only one node:

YAML
singleServerConfig:
address: "redis://127.0.0.1:6379"

This is another example with a redundant Redis configuration with replicas and three sentinels:

YAML
sentinelServersConfig:
  masterName: "mymaster"
  sentinelAddresses:
    - "redis://sentinel-1:26379"
    - "redis://sentinel-2:26379"
    - "redis://sentinel-3:26379"
  timeout: 3000
  masterConnectionPoolSize: 64
  slaveConnectionPoolSize: 64

docs.title: Program Control (PCT)
docs.text:
The PCT is the configuration file that defines the transactions available in the LIBER*TP region.

The PCT is a JSON file that contains an array of transaction resource objects.

Each transaction object contains the following properties:

Property Description id The transaction ID. group The transaction group. program The program name. twaLength The TWA length. timeout The runaway timeout in seconds.

docs.title: Example
Program Loading (PLT)
docs.text:
The PLT is the configuration file that defines the programs to be started at region startup.

The PLT is a JSON file that contains an array of startup program resource objects.

Each program object contains the following properties:

Property Description id The transaction ID. group The transaction group. program The program name. data An optional data string to be provided to the program mode The node startup mode in which the program should be started

docs.title: Startup modes
docs.text: The program can be started in one of the following modes: - warm - The program is started always when the node is started. - cold - The program is started only when the node is started with in cold mode.

docs.title: Example
TCP Services
docs.text:
TCP Services are configured using a JSON file that defines the characteristics of the TCP Services.

The JSON file includes and array of TCP service objects. Each tcp service object contains the following properties:

Property Description Default serviceType Type of the service (Ezalistener or Ctg). Ezalistener name The name of the TCP service. port Port number to listen on. 0 address IP address or hostname to bind. localhost userid User ID for authentication or identification. ssl Whether SSL is enabled for the service. false keystore Path to the SSL keystore file. keystorePassword Password for the SSL keystore. requireClientAuth Whether client authentication is required. Defaults to false. false

docs.title: Service types
docs.text:
Two types of TCP services can be configured:

  • Ctg: CTG-like service
  • Ezalistener: EZASOKET-like TCP listener

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
JSON
{
  "TcpServices": [
    {
      "serviceType": "Ezalistener",
      "name": "OZZY",
      "address": "localhost",
      "port": "2323",
      "userid": "EZASOKET"
    },
    {
      "serviceType": "Ezalistener",
      "name": "GEEZER",
      "address": "localhost",
      "port": "2324",
      "userid": "EZASOKET",
      "ssl": true,
      "keystore": "keystore.p12",
      "keystorePassword": "lafavaelarava",
      "requireClientAuth": "false"
    },
    {
      "serviceType": "Ctg",
      "name": "CTG",
      "address": "localhost",
      "port": "2325"
    },
    {
      "serviceType": "Ctg",
      "name": "CTGSSL",
      "address": "localhost",
      "port": "2326",
      "ssl": true,
      "keystore": "keystore.p12",
      "keystorePassword": "lafavaelarava"
    }
  ]
}

docs.title: Terminal Control (TCT)
docs.text:
The TCT is the configuration file that individually defines the terminals available in the LIBER*TP region.

The TCT is a JSON file that contains an array of terminal resource objects.

Each terminal object contains the following properties:

Property Description id The terminal ID. group The terminal group. type The terminal type (Virtual, Printer or Tn3270). netname The terminal network name. ipAddress The terminal IP address.

docs.title: Example
Terminal Types (TYPETERM)
docs.text:
The TYPETERM is the configuration file that defines groups of terminals available in the LIBER*TP region.

The TYPETERM is a JSON file that contains an array of typeterm objects.

Each typeterm object contains the following properties:

Property Description Id The terminal ID pattern. Group The terminal group. Size The group size. Type The terminal type. Netname The terminal network name pattern. IpAddress The terminal IP address pattern.

docs.title: Example
Overview
docs.text:
LIBER*TP can be deployed in various environments, including traditional infrastructure, containers, PaaS, and serverless platforms.

In order to set up properly LIBER*TP, it is key to understand the key configuration aspects that affect its functionality:

  • Services The LIBER*TP services.
  • Class Loading The way the application classes and dependencies are loaded.
  • Connectivity: The way the LIBER*TP node connects to the backbone and other components or exposes services.
  • Connection Pooling: The way the LIBER*TP node manages threads and database connections.
  • Redis Backbone The Redis backbone.
  • Lifecycle: The lifecycle of the LIBER*TP region.
  • Kubernetes: The deployment of LIBER*TP in Kubernetes.

docs.title: Class Loading
docs.text:
LIBER*TP loads application classes (also referred to as customer programs) from two distinct classpaths:

  • Primary Classpath: This is the standard Java classpath from which the LIBER*TP node loads its own classes. It is defined at the node's startup.
  • Application Classpath: A list of classpath entries (directories or JARs) specified in the LIBER*TP configuration, from which application classes are loaded.
  • Application Dependencies: A separate list of directories or JARs, also defined in the LIBER*TP configuration, that contains dependencies required by customer applications.

The primary classpath is typically used to provide all the classes required by the LIBERTP node itself, whereas the application classpath* is dedicated to loading customer programs.

docs.title: Primary Classpath
docs.text:
The primary classpath is defined at node startup and serves as the classpath for the LIBER*TP node's core functionality. It must include all dependencies required by the node, such as JDBC drivers and configuration-related classes.

Additionally, it can include customer application dependencies that do not change frequently. For example, if a customer application relies on a specific package, its JAR files can be added to the primary classpath for efficiency.

The primary classpath is configured using either the CLASSPATH environment variable or the -cp option of the java command.

docs.title: Application Classpath
docs.text:
The application classpath is specified in the LIBER*TP configuration file and is used exclusively for loading customer programs. It consists of a simple list of paths that define where application classes are located.

Typically, this includes directories where application classes are compiled or deployed.

docs.title: Application Dependencies
docs.text:
The application dependencies list, also defined in the LIBER*TP configuration file, is used to load auxiliary dependencies required by customer applications. It consists of paths that are scanned for JAR files, which are then added to the application classpath.

This is the recommended location for deploying additional packages required by application programs. For instance, if an application uses the JavaFaker library, its JAR files should be placed in this directory.

docs.title: Example Configuration
docs.text:
A sample LIBER*TP configuration file:

YAML
supernaut:
  programs:
    packages: >
      com.acme.myapp,
      com.acme.myapp.routines
    classpath: >
      /myapp/online/target/classes,
      /myapp/routines/target/classes,
    dependencies: >
      /myapp/lib

In this configuration, the LIBER*TP node will:

  • Search for classes within the specified packages: com.acme.myapp and com.acme.myapp.routines.
  • Load these classes from the directories /myapp/online/target/classes and /myapp/routines/target/classes.
  • Add all JAR files located in /myapp/lib to the application classpath.

docs.title: Configuring Classpaths in a Docker Container
Primary Classpath
docs.text:
The primary classpath can be modified by altering the contents of the /supernaut/jars directory.

The startup script used in the LIBERTP Docker image automatically includes all JAR files located in /supernaut/jars in the primary classpath. To customize the environment, you can extend the base LIBERTP Docker image and add your required JAR files to this directory. They will be automatically included in the primary classpath at startup.

docs.title: Application Classpath and Dependencies
docs.text:
Since the application classpath and dependencies are configured in the LIBER*TP configuration file, there are multiple ways to make application classes and JARs available in a containerized environment:

  1. Mounting External Directories:

  2. You can deploy application classes and dependencies at runtime by mounting host directories into the container. These directories should match the paths specified in the LIBER*TP configuration file.

  3. Including Classes and JARs in the Image:

  4. Alternatively, you can build a custom Docker image that includes the necessary classes and JAR files. This involves creating a Dockerfile that copies the application files into the specified directories before building the image.

docs.title: Conclusion
docs.text: By leveraging these class loading strategies, you can effectively configure LIBER*TP to load application classes within a Docker environment. Depending on your deployment requirements, you can either mount external dependencies dynamically or bundle them directly into your Docker image.

docs.title: Thread Pools and Database Connection Pool Sizing in {supernaut.name}
Overview
docs.text:
{supernaut.name} executes workloads originating from two different sources:

  1. Terminal-driven tasks (REST driven workloads, i.e. TN3270, Angular clients or REST CTG)
  2. Background tasks (asynchronous workloads, i.e. ATI, TCPIP services or MQ originated task)

These workloads are executed on different thread pools, but they share the same database connection pool ( Atomikos). Correct sizing of these pools is essential to avoid contention, latency spikes, and thread pile-ups.

This section describes how the main configuration parameters interact and provides recommended sizing guidelines.

docs.title: REST originated tasks
docs.text:
REST requests (for example TN3270 terminal interactions) are handled by the HTTP container thread pool.

In a standard Spring Boot deployment, this is the Tomcat thread pool.

Typical examples include:

  • TN3270 requests
  • Angular client requests
  • REST CTG requests
Text Only
        Terminal request
               │
               ▼
        Tomcat Thread Pool
    (server.tomcat.threads.max)
               │
               ▼
  {supernaut.name} task execution
       │                │
       ▼                ▼
     E/SQL             VSAM 
    database         database
     access           access

docs.title: Background Tasks
docs.text:
{supernaut.name} executes background workloads using the Spring Task Executor.

Typical examples include:

  • MQ processing
  • CTG requests
  • internal scheduled tasks
  • asynchronous processing
Text Only
        Background task
               │
               ▼
   Spring ThreadPoolTaskExecutor
(spring.task.execution.pool.max-size)
               │
               ▼
  {supernaut.name} task execution
       │                │
       ▼                ▼
     E/SQL             VSAM 
    database         database
     access           access

Each running background task occupies one executor thread.

docs.title: Database Connection Pool
docs.text:
All workloads share the same Atomikos connection pool.

Text Only
Tomcat threads ─┐
                │
Spring executor ├──► Atomikos connection pool
                │
Other tasks  ───┘

docs.title: Connection Pool Size
docs.text:
This parameter maxPoolSize defines the maximum number of concurrent database connections.

If the pool is exhausted:

  • threads block waiting for a connection
  • request latency increases
  • thread pools can become saturated

docs.title: Connection Wait Timeout
docs.text:
The parameter borrowConnectionTimeout defines how long a thread waits for a connection.

This value defines the maximum time (in seconds) a thread waits for a database connection from the pool.

Behavior:

  • If a connection becomes available within the timeout, the task continues normally.
  • If the timeout expires, Atomikos throws a connection acquisition exception and the transaction fails.

If the timeout is too large:

  • Tomcat threads may remain blocked for long periods
  • thread pools can accumulate waiting requests
  • latency can increase dramatically under load

If the timeout is too small:

  • transient spikes may cause unnecessary transaction failures

docs.title: Recommended values
docs.text:
For interactive workloads typical of {supernaut.name}:

workload type recommended value online transactions 5–10 seconds batch / background workloads 10–30 seconds

This ensures that connection pool saturation is detected quickly and prevents long thread blockages.

docs.title: Concurrency Model
docs.text:
The maximum number of concurrent database operations is limited by the smallest of the following:

Text Only
min(
    Tomcat maxThreads,
    Spring executor maxPoolSize,
    Atomikos maxPoolSize
)

However, in practice the limiting factor is usually Atomikos maxPoolSize, because it controls how many database transactions can execute simultaneously.

docs.title: Step 1 — Determine Online Concurrency
docs.text:
Configure the Tomcat thread pool based on expected terminal activity.

Example:

YAML
server:
  tomcat:
    threads:
      max: 100

This allows up to 100 concurrent terminal requests.

docs.title: Step 2 — Configure Background Parallelism
docs.text:
Limit background parallelism so it does not overwhelm the database.

Example:

YAML
spring:
  task:
    execution:
      pool:
        core-size: 5
        max-size: 10
        queue-capacity: 0

Typical values:

workload type recommended max light background activity 5–10 heavy integration workloads 10–20

docs.title: Step 3 — Size the Datasource Database Pool
docs.text:
The Atomikos pool must support both terminal and background workloads.

A good starting rule is:

Text Only
Atomikos maxPoolSize ≈ 25–40% of Tomcat maxThreads

Example:

Text Only
Tomcat threads: 100
Background threads: 10
Atomikos maxPoolSize: 30–40

This prevents excessive concurrency on the database while maintaining good throughput.

Note: The Atomikos pool size is defined in Supernaut properties for each datasource pool. See Datasources.

docs.title: Example Configuration
docs.text:
Example production configuration:

YAML
server:
  tomcat:
    threads:
      max: 100

spring:
  task:
    execution:
      pool:
        core-size: 5
        max-size: 10
        queue-capacity: 0
        keep-alive: 30s

Connection pool configuration:

YAML
supernaut:
  datasources:
    connection-pools:
      min-pool-size: 5
      max-pool-size: 30
      borrow-connection-timeout: 10

Concurrency limits:

component limit terminal tasks 100 background tasks 10 DB transactions 30

docs.title: Avoiding Thread Pile-Ups
docs.text:
If the Atomikos pool is too small relative to incoming requests:

  • Tomcat threads accumulate waiting for connections
  • latency increases sharply
  • the system may appear stalled

The borrowConnectionTimeout parameter helps prevent long blocking periods by forcing requests to fail quickly when the connection pool is exhausted.

If the pool is too large:

  • database contention increases
  • lock conflicts rise
  • query performance degrades

A moderate connection pool is usually optimal.

docs.title: Monitoring Recommendations
docs.text:
Monitor the following metrics:

metric meaning Tomcat busy threads online concurrency TaskExecutor active threads background workload Atomikos active connections database load

When tuning the system:

  • if Atomikos active ≈ maxPoolSize frequently → increase pool size
  • if DB CPU or lock contention rises → reduce pool size
  • if many requests fail with connection acquisition timeout → increase pool size or reduce concurrency

docs.title: Typical Production Values
docs.text:
For many {supernaut.name} deployments:

parameter typical value Tomcat maxThreads 80–150 TaskExecutor maxPoolSize 5–15 Atomikos maxPoolSize 25–40 borrowConnectionTimeout 5–10 s

Actual optimal values depend on:

  • database capacity
  • transaction duration
  • workload characteristics.

docs.title: Server Components
docs.text:
LIBER*TP and customer applications rely on various supporting components for proper functionality, including:

  • Backbone
  • File Catalog and VSAM JDBC Connection
  • E/SQL JDBC Connection
  • JMS Messaging Connections and Listeners
  • Logging
  • Measuring and Monitoring

docs.title: Backbone
docs.text:
The LIBERTP backbone is a core component that enables node interoperability and clustering within LIBERTP.

The backbone can be hosted on either Redis or Hazelcast. The selected backbone is configured in the LIBER*TP configuration file by specifying:

  • The type of backbone (Redis or Hazelcast)
  • The corresponding configuration file

docs.title: Redis Backbone
docs.text:
To configure LIBER*TP to use a Redis backbone (recommended), set the storage type to redis and provide the appropriate Redis client configuration file.

YAML
supernaut:
  #...
  backbone:
    storage: redis
    config: 'file:./redisson-localhost.yaml'

LIBER*TP utilizes the Redisson client (Redisson documentation), requiring configuration in the expected format. Example Redisson configuration:

YAML
singleServerConfig:
  address: "redis://127.0.0.1:6379"

Refer to Redis Backbone and Redisson configuration guide for further details.

docs.title: Hazelcast Backbone
docs.text:
To configure LIBER*TP to use a Hazelcast backbone, set the storage type to hazelcast.

Hazelcast configuration is automatically loaded from the default configuration files in the LIBER*TP working directory.

YAML
supernaut:
  #...
  backbone:
    storage: hazelcast

Example Hazelcast client configuration:

YAML
hazelcast-client:
  cluster-name: local-development
  network:
    cluster-members:
      - 127.0.0.1:5701
      - 127.0.0.1:5702
      - 127.0.0.1:5703
    outbound-ports:
      - 34600
      - 34700-34710
    smart-routing: true
    redo-operation: true
    connection-timeout: 60000

For additional details, refer to the Hazelcast configuration documentation.

docs.title: File Catalog and VSAM JDBC Connection
docs.text:
Both the VSAM and file catalog are stored in a database. To configure them, provide the relevant JDBC connection details in the datasources section of the LIBER*TP configuration file.

  • VSAM Connection: A JDBC connection to the database storing VSAM data. This connection requires JTA (Atomikos) control and must be configured as an XA connection.
  • Catalog Connection: A JDBC connection to the database storing the file catalog. Typically, this is the same database as VSAM but used in read-only mode, not requiring an XA connection or JTA control.
YAML
supernaut:
  #...
  datasources:
    vsam:
      driver: org.postgresql.Driver
      url: 'jdbc:postgresql://localhost/postgres?user=postgres&password=postgres&ssl=false&currentSchema=vsam&autosave=always'
      xa:
        driver: org.postgresql.xa.PGXADataSource
        user: 'postgres'
        password: 'postgres'
        encryption: false
    catalog:
      driver: org.postgresql.Driver
      url: 'jdbc:postgresql://localhost/postgres?user=postgres&password=postgres&ssl=false&currentSchema=vsam&autosave=always'

docs.title: E/SQL JDBC Connection
docs.text:
The E/SQL JDBC connection is used by application programs for executing SQL commands. Since it handles both read and write operations, it must be managed under JTA (Atomikos) and configured as an XA connection.

YAML
supernaut:
  #...
  datasources:
    rdbms:
      driver: org.postgresql.Driver
      url: 'jdbc:postgresql://localhost/postgres?user=postgres&password=postgres&ssl=false&currentSchema=darkside'
      xa:
        driver: org.postgresql.xa.PGXADataSource
        user: 'postgres'
        password: 'postgres'
        encryption: false

Note: This connection is required only if the customer application executes E/SQL commands.

docs.title: JMS Messaging Connections and Listeners
docs.text:
If the application utilizes JMS messaging, configure the JMS connection details in the jms section of the LIBER*TP configuration file.

For IBM MQ, specify queue manager details in the configuration file.

YAML
supernaut:
  jms:
    ibm-mq: false
    trigger-monitors: 'file:./mq-trigger-monitors.json'

ibm:
  mq:
    queueManager: "QM1"
    channel: "DEV.ADMIN.SVRCONN"
    connName: "localhost(1414)"
    user: "admin"
    password: "passw0rd"

docs.title: Logging
docs.text:
In a production environment, logs should be shipped to an external aggregator. LIBERTP leverages SLF4J, allowing integration with any Java logging framework. By default, LIBERTP uses Logback.

To configure logging, specify the Logback configuration file in the logging section of the LIBER*TP configuration file.

YAML
logging:
  config: file:./logback.xml
  level:
    com.myapp: INFO

Example Logback configuration for Loki:

XML
<configuration debug="false">
    <statusListener class="ch.qos.logback.core.status.NopStatusListener" />
    <define name="hostname"
            class="ch.qos.logback.core.property.CanonicalHostNamePropertyDefiner"/>
    <appender name="STDOUT"
              class="ch.qos.logback.core.ConsoleAppender">
        <encoder>
            <pattern>[%date{ISO8601}][%-24t][%-5level] %msg%n</pattern>
        </encoder>
    </appender>
    <appender name="LOKI" class="com.github.loki4j.logback.Loki4jAppender">
        <http>
            <url>http://localhost:3100/loki/api/v1/push</url>
        </http>
        <format>
            <label>
                <pattern>region=%mdc{region:-none},host=${hostname},transid=%mdc{transid:-none},termid=%mdc{termid:-none},program=%mdc{program:-none},tasknum=%mdc{tasknum:-none},traceID=%X{traceId:-},spanID=%X{spanId:-},level=%level,thread=%t</pattern>
            </label>
            <message>
                <pattern>%msg %ex</pattern>
            </message>
        </format>
    </appender>
    <root level="info">
        <appender-ref ref="STDOUT" />
        <appender-ref ref="LOKI" />
    </root>
</configuration>

Note: MDC values are used to label log messages, facilitating transaction and terminal correlation.

docs.title: Measuring and Monitoring
docs.text:
LIBER*TP exposes metrics via Micrometer. To enable metrics, configure Spring to expose management endpoints.

YAML
management:
  endpoints:
    web:
      exposure:
        include: "*"
  metrics:
    enable:
      jvm: true
      process: true
      system: true
      jdbc: true
      http: true
      logback: true
    tags:
      region: ${spring.application.name}
      environment: "test"
  tracing:
    enabled: false
    sampling:
      probability: 1.0

Notes: - LIBER*TP exposes additional custom metrics beyond standard Spring metrics. - Metrics are exposed through Spring Actuator endpoints—ensure proper security in production. - Enable tracing only when distributed tracing is required, as it impacts performance.

docs.title: Deploying on Kubernetes
docs.text:
LIBER*TP can be obviously deplyed on Kubernetes. The deployment requires the definition of:

  • A Deployment for the LIBER*TP nodes
  • A Service for the LIBER*TP nodes, to expose the node ports and allow the nodes to expose the API. Either a Loadbalancer or a ClusterIP.
  • An Ingress to route requests to your service (in case you are using a ClusterIP).

Optionally, if you plan to use a Tn3270 gateway, you can also deploy a Deployment and a service for the Tn3270 gateway.

---
title: Example LIBER*TP k8s deployment
---
graph LR
    subgraph k8s[☁️Kubernetes Cluster]

      subgraph FE[Front-end Namespace]
      direction LR
          IFE[Ingress 🌐] --> ISVC;
          ISVC[Angular Deployment] --> IDPL[Front-end Deployment]
          IDPL --> ANGULAR; 
          subgraph ANGULAR[Front-end Replicaset]
            direction LR
            Ang1[📦Front-end Pod 1]
            Ang2[📦Front-end Pod ...]
            Ang2[📦Front-end Pod N]
        end
      end

      subgraph SN[LIBER*TP Namespace]
        direction LR
        ANGULAR --> Ingress;
        Ingress[🌐Ingress] --> SVC_SN;
        SVC_SN[LIBER*TP Service] --> DPL_SN;
        DPL_SN[LIBER*TP Deployment] --> SUPERNAUT;

        subgraph SUPERNAUT[LIBER*TP Replicaset]
            direction LR
            Pod1[📦LIBER*TP Pod 1]
            Pod2[📦LIBER*TP Pod 2]
            Pod3[📦LIBER*TP Pod ...]
            PodN[📦LIBER*TP Pod N]
        end
        Config[⚙️LIBER*TP ConfigMap] --> SUPERNAUT
        Secret[🔒LIBER*TP Secret] --> SUPERNAUT

        TN_SVC[⚖️Tn3270 Loadbalancer] --> TN3270;
        subgraph TN3270[Tn3270 Replicaset]
              direction LR
              Tn1[📦Tn3270 Pod 1]
              Tn2[📦Tn3270 Pod ...]
              TnN[📦Tn3270 Pod N]
        end
        TN3270 --> SVC_SN;
        Config --> TN3270;
        Secret --> TN3270;
      end

    end 

    SUPERNAUT <--> In-MemoryDB[("⚡In-Memory DB<br/>(REDIS or Hazelcast)")]
    SUPERNAUT <--> RDBMS[("🛢Relational Database<br/>(DB2, PGSQL, MYSQL<br/>ORACLE, MSSQL)")]
    SUPERNAUT <-.-> IAM["🔑IAM<br/>(Keycloak, GCP Firebase, AWS IAM, etc.)"] 
    SUPERNAUT -.-> Monitoring["📊Monitoring<br/>(Prometheus, Cloud Watch, etc.)"];  
    SUPERNAUT -.-> Logs["📜Log Aggregators<br/>(Loki, Cloud Logs, etc.)"];
    TN3270 -.-> Monitoring

    subgraph ASC[Customer Network]
        AS1[🌐Web Apps & Application Servers] --> Ingress;
        Terminals[💻3270 Terminals] --> TN_SVC
        Browsers[🌐Web Browsers] --> IFE;
    end

docs.title: Backbone
docs.text:
As described in other documents, a LIBER*TP region requires a backbone.

A backbone can be provided either as a pod of the application or an external service. So you can either deploy a " self-contained" k8s application, that includes the backbone and the LIBER*TP nodes, or you can have your backbone provided by, i.e., as an external SaaS such as AWS ElastiCache or a Redis database running on bare metal.

docs.title: Node Deployment
docs.text:
As mentioned before, the LIBER*TP nodes are deployed as Deployment. We suggest to define a replica set sufficiently large to handle the workload of your application.

YAML
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: supernaut
  namespace: supernaut-demo
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: supernaut
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: supernaut
    spec:
      initContainers:
        - name: wait-for-redis
          image: busybox
          command: [ 'sh', '-c', 'until nc -z redis 6379; do echo waiting for redis; sleep 2; done' ]
      containers:
        - name: supernaut
          image: nib/supernaut
          imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent
          ports:
            - containerPort: 8080
          env:
            - name: NIB_JAVA_LICENSE_KEY
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: nib-java-license
                  key: NIB_JAVA_LICENSE_KEY
          volumeMounts:
            - name: config-volume
              mountPath: /config
            - name: bms-volume
              mountPath: /bms
            - name: classes-volume
              mountPath: /classes
            - name: nib-volume
              mountPath: /opt/nib
          readinessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /actuator/health
              port: 8080
            initialDelaySeconds: 10
            periodSeconds: 5
            failureThreshold: 3
            timeoutSeconds: 2
      volumes:
        - name: config-volume
          configMap:
            name: supernaut-config
        - name: bms-volume
          hostPath:
            path: /run/desktop/mnt/host/d/nib/nib-java/samples/aws-carddemo-java/target/classes/bms
        - name: classes-volume
          hostPath:
            path: /run/desktop/mnt/host/d/nib/nib-java/samples/aws-carddemo-java/target/classes
        - name: nib-volume
          hostPath:
            path: /run/desktop/mnt/host/d/nib/nib-java

As in the example, you can define a readiness probe to check if the node is up and running. The probe can check the /actuator/health endpoint, which is exposed by the Spring Boot actuator.

docs.title: Node configuration
docs.text:
Node configuration can be done using the ConfigMap object. The ConfigMap is mounted in the pod as a volume, and the configuration is read from there.

You can include any the configuration files in the ConfigMap

  • Node configuration (application.yml)
  • Files referenced by the configuration (i.e. the redis config, logging config, etc.)
  • Resources referenced by the configuration (i.e. pct.json, fct.json, etc.)
  • Any other application configuration files
Text Only
kubectl create configmap supernaut-config --namespace supernaut-demo --from-file=./supernaut

docs.title: Secrets
docs.text:
Of course, Secrets are used to store sensitive information, such as the license key, database credentials, etc.

For example, I could have my sensitive info stored in a Secret:

Text Only
kubectl create secret generic my-secret --namespace my-namespace --from-literal=DB_PASSWORD="MySecretPassword"

Mount it in the pod as an environment variable:

YAML
          env:
            - name: DB_PASSWORD
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: my-secret
                  key: DB_PASSWORD

And then use it in the supernaut configurations:

JSON
{
  "name": "pgsql-non-xa",
  "xa": false,
  "driver": "org.postgresql.Driver",
  "url": "jdbc:postgresql://host.docker.internal/postgres?user=postgres&password=${env:DB_PASSWORD}&ssl=false&currentSchema=darkside"
}

docs.title: Mount points
docs.text: The mount points are used to store the application classes, bms json files, the data files and the logs. Please make sure to mount all the required directories that will be used by the LIBER*TP nodes as well as by the application programs.

docs.title: Logging
docs.text:
LIBER*TP logs via slf4j and is deployed by default with logback. Therefore, you have maximum flexibility in configuring how and where to log.

In fact, you may configure logs to:

  • ship logs to a log aggregator (i.e. ELK, Splunk, Loki, etc.)
  • ship logs to a managed log service (i.e. AWS CloudWatch Logs, GCP Cloud Logging, etc.)
  • write logs just on files or stdout and gather via kubernetes or other log capture daemons

For example, in this logback.xml file, we configure the application to log on files, on the mount point /logs:

XML
<configuration>
    <define name="hostname"
            class="ch.qos.logback.core.property.CanonicalHostNamePropertyDefiner"/>
    <appender name="STDOUT"
              class="ch.qos.logback.core.ConsoleAppender">
        <encoder>
            <pattern>[%date{ISO8601}][%-24t][%-5level] %msg%n</pattern>
        </encoder>
    </appender>
    <logger name="com.nib" level="debug"/>
    <root level="info">
        <appender-ref ref="STDOUT"/>
    </root>
</configuration>

docs.title: Service
docs.text:
The Service is used to expose the node ports and allow the nodes to expose the API. You can use either a LoadBalancer or a ClusterIP.

YAML
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: supernaut
  namespace: supernaut-demo
  labels:
    app: supernaut
spec:
  selector:
    app: supernaut
  ports:
    - protocol: TCP
      port: 8880
      targetPort: 8080
  type: ClusterIP

docs.title: Ingress
docs.text:
If you are using a ClusterIP service, then you need an Ingress to route HTTP requests from the external world to the cluster IP.

YAML
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: supernaut-ingress
  namespace: supernaut-demo
  annotations:
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/rewrite-target: /
spec:
  ingressClassName: nginx
  rules:
    - host: supernaut.127.0.0.1.nip.io
      http:
        paths:
          - path: /
            pathType: Prefix
            backend:
              service:
                name: supernaut
                port:
                  number: 8880

This will handle all the request going to http://supernaut.127.0.0.1.nip.io/.

docs.title: Tn3270 Gateway
docs.text:
If you plan to use a Tn3270 gateway, you can also deploy a Deployment and a Service for the Tn3270 gateway.

The only mount point required is the config mount point, which is used to store the configuration files ( tn3270.yaml).

YAML
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: tn3270-gw
  namespace: supernaut-demo
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: tn3270-gw
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: tn3270-gw
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: tn3270-gw
          image: nib/tn3270
          imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent
          ports:
            - containerPort: 3270
          env:
            - name: NIB_JAVA_LICENSE_KEY
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: nib-java-license
                  key: NIB_JAVA_LICENSE_KEY
          volumeMounts:
            - name: config-volume
              mountPath: /config
      volumes:
        - name: config-volume
          configMap:
            name: supernaut-config

The Service must expose the TCP port used by tn3270 terminals to connect. Therefore, the service must be of type NodePort or LoadBalancer.

YAML
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: tn3270-gw
  namespace: supernaut-demo
spec:
  type: LoadBalancer
  selector:
    app: tn3270-gw
  ports:
    - name: tn3270-gw
      protocol: TCP
      port: 3270
      targetPort: 3270

docs.title: region lifecycle
docs.text:
The LIBER*TP region can be in one of the following states:

  • Stopped: the LIBER*TP region is not running.
  • Starting: the LIBER*TP region is starting up.
  • Running: the LIBER*TP region is running.
  • Stopping: the LIBER*TP region is shutting down.

When a LIBER*TP node starts:

  • If the backbone data structures are not defined or the region status is Stopped, it:
    • sets the region status to Starting.
    • performs a "Cold Start" of the region, which means that it creates the backbone data structures and loads and verifies the region resources (PCT, FCT, etc.)
    • sets the region status to Running.
  • if the region status is Starting or Running, it attaches to the region.
  • if the region status is Stopping, the node process is be terminated.

The region can be stopped using the ShutdownRegion command of the /admin API,

When a region shutdown is requested the status will be set to Stopping and all nodes will be requested shutdown. The last node left, sets the region status to Stopped.

docs.title: Application reloading
docs.text:
An important aspect of the LIBER*TP lifecycle is the ability to reload the application classes while the region is running.

LIBER*TP can reload application classes in two ways:

  • Reloading the classloaders: in this case the application classloader of every node is reset so the new version of application classes might be reloaded.
  • Rolling restart: in this case the nodes are restarted one by one, so the application classes are obviously reloaded.

docs.title: ClassLoader refresh
docs.text:
The classloader refresh is the fastest way to reload the application classes. It is triggered by the ReloadClassloader command of the /admin API.

When a classloader refresh is requested, every node waits for the current job to finish, if any, and then re-creates its application classloader.

docs.title: Rolling restart
docs.text:
The rolling restart is the safest way to reload the application classes. With this strategy, nodes are closed one by one and restarted.

LIBER*TP manages the correct termination of the node and the exit with the desired exit code. And, of course, the actual restart of the node is done by the underlying service orchestrator (i.e. docker compose, k8s, etc.).

The rolling restart can be triggered by the ShutdownNode command of the /admin API, passing a exitCode that the orchestrator recognizes as restart trigger.

The node shutdown allows providing the value to use as exit code by the node process, so to be allow the underlying service orchestrator to decide whether to restart the node or not.

Please remember to correctly set the orchestrator grace period (i.e. docker stop_grace_period) so to correctly handle the shutdown of the node.

docs.title: Redis backbone
docs.text:
In general, most of the Redis default configuration parameters work fine for LIBER*TP.

The only aspect that needs mandatory tuning is the Redis eviction policy: it is paramount that Redis does not evict any key in LIBER*TP database automatically.

Therefore, the parameter maxmemory-policy must be set to noeviction.

LIBERTP does not require the Redis DB to be persistent: the backbone is de-facto* the region's shared memory. So, in case of Redis failure, it would make no sense to restart the region with the backbone reset to the last pre-crash status. If Redis should fail (in the case of a non-redundant Redis setup), the regions node would:

  • complete the ongoing transactions that do not use any shared resources (i.e. a transaction that does not use any TS or GETMAIN)
  • rollback the ongoing transactions that use shared resources (i.e. a transaction that accesses a TS)
  • stop accepting requests

So you can specify the Redis config parameters save "" to avoid any DB snapshot. In case you still want Redis to perform snapshots, then you need a cold restart of the regions after the Redis crash.

docs.title: Key prefixes
docs.text:
Every LIBER*TP region stores all its data under a single prefix (the region's name). Therefore, multiple regions can coexist in the same Redis database.

However, for production systems, it is a good practice to have a dedicated Redis database for each region.

docs.title: Memory size estimation
docs.text:
Estimating upfront the size of a Redis database is quite challenging. LIBER*TP stores in the Redis database:

  • Node information
  • Resource tables (TCT, FCT, PCT, etc.)
  • Task initiation queues (ATI)
  • Shared memory segments (GETMAIN)
  • Temporary storage queues
  • Locks and synchronization structures

Most of the items above are stored as hash and lists and their size is largely dependent on the customer application characteristics.

For example, a CICS application that serves exclusively using CTG or MQ and make use of TS and GETMAIN would have a small memory footprint. On the contrary, a more traditional application serving 3270 terminals and that makes large use of TS queues would have a much bigger memory footprint, which size would be mainly dependent on the number of terminals simultaneously connected and the size and count of TS items.

Therefore, the best way to identify the memory requirements for a Redis database is to monitor the memory usage during the test phase, especially during the performance and load tests.

Please refer to the Redis documentation and work with your Redis specialist for the best configuration of the LIBER*TP backbone.

docs.title: Provisioning
docs.text:
LIBER*TP works with any type of Redis deployment model (bare-metal, VMs, containerized, k8s, SaaS, etc.). So, for example, Redis could be equally deployed as a container in the same k8s cluster or be made available by a SaaS service, such as AWS ElastiCache.

Ergo, customers can use their preferred deployment option.

docs.title: High Availability
docs.text:
LIBER*TP supports all standard Redis deployment models. The selected model determines the level of availability, scalability, and operational complexity of the backbone.

Redis can operate in three main configurations:

  • Standalone
  • Sentinel
  • Cluster

docs.title: Standalone
docs.text:
A single Redis instance without replication or failover.

Characteristics:

  • Single master node
  • No replicas
  • No automatic failover
  • Simplest deployment model

If the Redis instance fails:

  • The backbone becomes unavailable
  • LIBER*TP nodes stop accepting new requests
  • Ongoing transactions using shared resources are rolled back

Standalone mode is suitable for:

  • Development environments
  • Testing
  • Non-critical workloads

This configuration is not recommended for production systems.

docs.title: Sentinel
docs.text:
Redis Sentinel provides high availability for a single Redis master instance.

Typical topology:

  • 1 master
  • 1 or more replicas
  • 3 Sentinel processes (recommended minimum)

Sentinel continuously monitors the master and replicas. If the master fails:

  • A replica is automatically promoted to master
  • Clients reconnect to the new master
  • No manual intervention is required

Characteristics:

  • High availability
  • No data sharding
  • Single logical dataset
  • Minimal architectural impact on key design

This is the recommended configuration for production in the vast majority of the cases.

docs.title: Cluster
docs.text:
Redis Cluster provides horizontal scalability and optional high availability.

Typical topology:

  • Minimum 3 master nodes
  • Optional replicas per master (recommended for HA)

Characteristics:

  • The keyspace is divided into 16384 hash slots
  • Each master owns a subset of slots
  • Data is automatically sharded across nodes
  • Replicas can be promoted if a master fails

If a master fails:

  • With replicas configured → automatic failover occurs
  • Without replicas → the slots owned by that master become unavailable

Cluster mode introduces additional architectural considerations:

  • Key design affects data placement
  • Multi-key operations are only possible if keys belong to the same hash slot
  • Operational complexity is higher than Standalone or Sentinel

This configuration is supported by LIBER*TP but not necessary for production. The sentinel option fits better the characteristics of the backbone.

docs.title: Cluster behind a proxy
docs.text:
In some cases, a Redis Cluster can be deployed behind a proxy (i.e. GCP Memorystore for Redis Cluster or Azure Cache for Redis Premium Cluster), so the client connects the proxy and not the cluster directly.

This approach introduces some limitations and a performance penalty, due to the added proxy latency.

When using Redis as a service, we recommend using a simple replica-based High Availability setup for production systems.

docs.title: Sharding Strategy
docs.text:
When Redis Cluster mode is enabled, LIBER*TP allows control over key distribution across shards through the configuration parameter shardingStrategy.

The following options are available:

  • None: Default Redis Cluster behavior. Keys are distributed across the cluster according to the standard Redis hash slot calculation, without forcing colocation of related keys.
  • Region: All keys belonging to the same region are forced into the same shard. This keeps the entire region dataset colocated on a single Redis Cluster node.
  • ResourceGroup: Keys are grouped by resource group. Resources sharing the same path or logical group are colocated in the same shard, while still allowing distribution across the cluster.
  • Resource: Each individual resource key is forced into a single shard based on its full resource identity. This ensures that all operations related to the same specific resource are always directed to the same shard.

Choosing the appropriate strategy depends on the expected workload:

  • None provides the most even distribution of data across the cluster.
  • Region maximizes locality for a whole region, but may concentrate all its data on a single shard.
  • ResourceGroup offers a compromise between locality and distribution by grouping related resources together.
  • Resource provides the finest-grained colocation, keeping each specific resource self-contained on a single shard.

docs.title: Services
docs.text:
LIBER*TP can be clustered and load balanced. Its services are stateless and can be scaled horizontally.

LIBER*TP exposes stateless service endpoints as web services.

The addresses of these services are defined in the supernaut configuration file.

By deployment standpoint, such services can be treated like any standard web services and, therefore can be exposed behind a reverse proxy (i.e. NGINIX) and/or a load balancer (Level 7)

docs.title: TN3270
docs.text:
The TN3270 service that complements LIBER*TP can be clustered and load balanced too.

But it is services are stateful therefore require a level 4 load balancer.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
LIBER*TP supports the following interfaces:

  • LINK
  • TN3270
  • JMS
  • CICS MQ Adapter
  • CICS Web Interface (CWI)
  • CICS Web Services
  • CICS Transaction Gateway (CTG)
  • EZASOKET
  • Web Terminal Apps
  • 3270 Printers emulation
  • SPOOL emulation
  • REST API

docs.title: Link
docs.text:
Link Web Service can be used to start COMMAREA based programs or transactions in the cluster using plain REST JSON web services.

User cases: - CTG ECI replacement - IMS/Connect replacement - EXCI API replacement

docs.title: TN3270
docs.text:
Using the TN3270 gateway, LIBER*TP can handle TN3270 terminals.

For further information, see here.

docs.title: CICS Transaction Gateway
docs.text:
CTG is supported using the LIBER*TP CTG client libraries.

For further information, see here.

docs.title: JMS
docs.text: LIBER*TP supports JMS messaging.

docs.title: CICS MQ Adapter
docs.text:
On Java, the CICS MQ adapter is supported via JMS.

Currently, the MQ Bridge is not implemented.

For further information, see here.

docs.title: CICS Web Interface
docs.text:
CICS Web Interface (CWI) is not supported directly. It can be replaced using native Spring servlets.

For further information, see here.

docs.title: CICS Web Services
docs.text:
CICS Web Services are not supported directly. They can be replaced using native Spring Web Services.

For further details, please refer to:

  • REST Web Services
  • SOAP Web Services

docs.title: EZASOKET
docs.text:
EZASOKET is supported using native Java API.

For further information, see here.

docs.title: Web Terminal Apps
docs.text:
Rich client can be connected like terminals.

For further information, see here.

docs.title: CICS Transaction Gateway
docs.text:
LIBER*TP provides native support for CICS Transaction Gateway (CTG) ECI through its own dedicated client libraries, available for Java and .NET.

These libraries serve as a replacement for the standard IBM CTG client libraries and require minimal changes to the existing Java application code.

LIBER*TP provides two types of CTG services (and corresponding client gateways):

  • REST Gateway Service based (HTTP/HTTPS):
    • this allows deployment behind a Layer 7 load balancer (e.g. Kubernetes Ingress)
    • supports only NO_EXTEND ECI requests
  • TCP Gateway Service (custom protocol over TCP sockets)
    • this allows deployment behind a Layer 4 load balancer (e.g. Kubernetes LoadBalancer)
    • supports both NO_EXTEND and EXTENDED ECI requests

While the REST Gateway Service is active by default, TCP Gateway Services must be configured in the region as TCP services of type Ctg.

The region must define one or more TCP services of type Ctg. Example configuration:

YAML
{
  "TcpServices": [
    {
      "serviceType": "Ctg",
      "name": "CTG",
      "address": "localhost",
      "port": "2325"
    },
    {
      "serviceType": "Ctg",
      "name": "CTGSSL",
      "address": "localhost",
      "port": "2326",
      "ssl": true,
      "keystore": "keystore.p12",
      "keystorePassword": "supersecret"
    }
  ]
}

docs.title: Java Client Libraries
docs.text:
To incorporate the LIBER*TP CTG client libraries into your maven project, include the following Maven dependency:

XML
<dependency>
    <groupId>com.nib</groupId>
    <artifactId>nib-supernaut-ctg</artifactId>
    <version>${nib.version}</version>
</dependency>

Java applications currently using IBM's CTG libraries can be easily refactored to use the LIBER*TP implementation with minimal effort.

Two gateway types are provided to support different communication models:

  • REST Gateway: Connects to the LIBER*TP region over HTTP(S) using RESTful web services. This gateway supports only NO_EXTEND ECI requests.
  • TCP Gateway: Uses direct TCP socket communication with the LIBER*TP region and supports both NO_EXTEND and EXTENDED ECI requests.

Both gateways communicate directly with the LIBER*TP region, eliminating the need for an intermediate gateway layer as required by IBM CICS™. SSL is supported by both gateway types.

docs.title: REST Gateway
docs.text:
The RestSupernautGateway class provides a CTG-compatible interface over HTTP/HTTPS, leveraging REST principles. This enables deployment scenarios such as exposing the gateway behind a Layer 7 load balancer (e.g., a Kubernetes Ingress).

Due to the stateless nature of REST, this gateway supports only ECI_NO_EXTEND requests. No specific configuration is required on the region side—LIBER*TP exposes the transactionGateway endpoint by default. This endpoint can be served behind the same load balancer used for other LIBER*TP REST APIs.

Note: Only synchronous (ECI_SYNC) calls are supported.

Example:

Java
String rest() throws IOException {
    RestSupernautGateway gw = new RestSupernautGateway("http://liber-tp.mlogica.net:8080/transactionGateway");

    var inputCommarea = "this is an example".getBytes();
    var req = new ECIRequest(
            ECIRequest.ECI_SYNC,
            username,
            password,
            "CTGCAPGM",
            "CSOL",
            inputCommarea,
            ECIRequest.ECI_NO_EXTEND,
            ECIRequest.ECI_LUW_NEW
    );
    rc = gw.flow(req);
    assertEquals(0, rc);
    assertEquals(0, req.getGatewayRc());
    assertEquals(0, req.getCicsRc());
    return new String(inputCommarea);
}

docs.title: TCP Gateway
docs.text:
The TcpSupernautGateway class offers a CTG-compatible interface over a custom, stateful TCP protocol. It can be deployed behind a Layer 4 load balancer (e.g., a Kubernetes LoadBalancer).

This gateway supports a broader set of ECI request types, including:

  • NO_EXTEND
  • EXTENDED
  • BACKOUT
  • COMMIT

Note: Only synchronous (ECI_SYNC) communication is supported.

Example:

Java
String tcpCics() throws IOException {
    Properties sslprops = new Properties();
    sslprops.setProperty(TcpSupernautGateway.SSL_PROP_KEYSTORE_PATH, "keystore.p12");
    sslprops.setProperty(TcpSupernautGateway.SSL_PROP_KEYSTORE_PASSWORD, "supersecret");

    ISupernautGateway gw = new TcpSupernautGateway("localhost", 2326, sslprops);

    var inputCommarea = "this is an example".getBytes();
    var req = new ECIRequest(
            ECIRequest.ECI_SYNC,
            username,
            password,
            "CTGCAPGM",
            "CSOL",
            inputCommarea,
            ECIRequest.ECI_NO_EXTEND,
            ECIRequest.ECI_LUW_NEW
    );
    rc = gw.flow(req);
    assertEquals(0, rc);
    assertEquals(0, req.getGatewayRc());
    assertEquals(0, req.getCicsRc());
    return new String(inputCommarea);
}

docs.title: Channels and Containers
docs.text:
Both REST and TCP gateways support the use of channels and containers for structured data exchange. The following example demonstrates how to use these features with the TCP gateway:

Java
public final static String C1_VALUE = "1234567890";
public final static String C2_VALUE = "ABCDEFGHILMNO";

public void ctgTcpSslEciChannel() throws IOException {
    Properties sslprops = new Properties();
    sslprops.setProperty(TcpSupernautGateway.SSL_PROP_KEYSTORE_PATH, "keystore.p12");
    sslprops.setProperty(TcpSupernautGateway.SSL_PROP_KEYSTORE_PASSWORD, "lafavaelarava");
    ISupernautGateway gw = new TcpSupernautGateway("localhost", 2326, sslprops);

    var channel = new Channel(CTGCCPGM.CHANNEL_NAME);

    channel.createContainer(CTGCCPGM.CONTAINER_01_NAME, CTGCCPGM.C1_VALUE);
    channel.createContainer(CTGCCPGM.CONTAINER_02_NAME, CTGCCPGM.C2_VALUE);

    var req = new ECIRequest(
            ECIRequest.ECI_SYNC,
            "REGTEST",
            "regtest",
            "CTGCCPGM",
            "CSOL",
            channel,
            ECIRequest.ECI_NO_EXTEND,
            ECIRequest.ECI_LUW_NEW
    );
    int rc = gw.flow(req);
    assertEquals(0, rc);
    assertEquals(0, req.getGatewayRc());
    assertEquals(0, req.getCicsRc());

    System.out.println("CICS CHANNEL: " + req.getChannel().getName());
    for (var c : req.getChannel().getContainers()) {
        System.out.println("CICS CONTAINER NAME: " + c.getName());
        System.out.println("CICS CONTAINER DATA: " + new String(c.getData()));
    }
}

docs.title: .NET Client Libraries
docs.text:
To incorporate the LIBER*TP CTG client libraries into your dotnet project, add the package Nib.Supernaut.CTG.

Text Only
dotnet add <your-project>.csproj package Nib.Supernaut.CTG

.NET applications currently using IBM's CTG libraries can be easily refactored to use the LIBER*TP implementation with minimal effort.

Two gateway types are provided to support different communication models:

  • REST Gateway: Connects to the LIBER*TP region over HTTP(S) using RESTful web services. This gateway supports only NO_EXTEND ECI requests.
  • TCP Gateway: Uses direct TCP socket communication with the LIBER*TP region and supports both NO_EXTEND and EXTENDED ECI requests.

Both gateways communicate directly with the LIBER*TP region, eliminating the need for an intermediate gateway layer as required by IBM CICS™. SSL is supported by both gateway types.

docs.title: REST Gateway
docs.text:
The RestGatewayConnection class provides a CTG-compatible interface over HTTP/HTTPS, leveraging REST principles. This enables deployment scenarios such as exposing the gateway behind a Layer 7 load balancer (e.g., a Kubernetes Ingress).

Due to the stateless nature of REST, this gateway supports only ECI_NO_EXTEND requests. No specific configuration is required on the region side—LIBER*TP exposes the transactionGateway endpoint by default. This endpoint can be served behind the same load balancer used for other LIBER*TP REST APIs.

Note: Only synchronous (ECI_SYNC) calls are supported.

Example:

C#
public void CtgRestEciNoExtendCommarea()
{
    GatewayConnection gw = new RestGatewayConnection
    {
        URL = "http://127.0.0.1:8080/transactionGateway"
    };

    var ca = Encoding.UTF8.GetBytes(CTGCAPGM.INPUT_CA);

    var req = new EciRequest();
    req.SyncMode = EciRequest.ECI_SYNC;
    req.UserId = "REGTEST";
    req.Password = "regtest";
    req.Program = "CTGCAPGM";
    req.TransId = "CSOL";
    req.ExtendMode = EciExtendMode.EciNoExtend;
    req.LuwToken = EciRequest.ECI_LUW_NEW;
    req.SetCommareaData(ca);

    int rc = 0;
    try
    {
        rc = gw.Flow(req);
    }
    catch (IOException e)
    {
        Assert.Fail(e.ToString());
    }

    Assert.That(rc, Is.EqualTo(0));
    Assert.That(req.GatewayReturnCode, Is.EqualTo(GatewayReturnCode.CtgNoError));
    Assert.That(req.EciReturnCode, Is.EqualTo(EciReturnCode.EciNoError));
    Assert.That(Encoding.UTF8.GetString(req.GetCommareaData()), Is.EqualTo(CTGCAPGM.RETURNED_CA));
}

docs.title: TCP Gateway
docs.text:
The TcpGatewayConnection class offers a CTG-compatible interface over a custom, stateful TCP protocol. It can be deployed behind a Layer 4 load balancer (e.g., a Kubernetes LoadBalancer).

This gateway supports a broader set of ECI request types, including:

  • NO_EXTEND
  • EXTENDED
  • BACKOUT
  • COMMIT

Note: Only synchronous (ECI_SYNC) communication is supported.

Example:

C#
public void CtgTcpEciNoExtendCommarea()
{
    GatewayConnection gw = new TcpGatewayConnection("localhost", 2325);

    var ca = Encoding.UTF8.GetBytes(CTGCAPGM.INPUT_CA);

    var req = new EciRequest();
    req.SyncMode = EciRequest.ECI_SYNC;
    req.UserId = "REGTEST";
    req.Password = "regtest";
    req.Program = "CTGCAPGM";
    req.TransId = "CSOL";
    req.ExtendMode = EciExtendMode.EciNoExtend;
    req.LuwToken = EciRequest.ECI_LUW_NEW;
    req.SetCommareaData(ca);

    int rc = 0;
    try
    {
        rc = gw.Flow(req);
    }
    catch (IOException e)
    {
        Assert.Fail(e.ToString());
    }

    Assert.That(rc, Is.EqualTo(0));
    Assert.That(req.GatewayReturnCode, Is.EqualTo(GatewayReturnCode.CtgNoError));
    Assert.That(req.EciReturnCode, Is.EqualTo(EciReturnCode.EciNoError));
    Assert.That(Encoding.UTF8.GetString(req.GetCommareaData()), Is.EqualTo(CTGCAPGM.RETURNED_CA));
}

docs.title: Channels and Containers
docs.text:
Both REST and TCP gateways support the use of channels and containers for structured data exchange. The following example demonstrates how to use these features with the TCP gateway:

C#
public void CtgTcpSslEciChannel()
{
    Dictionary<string, string> sslprops = new();
    sslprops[TcpGatewayConnection.SSL_PROP_KEYSTORE_PATH] = "keystore.p12";
    sslprops[TcpGatewayConnection.SSL_PROP_KEYSTORE_PASSWORD] = "lafavaelarava";
    GatewayConnection gw = new TcpGatewayConnection("localhost", 2326, sslprops);
    var channel = new Nib.Supernaut.Ctg.Channel(CTGCCPGM.CHANNEL_NAME);
    channel.CreateContainer(CTGCCPGM.CONTAINER_01_NAME, CTGCCPGM.C1_VALUE);
    channel.CreateContainer(CTGCCPGM.CONTAINER_02_NAME, CTGCCPGM.C2_VALUE);

    var req = new EciRequest();
    req.SyncMode = EciRequest.ECI_SYNC;
    req.UserId = "REGTEST";
    req.Password = "regtest";
    req.Program = "CTGCCPGM";
    req.TransId = "CSOL";
    req.ExtendMode = EciExtendMode.EciNoExtend;
    req.LuwToken = EciRequest.ECI_LUW_NEW;
    req.Channel = channel;

    int rc = 0;
    try
    {
        rc = gw.Flow(req);
    }
    catch (IOException e)
    {
        Assert.Fail(e.ToString());
    }

    Assert.That(rc, Is.EqualTo(0));
    Assert.That(req.GatewayReturnCode, Is.EqualTo(GatewayReturnCode.CtgNoError));
    Assert.That(req.EciReturnCode, Is.EqualTo(EciReturnCode.EciNoError));

    Console.WriteLine("CICS CHANNEL : " + req.Channel.Name);
    Assert.That(req.Channel.Name, Is.EqualTo(CTGCCPGM.CHANNEL_NAME));
    Assert.That(req.Channel.Containers.Count, Is.EqualTo(3));
    foreach (var c in req.Channel.Containers)
    {
        if (c.Name.Equals(CTGCCPGM.CONTAINER_01_NAME))
        {
            Assert.That(Encoding.UTF8.GetString(c.Data), Is.EqualTo(CTGCCPGM.C1_VALUE));
        }
        else if (c.Name.Equals(CTGCCPGM.CONTAINER_03_NAME))
        {
            Assert.That(Encoding.UTF8.GetString(c.Data), Is.EqualTo(CTGCCPGM.C3_VALUE));
        }
        else if (c.Name.Equals(CTGCCPGM.CONTAINER_02_NAME))
        {
            //assertEquals("Server response #2", new String(c.getData()));
        }
        else
        {
            Assert.Fail("Unexpected container: " + c.Name);
        }
    }
}

docs.title: CICS Web Interface
docs.text:
As described on IBM Manuals, Web-aware application programs in CICS utilize EXEC CICS WEB commands to interact with a web client or server through CICS: When acting as an HTTP server, CICS programs can receive, analyze HTTP requests, and provide application-generated responses to the web client. When acting as an HTTP client, CICS programs can communicate with an HTTP server on the Internet. A user-written application program sends requests through CICS to the HTTP server and receives the responses from it.

The CICS WEB interface, both client and server, is intricate and encompasses:

  • Numerous commands that primarily analyze the HTTP request and prepare an HTTP response:
    • EXTRACT WEB
    • WEB CLOSE
    • WEB CONVERSE
    • WEB ENDBROWSE FORMFIELD
    • WEB ENDBROWSE HTTPHEADER
    • WEB ENDBROWSE QUERYPARM
    • WEB EXTRACT
    • WEB OPEN
    • WEB PARSE URL
    • WEB READ FORMFIELD
    • WEB READ HTTPHEADER
    • WEB READ QUERYPARM
    • WEB READNEXT FORMFIELD
    • WEB READNEXT HTTPHEADER
    • WEB READNEXT QUERYPARM
    • WEB RECEIVE (Server)
    • WEB RECEIVE (Client)
    • WEB RETRIEVE
    • WEB SEND (Server)
    • WEB SEND (Client)
    • WEB STARTBROWSE FORMFIELD
    • WEB STARTBROWSE HTTPHEADER
    • WEB STARTBROWSE QUERYPARM
    • WEB WRITE HTTPHEADER
  • The URIMAP mechanism, which essentially maps a URL to a predefined application behavior.
  • The document service, which is not directly connected to the CWI but is often used in conjunction with CICS WEB commands:
    • DOCUMENT CREATE
    • DOCUMENT DELETE
    • DOCUMENT INSERT
    • DOCUMENT RETRIEVE
    • DOCUMENT SET

Normally, CICS WEB commands, particularly on the server side, are confined to a limited number of programs within the customer's codebase. Given the inherent nature of WEB commands (handling HTTP), allows to replace them with native Spring Servlets or web clients.

docs.title: CICS as a Server
docs.text:
The CICS HTTP server mechanism relies on the following components:

  • URIMAP: Associates an HTTP request path with specific behaviors:
    • Static content: Serves static HTML resources.
    • Dynamic content: Server programs that analyze the request and generate an HTTP response. This includes:
    • Pipelines: Typically used for additional processes, such as Web Services. This behavior is not covered by the implementation suggested here and is likely unnecessary as web services are converted differently (see the dedicated page).
  • Optional analyzer program: Parses the request and defines the converter, alias transaction, and the actual server program.
  • Optional converter program: Performs custom encoding/decoding of the request data.
  • Server program: Processes the request and provides the response.

Spring and its embedded web servers offer many features to replicate the required behavior. The best way to utilize them is to invoke them natively. Therefore, as previously mentioned, the CICS server programs will be:

  1. Automatically converted to Java.
  2. Manually adapted to use the Spring APIs.

docs.title: Dynamic content
docs.text:
Dynamic content is replaced by Spring controllers (AKA Web Servlets), which mapping is defined by the Spring @Controller notation.

The "servlet" processing in CICS, depending on URIMAP definition flows as follows:

 graph TD
;
    A[Request] --> B{Analyzer?}
    B -- LINK --> C[Custom ANALYZER]
    B -- LINK --> D[CICS default analyzer]
    B --> E{Converter?}
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E -- LINK --> F[Custom CONVERTER]
    E -- LINK --> N[CICS default CONVERTER]
    F --> H[Alias Transaction]
    N --> H
    H -- LINK --> I[Application Program]

The aforementioned process can be substituted with servlets that will handle the following tasks:

  • Directly perform the ANALYZER function or prepare a commarea and invoke the converted analyzer.
  • Execute any necessary encoding/decoding.
  • Create a task and initiate a transaction by invoking the user program.

docs.title: The Default CICS Analyzer
docs.text:
The default ANALYZER (DFHWBA*X) extracts the necessary CICS information by assuming the following structure for the resource section of the HTTP request:

Text Only
/CONVNAME/TRANID/PROGNAME</FILENAME><?TOKEN>

where:

  • CONVNAME: The name of the converter program. The string 'CICS' indicates that no converter is required.
  • TRANID: The transaction ID to be used for the alias. 'CWBA' must be specified to use the supplied transaction.
  • PROGNAME: The name of the user program to be linked by the alias.
  • FILENAME: An optional filename (or other information) when PROGNAME is regarded as a directory.
  • TOKEN: An optional 8-character field to be passed in the user_token field to the decode function of the converter program.

docs.title: Static content
docs.text:
Static resources will be directly mapped to static resources exposed either by the Spring web server or, even better, by the reverse proxy normally placed in front of the Spring App Server

 graph LR
;
    A[HTTP Request]
    B[NGNIX]
    C[Spring]
    D[Static resources]
    A --> B
    B -- dynamic urimap --> C
    B -- static urimap --> D

docs.title: Example
CICS as client
docs.text:
The CICS Web Client allows customer programs to send HTTP request and retrieve teh response, offering a certain degree of customization of the request.

In the converted program, CICS WEB client commands, may be easily replaced by one of the many Java HTTP client, such as:

  • Apache HTTPClient from Apache HttpComponents project (https://hc.apache.org/)
  • OkHttpClient from Square (https://developer.squareup.com/us/en)
  • Spring WebClient for Spring Boot applications (https://spring.io/projects/spring-boot/)

The selection depends on the options required by the original program.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
In this example, we Spring WebClient to perform a GET request to amazon.com

Java
    var client = WebClient.create();
var response = client.get()
        .uri("<http://www.amazon.com>")
        .retrieve()
        .bodyToMono(String.class)
        .block();
    supernaut.

trace("response is "+response);
    return 0;

docs.title: URIMAP
docs.text: CICS also offers the URIMAP mechanism for the WEB client API. In this context, the URIMAP serves as a mnemonic to associate a specific path, allowing the decoupling of web resource definitions from the user program. This can be manually replaced.

docs.title: EZASOKET
docs.text:
EZASOKET is a CICS interface that allows CICS programs to communicate with other CICS programs using a socket interface.

Sockets are available by default in Java and .NET, so the migrated applications can use directly the native socket interfaces.

However, CICS provides a socket listener, the EZALISTENER indeed, that can be used to start transactions via sockets. Using the GIVESOKET/TAKESOKET mechanism, such transactions can communicate with the client.

LIBER*TP provides a TCP/IP server that replicates the EZALISTENER feature. This server can be used to start transactions and the latter can communicate with the client using the native socket interfaces.

EZASOKET listener can be configured to use SSL.

To configure one or more listeners, a tcp service configuration file must be specified in the supernaut configuration file:

YAML
supernaut:
  # ...
  tcp-services:
    config: 'file:./tcp-services.json'

The listeners configuration file is a JSON file that contains an array of listener configurations. Each listener is defined by a JSON object as follows:

Field Description name The name of the listener. address The address to listen to. port The port number to listen to. userid The user ID associated with the started transactions. ssl If true, uses SSL. keystore For SSL, path to SSL keystore, in PKCS12 format. keystorePassword For SSL, keystore password. requireClientAuth For SSL, if true client authorization is requested.

Example:

JSON
{
  "TcpServices": [
    {
      "serviceType": "Ezalistener",
      "name": "LIST2323",
      "address": "localhost",
      "port": "2323",
      "userid": "EZASOKET"
    },
    {
      "serviceType": "Ezalistener",
      "name": "OTHER888",
      "address": "0.0.0.0",
      "port": "1888",
      "userid": "EZASOKET"
    },
    {
      "serviceType": "Ezalistener",
      "name": "GEEZER",
      "address": "localhost",
      "port": "2324",
      "userid": "EZASOKET",
      "ssl": true,
      "keystore": "keystore.p12",
      "keystorePassword": "lafavaelarava",
      "requireClientAuth": "false"
    }
  ]
}

The LIBER*TP Ezalistener accepts data in the standard CICS format: TRANSACTION,USERDATA,START_MODE,HHMMSS:

  • TRANSACTION: TRANSID to start.
  • USERDATA: 32 bytes user data to pass to the transaction.
  • START_MODE: KC, IC or TD. Only KC or IC,000000 are supported.
  • HHMMSS: Time to start the transaction (ignored).

As on CICS, it starts the transaction specified in the message providing a commarea matching the TCP-SOCKET-PARM structure, which is defined as follows in the class com.nib.supernaut.tcp.TcpSocketParm:

Java
public class TcpSocketParm extends NGroup {
    public final NInt32 giveTakeSocket = new NInt32();

    public static class ClientId extends NGroup {
        public final NInt32 lstnCidDomain = new NInt32();
        public final NChar lstnCidName = new NChar(8);
        public final NChar lstnCidTask = new NChar(8);
        public final NChar lstnCidRsvd = new NChar(20);
    }

    public final ClientId clientId = new ClientId();
    public final NChar clientInData = new NChar(35);
    public final NChar filler = new NChar(1);

    public static class SockAddrInParm extends NGroup {
        public final NInt32 sinFamily = new NInt32();
        public final NInt32 sinPort = new NInt32();
        public final NInt32 sinAddress = new NInt32();
        public final NChar sinZero = new NChar(8);
    }

    public final SockAddrInParm sockAddrInParm = new SockAddrInParm();
}

docs.title: Started transaction
docs.text:
The started program can use the sockets passed by the listener using the EzaSocket class.

Example:

Java
public class EZASRV01 extends AbstractOnlineProgram {
    public EZASRV01(Context rt) {
        super(rt);
    }

    class WorkingStorage extends NGroup {
        NInt16 len = new NInt16();
        TcpSocketParm tcpSockParm = new TcpSocketParm();
    }

    @ProgramStorage
    final WorkingStorage WS = new WorkingStorage();

    @Override
    protected int procedure(AbstractNField dfhcommarea) throws ContextException {
        WS.len.setValue(WS.tcpSockParm.length());
        supernaut.retrieve()
                .into(WS.tcpSockParm)
                .length(WS.len)
                .nohandle()
                .exec();
        try {
            var socket = EzaSocket.takesocket(WS.tcpSockParm.giveTakeSocket.getInt());
            socket.getSocket().getOutputStream().write("MESSAGE FROM EZASERVER".getBytes());
            socket.close();
        } catch (IOException e) {
            throw new AbendException("Error while taking socket: " + e.getMessage());
        }
        return RETURN_CODE;
    }
}

docs.title: MQ Adapter
docs.text:
MQ access is facilitated through the Spring/JMS API. While this approach offers limited flexibility compared to the native MQ API, it provides significant future benefits:

  • It is recommended by IBM as the preferred method for accessing MQ from Java.
  • It enables support for alternative messaging protocols (e.g., ActiveMQ).

Access to MQ is ensured by the JMS Connection Factory provided by IBM's mq-jms-spring-boot-starter package.

docs.title: CICS MQ Adapter
docs.text:
The MQ Adapter is implemented using a JMS Message Listener, which listens for MQ messages on the trigger queue. Upon receiving a message, it initiates the specified transaction, allowing access to the MQ queue using Spring JMS APIs (via JmsTemplate).

The message posted on the trigger queue must be in MQTM struct V1 format.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
To configure MQ/Adapters, the folollowing settings must be updated.

  1. LIBER*TP Configuration properties
  2. MQ Queue Manager properties
  3. Trigger Monitors configuration

docs.title: LIBER*TP configuration
docs.text:
To enable MQ, the region configuration must include the setting supernaut.jms.ibm-mq=true. Once MQ is activated, the region will initialize the MQ AutoConfiguration service, which establishes XA connections to the queue manager specified in the MQ properties (outlined below).

Enabling MQ allows user programs to access the JMS APIs and communicate with MQ.

YAML
supernaut:
  jms:
    ibm-mq: true
    trigger-monitors: 'file:./mq-trigger-monitors.json'

docs.title: MQ properties
docs.text:
MQ properties define the connection details for the queue manager and are specified in accordance with the IBM MQ JMS Spring Components.

YAML
ibm:
  mq:
    queueManager: "QM1"
    channel: "DEV.ADMIN.SVRCONN"
    connName: "localhost(1414)"
    user: "admin"
    password: "passw0rd"

docs.title: Trigger Monitors
docs.text:
Trigger monitors are configured in a JSON file, which is specified in the LIBER*TP configuration. The file contains an array of trigger monitor configurations, each of which specifies the queue name and the program to be executed.

JSON
{
  "TriggerMonitors": [
    {
      "name": "TM01",
      "queue": "TEST.QUEUE.TRG.01",
      "userid": "ADMIN"
    },
    {
      "name": "TM02",
      "queue": "DEV.QUEUE.2",
      "userid": "ADMIN"
    }
  ]
}

To configure trigger monitors, see MQ Trigger Monitors.

docs.title: Example customer program
docs.text:
Transaction using MQI (including those started by the MQ Trigger Monitors) can access MQ using directly the JMS API.

NOTE: of course MQI equivalent APIs can be created on top of JMS, but as long as we don't know how big MMF should be, let's assume a deeper transformation

docs.title: Example
Printers emulation
docs.text:
LIBER*TP supports printing from background terminals through custom Printer providers. Therefore, it supports commands like EXEC CICS SEND or SEND MAP on background tasks.

With a printer provider, it is possible to tailor the handling of print data for every scenario, such as:

  • sending data to a printer
  • generate a PDF file
  • spool file to an output management system

The print provider class must implements IPrinterProvider and can be provided in the LIBER*TP configuration file (see Configuration).

The IPrinterProvider interface has the following methods:

Method Purpose init Initialize the print provider, providing the configuration string. printOrder Invoked on SEND. release Invoked when the terminal is release. getPrinterInfo Must return the printer characteristics.

docs.title: SPOOL
docs.text:
LIBER*TP supports SPOOL commands through custom Spool providers. Therefore, it supports commands like EXEC CICS SPOOLOPEN or SPOOLWRITE.

With the spool provider it is possible to tailor the process for every scenario, such as:

  • sending data to a printer
  • generate a PDF file
  • compose a document with a document composition tool
  • spool file to an output management system
  • submit jobs
