The spool provider class must implement ISpoolProvider and can be provided in the LIBER*TP configuration file (see Configuration).

The ISpoolProvider interface has the following methods:

Method Purpose init Initialize the spool provider, providing the configuration string. openInput Invoked at SPOOLOPEN INPUT. openOutput Invoked at SPOOLOPEN OUTPUT. write Invoked at SPOOLWRITE, is invoked passing the data written by the program. read Invoked at SPOOLREAD, must return the data for the program. close Invoked at SPOOLCLOSE.

docs.title: TN3270
docs.text:
The TN3270 service is a standalone gateway that allows to connect TN3270 terminals to a LIBER*TP region.

It converts traditional TN3270 terminal data streams into a LIBER*TP region REST requests.

The communication between terminals and the tn3270 service is done via TN3270 data streams over a permanent TCP socket. On the other side, the communication between the TN3270 service and the LIBER*TP region is done via REST JSON web services.

This means that between the TN3270 service and the LIBER*TP region one or more load balancers can be used to distribute the load over the supernaut cluster.

The tn3270 service can be replicated and put behind a network load balancer to distribute the load over multiple tn3270 services.

IMPORTANT: the TN3270 connections are permanent, therefore, once a TN3270 terminal is connected to a TN3270 service, it will remain connected to the same service node until the terminal is disconnected. SO if the tn3270 node is closed, the terminal will be disconnected.

docs.title: Monitoring
docs.text:
The tn3270 service exposes observability metrics compatible with standard monitoring technologies, such as Prometheus, Grafana, etc.

It implements observability using Micrometer. It exposes the following observation points:

  • tn3270.terminals.count: The number of terminals currently connected to the TN3270 service.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
The master configuration is contained in the Spring application properties, and refers to the resource definitions in the collateral configuration files.

The application configuration follows the general Spring Boot configuration structure and rules. Therefore, multiple configuration files can be used to define different profiles and configurations.

The configuration properties are prefixed with tn3270 and are used to configure the LIBER*TP subsystems and components.

Property Description Default tn3270.service.address The address where the TN3270 service listens for connections localhost tn3270.service.port The port where the TN3270 service listens for connections 3270 tn3270.service.ssl.enabled Enable SSL for the TN3270 service false tn3270.service.ssl.keystore The keystore file for the SSL connection - tn3270.service.ssl.password The password for the keystore file - tn3270.protocol.welcome Enable the welcome message true tn3270.protocol.tn3270e Enable the TN3270E protocol true tn3270.protocol.uppercase Force uppercase for all terminals false tn3270.protocol.timeout The timeout (in seconds) for the TN3270 connections 0 tn3270.protocol.http-timeout The timeout (in seconds) for the HTTP connections 240 tn3270.protocol.codepage The codepage for the TN3270 connection embedded ENUS037 tn3270.regions.default The default region for the TN3270 service -

docs.title: Example Configuration
docs.text:
YAML
spring:
  application:
    name: aws-carddemo-tn3270-server
  profiles:
    active: dev

tn3270:
  service:
    address: "0.0.0.0"
    port: 3270
    ssl:
      enabled: false
      keystore: ""
      password: ""
  protocol:
    welcome: true
    # set false for rumba
    tn3270e: false
    uppercase: false
    timeout: 0
    http-timeout: 600
    codepage: "classpath:/codepages/ENUS037.cpg"
  regions:
    default: "http://supernaut:8080/tn3270"
logging:
  level:
    com.nib: DEBUG

management:
  endpoints:
    web:
      exposure:
        include: "*"
  metrics:
    enable:
      tomcat: false
      jvm: true
      process: true
      hikaricp: false
      system: true
      jdbc: false
      http: true
      logback: true
    tags:
      tn3270: ${spring.application.name}
      environment: "test"
  #    distribution:
  #      percentiles-histogram:
  #        http:
  #          server:
  #            requests=true:
  tracing:
    enabled: true
    sampling:
      probability=: 1.0
  zipkin:
    tracing:
      endpoint: http://tempo:9411/api/v2/spans

docs.title: API
docs.text: The tn3270 service exposes a REST API with the following endpoint:

docs.title: GET /status
docs.text:
• Description: Retrieves the status of the TN3270 service, including the number of terminals currently connected.
  • Response:
    • Status Code: 202 Accepted
    • Body: A JSON object containing the number of terminals connected.
      • Example: JSON
        {
  "terminalsConnected": 5
}

docs.title: Example Request
docs.text:
HTTP
GET /status HTTP/1.1
Host: <server-host>
Accept: application/json

docs.title: Example Response
Example Request with curl
docs.text:
To retrieve the status of the TN3270 service using curl, you can use the following command:

Bash
curl -X GET http://<server-host>/status -H "Accept: application/json"

Replace <server-host> with the actual host address of your TN3270 service.

docs.title: CICS Web Services
docs.text:
CICS Web Services are replaced by standard mechanisms provided by the Spring/Boot and the Java ecosystem.

For further details, please refer to:

  • REST Web Services
  • SOAP Web Services

docs.title: Web Terminal Apps
docs.text:
LIBER*TP exposes a rich client REST API that can be used to exchange screen communication over web services.

Using this interface and the nib-angular package it is possible to build Angular front-end application called "Web Terminal Apps".

In these apps, traditional screens are replaced by web pages that implement the application screens and interact with the LIBER*TP region, replacing the legacy TN3270 terminal with a modern web front-end.

At conversion time, BMS macros are converted in HTML pages to be deployed in the Angular app. By default, the HTML reproduces 1:1 the legacy screen layout. But it is possible to customize those pages and change their look and feel as well as enrich them with additonal functionalities (i.e. checks on input fields, check boxes, etc.).

docs.title: Node Apps
docs.text:
LIBER*TP is a set of components ready to be instantiated by the platform framework Dependency Injection mechanism.

Therefore, the LIBER*TP node itself is an app that must be packaged depending on architecture and deployment strategy.

The app is composed by:

  • the platform framework (Spring or ASP.NET)
  • LIBER*TP core modules
  • LIBER*TP backbone modules
  • Application classes
  • Dependencies

The node app is a Spring Boot app and the LIBER*TP components are instantiated by the Spring Boot DI as and when needed.

The supernaut configuration drives the creation of LIBER*TP services and beans, but the required dependencies must be on CLASSPATH. So, when the node app is built (with Maven or Gradle) must include the required components.

So, first of all, the build mechanism must include the Spring Boot and Spring Cloud dependencies. The most convenient way to include Spring dependencies is to work Maven dependency management.

Then the required LIBER*TP packages must be added:

Package Purpose nib-supernaut Core LIBER*TP components. Mandatory nib-supernaut-redis Redis backbone components. Required when Redis is used for the backbone. It includes all the necessary Spring dependencies for Redis nib-supernaut-hazelcast Hazelcast backbone components. Required when Hazelcast is used for the backbone. It includes all the necessary Spring dependencies for Hazelcast.

docs.title: Example POM
Overview
docs.text: LIBER*TP replicates the most important CICS(tm) functionalities using a fluent API interface.

docs.title: AbstractOnlineProgram
docs.text:
All online programs must extend AbstractOnlineProgram, that provides access to the LIBER*TP context through the field supernaut. AbstractOnlineProgram extends AbstractProgram, so it provides all the facilities its parent does.

Example:

Java
public class LINKPGM1 extends AbstractOnlineProgram {
    public LINKPGM1(Context rt) {
        super(rt);
    }

    class WorkingStorage extends NGroup {
    }

    @ProgramStorage
    final WorkingStorage WS = new WorkingStorage();

    LinkCommarea commarea = new LinkCommarea();

    @Override
    protected int procedure(AbstractNField dfhcommarea) throws ContextException {
        commarea.setAddress(dfhcommarea.getAddress());
        commarea.linkpgm1.add(1);
        if (commarea.isLink()) {
            supernaut.link("LINKPGM2")
                    .commarea(commarea)
                    .length(dfheiblk.getEibcalen())
                    .exec();
        } else {
            supernaut.xctl("LINKPGM2")
                    .commarea(commarea)
                    .length(dfheiblk.getEibcalen())
                    .exec();
            fail();
        }
        commarea.linkpgm1.add(1);
        return 0;
    }

}

docs.title: Context
docs.text:
LIBER*TP APIs are exposed by the program context, the SupernautContext object, that can be accessed using the field supernaut of AbstractOnlineProgram.

SupernautContext extends Context, described here, so all the APIs provided by the Context object context are also available via supernaut.

AbstractOnlineProgram has both fields Context context and SupernautContext supernaut and context==supernaut.

Example:

Java
public class LINKPGM1 extends AbstractOnlineProgram {
    public LINKPGM1(Context rt) {
        super(rt);
    }

    @Override
    protected int procedure(AbstractNField dfhcommarea) throws ContextException {
        supernaut.link("LINKPGM2")                  // CICS LINK
                .commarea(commarea)
                .length(dfheiblk.getEibcalen())
                .exec();
        context.call("PGMABCDE");       // plain call, using context
        supernaut.call("PGMABCDE");     // plain call, using supernaut
        return 0;
    }

}

docs.title: Basic Mapping Support
docs.text:
LIBER*TP supports Basic Mapping Support (BMS). Therefore, it supports commands like EXEC CICS SEND or RECEIVE MAP, and has concepts like MAP and MAPSET.

MAPSETs are represented as JSON objects that describe the maps and the fields that compose the screen, with characteristics matching those of a BMS macro.

docs.title: Mapset
docs.text: Field Type Required Description name String Yes The name of the mapset. maps Array of Map Yes The maps in the mapset.

docs.title: Map
docs.text: Field Type Required Description name String Yes Name of the map. freeKb Boolean Yes Whether the map uses free keyboard mode. resetMdt Boolean Yes Whether the MDT (Modified Data Tag) is reset. language String Yes Language setting of the map. basename String Yes Base name of the map. height Integer Yes Height of the map (rows). width Integer Yes Width of the map (columns). row Integer Yes Starting row position. column Integer Yes Starting column position. accum Enum (Accum) Yes Map type: Detail, Header, or Trailer. sync Boolean Yes Whether the map copybook is word aligned. eaColor Boolean Yes Has Extended attribute color. eaPs Boolean Yes Has Extended attribute PS (Presentation Space). eaHilight Boolean Yes Has Extended attribute highlight. eaValidn Boolean Yes Has Extended attribute validation. eaOutline Boolean Yes Has Extended attribute outline. eaSosi Boolean Yes Has Extended attribute SOSI (Shift Out/Shift In). eaBkgTrans Boolean Yes Has Extended attribute background transparency. justified Enum (Justified) Yes Map justification: Default, First, or Last. fields Array of Field Yes List of fields on the map.

docs.title: Enums
docs.text:
Accum:

  • Detail: Standard map
  • Header: Header map
  • Trailer: Trailer map

Justified:

  • Default: No special justification
  • First: Align to the first
  • Last: Align to the last

docs.title: Field
docs.text: Field Type Required Description name String Yes Field name. row Integer Yes Row position on the map. column Integer Yes Column position on the map. askip Boolean Yes Autoskip field when completed. numeric Boolean Yes Whether the field is numeric. prot Boolean Yes Whether the field is protected (read-only). type Enum MapFieldType Yes Type of the field: Constant, Input, Output, Slack. attribute Byte (hex) Yes Base attribute byte (parsed from hex). length Integer Yes Length of the field. occurs Integer (nullable) No Number of occurrences (if repeating). inputAlign Enum Alignment No Input alignment: Left or Right. outputAlign Enum Alignment No Output alignment: Left or Right. cursor Boolean Yes Whether the cursor starts in this field. filler Char Yes Character used to fill empty space. validation Byte (hex) Yes Validation byte (parsed from hex). dbcsOpts Byte (hex) Yes Double-byte character set options (parsed from hex). color Byte (hex) Yes Color attribute (parsed from hex). hilight Byte (hex) Yes Highlight attribute (parsed from hex). outline Byte (hex) Yes Outline attribute (parsed from hex). ps Byte (hex) Yes Presentation space attribute (parsed from hex). sosi Boolean Yes Shift Out/Shift In (for multi-byte support). initial String No Initial value of the field (auto-padded to length).

docs.title: Enums
docs.text:
MapFieldType:

  • Constant: Fixed value.
  • Input: User-input field.
  • Output: Output-only field.
  • Slack: Padding or filler field.

Alignment:

  • Left: Align to the left.
  • Right: Align to the right.

docs.title: CICS-like API
docs.text:
The field supernaut provides access to the LIBERTP context, that is used to invoke the LIBERTP CICS-like API.

The LIBER*TP API resembles the CICS API naming convention with Java conding style. So, for example, an EXEC CICS SEND MAP becomes supernaut.sendMap.

The following table indicates the exect mapping between an EXEC CICS verb and a LIBER*TP command.

EXEC CICS LIBER*TP method ABEND abend() ADDRESS address() ASKTIME asktime() ASSIGN assign() BIFDEEDIT bidDeedit() DELAY delay() DELETE FILE delete DELETEQ TD deleteQTd(<QNAME>) DELETEQ TS deleteQTs(<QNAME>) DEQ deqResource(<RESNAME>) DOCUMENT CREATE documentCreate(<DOCTOKEN>) DOCUMENT DELETE documentDelete(<DOCTOKEN>) DOCUMENT INSERT documentInsert(<DOCTOKEN>) DOCUMENT RETRIEVE documentRetrieve(<DOCTOKEN>) DOCUMENT SET documentSet(<DOCTOKEN>) DUMP TRANSACTION dumpTransaction() ENDBR endBr(<FILENAME>) ENQ endResource(<RESNAME>) FORMATTIME formatTime(<ABSTIME>) FREEMAIN freemain() GET CONTAINER getContainer(<CONTAINERNAME>) GETMAIN getmain() HANDLE ABEND handleAbend() HANDLE AID handleAid() HANDLE CONDITION handleCondition() IGNORE CONDITION ignoreCondition() INQUIRE FILE inquireFile(<FILENAME>) INQUIRE TDQUEUE inquireTdqueue(<QNAME>) INQUIRE TRANSACTION inquireTransaction(<TRANSID>) ISSUE DISCONNECT issueDisconnect() LINK link(<PROGRAM>) LOAD load() POP HANDLE popHandle() PUSH HANDLE pushHandle() PUT CONTAINER putContainer(<CONTAINERNAME>) READ FILE read(<FILENAME>) READNEXT readNext(<FILENAME>) READPREV readPrev(<FILENAME>) READQ TD readQTd(<QNAME>) READQ TS readQTs(<QNAME>) RECEIVE receive() RECEIVE MAP receiveMap(<MAPNAME>) RETURN returnToCaller() RETURN TRANSID returnTransid(<TRANSID>) RETRIEVE retrieve() REWRITE rewrite(<FILENAME>) SEND send() SEND CONTROL sendControl() SEND MAP sendMap(<MAPNAME>) SEND PAGE sendPage() SEND TEXT sendText() SET FILE setFile(<FILENAME>) SET TDQUEUE setTdqueue(<QNAME>) SET TERMINAL setTerminal() SIGNOFF signoff() SIGNON signon() SPOOLCLOSE spoolClose(<TOKEN>) SPOOLOPEN INPUT spoolOpenInput(<TOKEN>) SPOOLOPEN OUTPUT spoolOpenOutput(<TOKEN>) SPOOLREAD spoolRead(<TOKEN>) SPOOLWRITE spoolWrite(<TOKEN>) START startTransid(<TRANSID>) STARTBR startBr(<FILENAME>) SYNCPOINT syncpoint() WRITE FILE write(<FILENAME>) WRITEQ TD writeQTd(<QNAME>) WRITEQ TS writeQTs(<QNAME>) XCTL xtcl(<PROGRAM>)

docs.title: Response codes
docs.text:
The following response code (DFHRESP) are supported:

Reponse Code Normal 0 Error 1 Rdatt 2 Wrbrk 3 Eof 4 Eods 5 Eoc 6 Inbfmh 7 Endinpt 8 Nonval 9 Nostart 10 Termiderr 11 Dsiderr 12 Notfnd 13 Duprec 14 Dupkey 15 Invreq 16 Ioerr 17 Nospace 18 Notopen 19 Endfile 20 Illogic 21 Lengerr 22 Qzero 23 Signal 24 Qbusy 25 Itemerr 26 Pgmiderr 27 Transiderr 28 Enddata 29 Invtsrec 30 Expired 31 Retpage 32 Rtefile 33 Rtesome 34 Tsioerr 35 Mapfail 36 Inverrterm 37 Invmpsz 38 Igreqid 39 Overflow 40 Invldc 41 Nostg 42 Jiderr 43 Qiderr 44 Nojbufsp 45 Dsstat 46 Selnerr 47 Funcerr 48 Unexpin 49 Nopassbkrd 50 Nopassbkwr 51 Sysiderr 53 Iscinvreq 54 Enqbusy 55 Envdeferr 56 Igreqcd 57 Sessionerr 58 Sysbusy 59 Sessbusy 60 Notalloc 61 Cbiderr 62 Invexitreq 63 Invpartnset 64 Invpartn 65 Partnfail 66 Useriderr 69 Notauth 70 Wrongstat 73 Nameerror 74 Ccerror 76 Maperror 77 Nospool 80 Termerr 81 Rolledback 82 End 83 Disabled 84 Allocerr 85 Strelerr 86 Openerr 87 Spolbusy 88 Spolerr 89 Nodeiderr 90 Taskiderr 91 Tciderr 92 Dsnnotfound 93 Loading 94 Modeliderr 95 Outdescerr 96 Partneriderr 97 Profileiderr 98 Netnameiderr 99 Locked 100 Recordbusy 101 Uownotfound 102 Uowlnotfound 103 Linkabend 104 Changed 105 Processbusy 106 Activitybusy 107 Processerr 108 Activityerr 109 Containererr 110 Eventerr 111 Tokenerr 112 Notfinished 113 Poolerr 114 Timererr 115 Symbolerr 116 Templaterr 117 Channelerr 122 Timedout 124 Codepageerr 125

docs.title: HANDLE mechanism
docs.text:
LIBERTP provides a HANDLE mechanism like CICS. But, of course, Java does not have a GOTO statement, therefore the LIBERTP HANDLE mechanism is slightly different.

Two types of HANDLE mechanism are supported by LIBER*TP: delegate and ID.

It applies to HANDLE CONDITION, HANDLE AID and HANDLE ABEND LABEL.

docs.title: HANDLE Delegate
docs.text:
With the HANDLE delegate, programmer associates a delegate method to each condition to be handled. When the condition arises, LIBER*TP invokes the assigned delegate method.

Once the delegate method is the control is returned to the caller program.

The delegate method must be of type void().

Example:

Java
    void mainSection() throws ContextException {
    supernaut.handleCondition()
            .condition(Dfhresp.Error, this::handleGenericError)
            .condition(Dfhresp.Pgmiderr, this::handlePgmiderr)      // (1) HANDLE PGMIDERR!
            .condition(Dfhresp.Qiderr, this::handleQiderr)
            .condition(Dfhresp.Dsiderr, this::handleDsiderr)
            .exec();

    // invoking wrong program
    supernaut.link("MISSPGM0")              // (2) MISSPGM0 does not exist, so it raises a PGMIDERR
            .exec();
    supernaut.trace("you should not see me!");   // (4) but not this
}

void handlePgmiderr() throws ExitProgramException, OnlineErrorException {
    supernaut.trace("PGMIDERR trapped");            // (3) you should see this
}

docs.title: HANDLE ID
docs.text:
With the HANDLE ID, programmer associates a unique identifier to each condition to be handled. When the condition arises, LIBER*TP returns throws a HandledConditionException which handle ID is the one of the raised connection.

The programmer will have to handle the exception, analysing the handle ID.

Example:

Java
    static int ABEND_COND = 1;
    static int PGMIDERR_COND = 2;

    protected Flow _0000Main() {
        supernaut.handleAbend() 
                 .label(ABEND_COND) 
                 .exec();
        supernaut.handleCondition()
                 .condition(Dfhresp.Pgmiderr, PGMIDERR_COND)      
                 .exec();
        try {
            supernaut.xctl("PROGRAMX").exec();
        } catch (HandledConditionException e) {
            handleCommandCondition(e);
        }
    }

    protected void handleCommandCondition(HandledConditionException hce) {
        switch (hce.getHandleId()) {
            case ABEND_COND:
                handleAbend();
                break;
            case PGMIDERR_COND:
                handleMissingProgram();
                break;
            default:
                throw new RuntimeException(e);
        }
    }

docs.title: REST Web Services
docs.text:
CICS Web Services can be replaced exposing Spring REST Web Services, using a Spring @RestController.

The flow in the REST controller is relatively simple:

  1. The REST controller receives the request.
  2. The controller maps the request to a commarea.
  3. The controller invokes the transaction.
  4. The controller maps the commarea to a JSON object.
  5. The controller returns the JSON object.

An example of the web service controller is contained in the AWS CardDemo COBOL demo.

IMPORTANT: the package containing the controller must be available for the Spring component scan at boot time. Therefore, please make sure that - the jar package is on the LIBERTP boot classpath - the package name is included in the LIBERTP configuration property supernaut.component-scan ( see Configuration)

docs.title: Example
Spring Component scan
docs.text:
LIBER*TP requires no special configuration to enable REST web services, as it comes with Spring dependencies. But Spring must be able to scan the package containing the controller class(es).

Therefore, the packages must be on the LIBER*TP boot classpath and included in the supernaut.component-scan configuration (see Configuration).

YAML
supernaut:
  component-scan:
    org.sample.online.servlets,
    org.sample.online.webservices

docs.title: SOAP Web Services
docs.text:
SOAP web services can be replaced using SOAP-based web service provided by Spring Boot Starter Web Services (Spring-WS).

LIBER*TP comes bundled with the necessary dependencies for Spring-WS, so you don't need to add them manually to your project.

docs.title: Contract-First Approach
docs.text:
Spring-WS uses a Contract-First approach, where the WSDL file is the source of truth and Java classes are generated from it. The contract-first approach requires us to create the domain (methods and parameters) for our service first. We’ll use an XML schema file (XSD) that Spring-WS will export automatically as a WSDL.

Example of a simple XSD file:

XML
<xs:schema xmlns:xs="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema"
           targetNamespace="http://www.soap.webservices.online.sample.org" elementFormDefault="qualified">

    <xs:element name="CustomerInfoRequest">
        <xs:complexType>
            <xs:sequence>
                <xs:element name="customerName" type="xs:string"/>
                <xs:element name="customerNumber" type="xs:int"/>
                <xs:element name="customerMessage" type="xs:string"/>
            </xs:sequence>
        </xs:complexType>
    </xs:element>

    <xs:element name="CustomerInfoResponse">
        <xs:complexType>
            <xs:sequence>
                <xs:element name="customerName" type="xs:string"/>
                <xs:element name="customerNumber" type="xs:int"/>
                <xs:element name="customerMessage" type="xs:string"/>
            </xs:sequence>
        </xs:complexType>
    </xs:element>
</xs:schema>

The XSD file should be placed in the src/main/resources/xsd directory of your project (or wherever you prefer, just remember to tweak the JAXB configuration accordingly).

docs.title: Domain class generation
docs.text:
Now we need the java classes that will map the XML elements defined in the XSD file in a Java object. This is done using JAXB (Java Architecture for XML Binding). You can use the Maven JAXB2 Plugin to generate Java classes from the XSD file.

Add the following plugin configuration to your pom.xml:

XML
<build>
    <plugins>
        <plugin>
            <groupId>org.codehaus.mojo</groupId>
            <artifactId>jaxb2-maven-plugin</artifactId>
            <version>3.3.0</version>
            <executions>
                <execution>
                    <id>xjc</id>
                    <goals>
                        <goal>xjc</goal>
                    </goals>
                </execution>
            </executions>
            <configuration>
                <sources>
                    <source>${project.basedir}/src/main/resources/xsd</source>
                </sources>
                <packageName>org.sample.online.webservices.soap</packageName>   <!-- change with your package -->
            </configuration>
        </plugin>
        <plugin>
            <groupId>org.codehaus.mojo</groupId>
            <artifactId>build-helper-maven-plugin</artifactId>
            <version>3.5.0</version>
            <executions>
                <execution>
                    <id>add-source</id>
                    <phase>generate-sources</phase>
                    <goals>
                        <goal>add-source</goal>
                    </goals>
                    <configuration>
                        <sources>
                            <source>target/generated-sources/jaxb</source>
                        </sources>
                    </configuration>
                </execution>
            </executions>
        </plugin>
    </plugins>
</build>

docs.title: Web Service Configuration
docs.text:
Spring-WS needs to be configured to expose the web service. This is done by creating a configuration class that implements WsConfigurer and defines a bean of type DefaultWsdl11Definition for each web service you want to expose.

Example configuration class:

Java
@Configuration
@EnableWs
public class SoapWebServicesConfig implements WsConfigurer {
    public static final String NAMESPACE_URI = "http://www.soap.webservices.online.sample.org";

    @Bean
    public ServletRegistrationBean<MessageDispatcherServlet> messageDispatcher(ApplicationContext ctx) {
        var servlet = new MessageDispatcherServlet();
        servlet.setApplicationContext(ctx);
        servlet.setTransformWsdlLocations(true);
        return new ServletRegistrationBean<>(servlet, "/ws/*");
    }

    @Bean(name = "customerInfo")
    public DefaultWsdl11Definition defaultWsdl11Definition(XsdSchema customerInfoSchema) {
        DefaultWsdl11Definition wsdl11Definition = new DefaultWsdl11Definition();
        wsdl11Definition.setPortTypeName("CustomerInfoPort");
        wsdl11Definition.setLocationUri("/ws/customerInfo");
        wsdl11Definition.setTargetNamespace(NAMESPACE_URI);
        wsdl11Definition.setSchema(customerInfoSchema);
        return wsdl11Definition;
    }

    @Bean
    public XsdSchema customerInfoSchema() {
        return new SimpleXsdSchema(new ClassPathResource("xsd/CustomerInfo.xsd"));
    }
}

In this example, we define a web service with the name customerInfo that will be available at the URL /ws/customerInfo. The WSDL will be generated automatically based on the XSD schema defined in the customerInfoSchema bean.

docs.title: Endpoint Implementation
docs.text:
Finally, we need to implement the endpoint that will handle the requests and responses for our web service. This is done by creating a class annotated with @Endpoint and defining methods annotated with @PayloadRoot to handle specific operations.

The flow in the web service endpoint implementation is relatively simple:

  1. The endpoint receives the request payload.
  2. The endpoint maps the request to a commarea.
  3. The endpoint invokes the transaction.
  4. The endpoint maps the commarea to the response payload.
  5. The endpoint returns payload.

You can have multiple endpoints in a single class or separate classes for better organization.

Example endpoint class:

Java
@Endpoint
public class CustomerInfoEndpoint {
    private static Logger log = LoggerFactory.getLogger(CustomerInfoEndpoint.class);

    @Autowired
    private SupernautRegion region;

    @PayloadRoot(namespace = SoapWebServicesConfig.NAMESPACE_URI, localPart = "CustomerInfoRequest")
    @ResponsePayload
    public CustomerInfoResponse customerInfo(@RequestPayload CustomerInfoRequest request) {
        log.info("SOAP request {}", request);

        try {
            org.sample.online.testapp.copybooks.CUSTINFO ca = new org.sample.online.testapp.copybooks.CUSTINFO();
            ca.allocate();
            ca.customerName.setValue(request.getCustomerName());
            ca.customerNo.setValue(request.getCustomerNumber());
            ca.message.setValue(request.getCustomerMessage());
            //
            // executing the desired program
            //
            try {
                BackgroundTerminalHandler bkgTerm = new BackgroundTerminalHandler(region, null);
                User user = null; //region.authenticate(req.getUserid(), req.getPassword());
                Task t = region.newTask(StartCode.Dpl, bkgTerm, new WebServicePctEntry("CXWU", CUSTINFO.class.getName()))
                        .data(ca.getAddress().getStorage().getBytes(), ca.length());
                if (user != null) {
                    t.user(user);
                }
                var cr = t.run();
                if (!cr.isNormal()) {
                    OnlineExceptionFactory.throwExceptionFor(cr.getResp());
                }
                ca.setBytes(cr.getData(), cr.getLength());
            } catch (NotauthException e) {
                log.error("cics web service not authorized");
                throw new ResponseStatusException(HttpStatus.UNAUTHORIZED, e.getMessage(), e);
            } catch (Exception e) {
                log.error("cics web service failed: {}", e.getMessage());
                throw new ResponseStatusException(HttpStatus.BAD_REQUEST, e.getMessage(), e);
            }

            //
            // moving COMMAREA in the response object
            //
            CustomerInfoResponse response = new CustomerInfoResponse();
            response.setCustomerName(ca.customerName.getValue().trim());
            response.setCustomerNumber(ca.customerNo.getInt());
            response.setCustomerMessage(ca.message.getValue().trim());
            return response;
        } catch (NotauthException e) {
            log.error("cics web service not authorized");
            throw new ResponseStatusException(HttpStatus.UNAUTHORIZED, e.getMessage(), e);
        } catch (Exception e) {
            log.error("cics web service failed: {}", e.getMessage());
            throw new ResponseStatusException(HttpStatus.BAD_REQUEST, e.getMessage(), e);
        }
    }
}

docs.title: Spring Component scan
docs.text:
LIBER*TP requires no special configuration to enable SOAP web services, as it comes with Spring-WS dependencies. But Spring must be able to scan the package containing the endpoint class(es).

Therefore, the packages must be on the LIBER*TP boot classpath and included in the supernaut.component-scan configuration (see Configuration).

YAML
supernaut:
  component-scan:
    org.sample.online.servlets,
    org.sample.online.webservices

docs.title: Mixing up SOAP 1.1 and 1.2
docs.text: By default, Spring-WS exposes SOAP 1.1 endpoints. If you need to expose SOAP 1.2 endpoints, you can do so following the example below:

docs.title: SOAP Configuration
docs.text:
You must create two different servlets, one for SOAP 1.1 and one for SOAP 1.2, and two different Message Factories.

Java
@Configuration
@EnableWs
public class SoapWebServicesConfig implements WsConfigurer {
    @Bean
    public ServletRegistrationBean<MessageDispatcherServlet> soap11Dispatcher(ApplicationContext ctx) {
        var servlet = new MessageDispatcherServlet();
        servlet.setApplicationContext(ctx);
        servlet.setTransformWsdlLocations(true);
        servlet.setMessageFactoryBeanName("soap11MessageFactory");
        var reg = new ServletRegistrationBean<>(servlet, "/ws/soap11/*");
        reg.setName("soap11");
        return reg;
    }

    @Bean
    public ServletRegistrationBean<MessageDispatcherServlet> soap12Dispatcher(ApplicationContext ctx) {
        var servlet = new MessageDispatcherServlet();
        servlet.setApplicationContext(ctx);
        servlet.setTransformWsdlLocations(true);
        servlet.setMessageFactoryBeanName("soap12MessageFactory");
        var reg = new ServletRegistrationBean<>(servlet, "/ws/soap12/*");
        reg.setName("soap12");
        return reg;
    }

    @Bean("soap11MessageFactory")
    public SaajSoapMessageFactory soap11MessageFactory() {
        var f = new SaajSoapMessageFactory();
        f.setSoapVersion(SoapVersion.SOAP_11);
        return f;
    }

    @Bean("soap12MessageFactory")
    public SaajSoapMessageFactory soap12MessageFactory() {
        var f = new SaajSoapMessageFactory();
        f.setSoapVersion(SoapVersion.SOAP_12);
        return f;
    }

}

docs.title: WSDL Definition
docs.text:
Define the necessary WSDL definitions, enabling both SOAP 1.1 and SOAP 1.2 bindings (or whatever combination you need).

Java
@Bean(name = "accountInfo")
public DefaultWsdl11Definition accountInfoWsdl11Definition(@Qualifier("accountInfoSchema") XsdSchema accountInfoSchema) {
    DefaultWsdl11Definition wsdl = new DefaultWsdl11Definition();
    wsdl.setPortTypeName("AccountInfoPort");
    wsdl.setLocationUri("/ws");
    wsdl.setTargetNamespace(NAMESPACE_URI);
    wsdl.setSchema(accountInfoSchema);
    wsdl.setCreateSoap11Binding(true);
    wsdl.setCreateSoap12Binding(true);
    return wsdl;
}

@Bean(name = "np100001")
public DefaultWsdl11Definition np100001Wsdl11Definition(@Qualifier("np100001Schema") XsdSchema np100001Schema) {
    DefaultWsdl11Definition wsdl = new DefaultWsdl11Definition();
    wsdl.setPortTypeName("NP100001Port");
    wsdl.setLocationUri("/ws");
    wsdl.setTargetNamespace(NAMESPACE_URI);
    wsdl.setSchema(np100001Schema);
    wsdl.setCreateSoap11Binding(true);
    wsdl.setCreateSoap12Binding(true);
    return wsdl;
}

@Bean(name = "accountInfoSchema")
public XsdSchema accountInfoSchema() {
    return new SimpleXsdSchema(new ClassPathResource("xsd/AccountInfo.xsd"));
}

@Bean(name = "np100001Schema")
public XsdSchema np100001Schema() {
    return new SimpleXsdSchema(new ClassPathResource("xsd/NP100001.xsd"));
}

docs.title: Endpoints
docs.text: Endpoints stay the same, you don't need to do anything special. just remember to use the correct URL to access the desired SOAP version and to set the correct Content-Type header in your requests.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
LIBER*TP can be controlled using REST web services API. The following endpoints are available for customers to invoke program services or to control the region:

URL Method Purpose /link POST LINK programs. For further info, see here /admin POST Nodes and cluster administration. For furter info, see here

docs.title: Internal use endpoints
docs.text:
LIBER*TP exposes also the following endpoints for internal use:

URL Method Purpose /tn3270 POST Tn3270 terminal services, for the tn3270 gateway /jsonTerminal POST Rich client terminal services, for the Angular web apps /transactionGateway POST CTG/ECI services, for the CTG client library

docs.title: Endpoint
docs.text:
• URL: /admin
  • Method: POST

docs.title: Request Body
docs.text:
• Type: AdminRequest
    • Fields:
      • userid (string): The user ID for authentication.
      • password (string): The password for authentication.
      • command (enum): The command to execute. Supported values:
        • Ping: Checks the status of the region.
        • Status: Returns the core parameters of the region.
        • ShutdownRegion: Requests a shutdown of the region.
          • ShutdownNode: Requests a shutdown of a node.
          • ReloadClassloader: Reloads the classloader.
          • Set: Updates a region resource
          • Get: Retrieves a region resource
          • List: Lists region resources
        • file (FileArg): The file resource to be managed (optional, used for file operations).
        • program (ProgramArg): The program resource to be executed (optional, used for program operations).
        • tdqueue (TdQueueArg): The TD queue resource to be managed (optional, used for TD queue operations).
        • terminal (TerminalArg): The terminal resource to be managed (optional, used for terminal operations).
        • doctemplate (DoctemplateArg): The document template resource to be managed (optional, used for document template operations).
        • sessions (SessionArg): The active terminal sessions to be managed (optional, used for terminal sessions operations).

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
• Type: AdminResponse
    • Fields:
      • response (enum): The status of the command. Possible values:
        • Success: The command was executed successfully.
        • Error: An error occurred while executing the command.
        • Unsupported: The command is not supported.
      • message (string): response message providing additional information about the command execution.
      • payload (object): command result, if applicable.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: The Admin API allows clients to perform administrative tasks on the region, such as checking its status, shutting it down, reloading the classloader, or managing resource. The API authenticates the user and processes the request based on the provided command.

docs.title: Security
docs.text: The admin command is handled through the transaction CADM. The command can be secured assigning the correct authorization groups to the transaction CADM.

docs.title: Commands
docs.text: The Admin API supports several commands that can be executed by authenticated users. Each command performs a specific action within the region. The commands include:

docs.title: Ping
docs.text: The command Ping checks the status of the region. Returns a success response if the region is operational.

docs.title: Status
docs.text: The command Status checks the status of the region and returns core parameter. Returns a success response and a map of parameters.

docs.title: Shutdown Region
docs.text: The command ShutdownRegion requests a shutdown of the entire region. This command initiates a graceful shutdown process, allowing the region to complete any ongoing tasks before shutting down.

docs.title: Shutdown Node
docs.text: The command ShutdownNode requests a shutdown of a specific node within the region. This is useful for managing the region's resources and ensuring that specific nodes can be taken offline without affecting the entire region.

docs.title: Reload Classloader
docs.text: The command ReloadClassloader reloads the classloader for the region. This is particularly useful for applying updates or changes to the application's classes and mapset without requiring a full or rolling restart of the region.

docs.title: Set, Get, List
docs.text:
The commands Set, Get, and List are used to manage region resources, such as FCT, PCT, DCT, TCT and DOCTEMPLATE entries. The Set command updates a specific resource, the Get command retrieves a specific resource, and the List command lists resources.

The command resource and parameters are provided in resource-specific arguments, such as file, program, tdqueue, terminal, and doctemplate. These arguments allow for detailed management of various resources within the region.

Set and Get require the resource identifier to be specified on the field name. List does not require any parameter.

The response payload contains the details of the resources being managed, including their attributes and status.

docs.title: File resources
docs.text:
File resources are managed using the file argument, a FileArg object that supports the following properties:

Property type Description name string The name of the file. open boolean Indicates if the file is open. enabled boolean Indicates if the file is enabled. addable boolean Indicates if the file is addable. browseable boolean Indicates if the file is browseable. deletable boolean Indicates if the file is deletable. readable boolean Indicates if the file is readable. updatable boolean Indicates if the file is updatable.

Example:

JSON
{
  "userid": "admin",
  "password": "supersecret",
  "command": "Set",
  "file": {
    "name": "DATASET1",
    "open": true,
    "addable": false,
    "deletable": false,
    "updatable": false
  }
}

Response:

JSON
{
  "response": "Success",
  "message": "File 'ESDS01' updated",
  "payload": {
    "id": "ESDS01",
    "group": "TEST",
    "enabled": true,
    "fileDescriptor": {
      "dsn": "REGTEST.CICS.ESDS01",
      "catalog": "SYSCAT00",
      "fileOrganization": "VSAM",
      "recordFormat": "Fixed",
      "variableFormatType": "Ibm",
      "vsamOrganization": "ESDS",
      "minRecordLength": 80,
      "maxRecordLength": 80,
      "related": null,
      "gdgLimit": null,
      "gdgOldestVersion": null,
      "gdgCurrentVersion": null,
      "physical": "REGTEST.CICS.ESDS01",
      "inStreamData": null,
      "dummy": false,
      "nextConcatenated": null,
      "member": null,
      "cached": false,
      "keys": null,
      "keyOffset": 0,
      "keyLength": 0,
      "attributes": "{}",
      "ddname": "",
      "attributesString": "reuse empty"
    },
    "dsn": "REGTEST.CICS.ESDS01",
    "catalog": null,
    "open": true,
    "addable": false,
    "browsable": true,
    "deletable": false,
    "readable": true,
    "updatable": false
  }
}

docs.title: Program resources
docs.text:
Program resources are managed using the program argument, a ProgramArg object that supports the following arguments:

Property type Description name string The name of the program.

docs.title: TD queue resources
docs.text:
TD queue resources are managed using the tdqueue argument, a TdQueueArg object that supports the following arguments:

Property type Description name string The name of the TD queue. open boolean Indicates if the TD queue is open. enabled boolean Indicates if the TD queue is enabled. triggerLevel integer Trigger level of the queue TD queue is enabled.

docs.title: Terminal resources
docs.text:
TD queue resources are managed using the terminal argument, a TerminalArg object that supports the following arguments:

Property type Description name string The name of the terminal (EIBTRMID).

docs.title: DOCTEMPLATE resources
docs.text:
TD queue resources are managed using the doctemplate argument, a DocTemplateArg object that supports the following arguments:

Property type Description name string The name of the DOCTEMPLATE.

docs.title: Active terminal sessions
docs.text:
Active sessions are managed using the sessions argument, a SessionArg object that supports the following arguments:

Property type Description name string The name of the terminal (EIBTRMID).

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
Setting a file as closed:

JSON
{
  "userid": "admin",
  "password": "supersecret",
  "command": "Set",
  "file": {
    "name": "DATASET1",
    "open": false
  }
}

Getting a TD queue:

JSON
{
  "userid": "admin",
  "password": "supersecret",
  "command": "Get",
  "tdqueue": {
    "name": "TDI1"
  }
}

Listing all terminals:

JSON
{
  "userid": "admin",
  "password": "supersecret",
  "command": "List",
  "terminal": {}
}

Listing active sessions:

JSON
{
  "userid": "admin",
  "password": "supersecret",
  "command": "List",
  "sessions": {}
}

docs.title: cURL Example
docs.text:
Bash
curl -X POST http://yourapiurl.com/admin \
-H "Content-Type: application/json" \
-d '{
  "command": "ReloadClassloader",
  "userid": "adminUser",
  "password": "adminPassword",
}'

docs.title: Endpoint
docs.text:
• URL: /link
  • Method: POST

docs.title: Request Body
docs.text:
• Type: LinkRequest
    • Fields:
      • userid (string): The user ID for authentication.
      • password (string): The password for authentication.
      • program (string): The name of the program to link (required for Link command).
      • data (string): The input data (encoded) for the program.
      • length (int): The length of the input data.
      • payload (object): Additional payload data.

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
• Type: LinkResponse
    • Fields:
      • response (CommandResponse): The response status of the command.
      • payload (object): The payload returned by the executed program.

docs.title: Description
docs.text:
The Link API allows clients to invoke programs. The API authenticates the user and processes the request based on the provided command.

The link operation is performed under the transid CIEP. The command can be secured assigning the correct authorization groups to the transaction CIEP.

docs.title: Request
docs.text:
HTTP
POST /link
Content-Type: application/json

{
    "program":"LONGTASK",
    "length":256,
    "data":"AAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAA==",
    "userid": "testuser",
    "password": "testpass",
    "requestId":null
}

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
HTTP
200 OK
Content-Type: application/json

{
    "response": {
        "resp": "Normal",
        "resp2": 0,
        "data": "",
        "item": 0,
        "length": 256,
        "numitems": 0,
        "numrec": 0,
        "message": null,
        "qname": null,
        "overflow": false,
        "isNormal": true,
        "isOnError": false
    },
    "payload": null
}

docs.title: cURL Example
docs.text:
Bash
curl -X POST http://yourapiurl.com/link \
-H "Content-Type: application/json" \
-d '{
  "userid": "testUser",
  "password": "testPassword",
  "program": "SampleProgram",
  "data": "AAAAAAAAAAAAA==",
  "length": 4,
  "payload": {}
}'

docs.title: Region Security
docs.text:
LIBERTP enforces user security through a flexible, pluggable mechanism based on Sping web security and LIBERTP authentication and authorization providers.

The Spring web security is in charge of securing REST API access and, optionally, authenticate the user credentials. For further info on web security, refer to Web Security.

The LIBER*TP authentication and authorization providers are responsible for verifying user credentials ( authentication) and determining user permissions for accessing specific resources (authorization).

docs.title: Provider Configuration
docs.text:
Authentication and authorization providers must be declared in the LIBER*TP configuration file using the following properties:

Property Description Default supernaut.security.authentication.provider Fully qualified class name of the authentication provider none supernaut.security.authentication.config Configuration string passed to the authentication provider none supernaut.security.authorization.provider Fully qualified class name of the authorization provider none supernaut.security.authorization.config Configuration string passed to the authorization provider none

docs.title: Example Configuration
docs.text:
YAML
supernaut:
  # ...
  security:
    users:
      default: "CICSUSER"
      system: "SYSTEM"
    authorization:
      provider: "com.nib.supernaut.security.RoleBasedAuthorizationProvider"
      config: "file:./roles.json"
    authentication:
      provider: "com.nib.supernaut.security.RdbmsAuthenticationProvider"
      config: "jdbc:postgresql://localhost/postgres?user=postgres&password=postgres&ssl=false&currentSchema=online_security"

docs.title: Authentication Providers
docs.text:
Authentication in LIBER*TP is handled by a pluggable authentication provider, which must implement the IAuthenticationProvider interface. The interface defines the following methods:

Method Description init Initializes the provider at node startup with configuration data authenticate Validates user credentials and returns the corresponding user object retrieveUserByUserid Retrieves a user object by USERID retrieveUserByUsername Retrieves a user object by USERNAME change Updates a user’s password and returns the updated user object list Returns a list of registered users

Note: Providers must return fully populated user objects, including all relevant user attributes.

docs.title: Default Implementations
docs.text:
LIBER*TP includes the following built-in authentication providers:

  • com.nib.supernaut.security.SecurityDisabledAuthenticationProvider Disables authentication entirely. Suitable for development, testing, or scenarios where authentication is not required.

  • com.nib.supernaut.security.RdbmsAuthenticationProvider Uses a relational database to authenticate users. Requires a database table containing user credentials and a connection string configured via the supernaut.security.authentication.config property. See RDBMS Authentication Provider for more details.

  • com.nib.supernaut.security.JwtAuthenticationProvider Uses a JWT token to authenticate users. Requires the JWT to contain all necessary user attributes. It can be configured using the supernaut.security.authentication.config property. See JWT Authentication Provider for more details.

  • com.nib.supernaut.security.keycloak.KeycloakAuthenticationProvider Uses Keycloak to authenticate users. Requires Keycloak to contain all necessary user atributes. It can be configured using the supernaut.security.authentication.config property. See Keycloak Authentication Provider for more details.

Custom authentication providers can be developed by implementing the IAuthenticationProvider interface. This enables integration with external systems such as LDAP, Active Directory, or cloud-based identity platforms.

Note: Not all providers support JWT trusted authentication or user password modification

docs.title: Authorization Providers
docs.text:
User authorization in LIBER*TP is delegated to an authorization provider that implements the IAuthorizationProvider interface. This interface defines the following methods:

Method Description init Initializes the provider at node startup with configuration data authorize Determines whether a user has access to a given resource

docs.title: Default Implementations
docs.text:
LIBER*TP provides the following authorization providers out of the box:

  • com.nib.supernaut.security.SecurityDisabledAuthorizationProvider Disables authorization checks. Useful for testing or non-restrictive environments.

  • com.nib.supernaut.security.RoleBasedAuthorizationProvider Implements role-based access control (RBAC), where user permissions are defined in a JSON file. The configuration file is specified via the supernaut.security.authorization.config property. See Role-Based Authorization Provider for details.

Custom providers can be implemented to support alternative authorization strategies tailored to specific requirements.

docs.title: JWT Authentication Provider
docs.text: The JWT authentication provider (com.nib.supernaut.security.JwtAuthenticationProvider) enables user authentication and user attribute retrieval (such as roles, opid, and more) from the JWT provided.

docs.title: JWT Configuration Requirements
docs.text:
To use JWT to authenticate users, the identity provider must be configured to provide JWT tokens including user attributes and roles.

Roles are extracted from realm and client roles.

The provider configuration allows to indicate:

  • the claims containing the attributes
  • the client-id to refine the role search scope
  • the background task users that are obviously not authenticated with a JWT

docs.title: Region Configuration
docs.text:
Configure the LIBER*TP region to use the JWT provider by specifying the class name and configuration file:

YAML
supernaut:
  security:
    users:
      default: "CICSUSER"
      system: "SYSTEM"
    web-security:
      auth: jwt
      userid-claim-name: userid
    authentication:
      provider: "com.nib.supernaut.security.JwtAuthenticationProvider"
      config: "file:./jwt-authentication-provider.json"
    authorization:
      provider: "com.nib.supernaut.security.RoleBasedAuthorizationProvider"
      config: "file:./roles.json"

docs.title: Provider Configuration
docs.text: The provider is configured with a JSON object that defines a client configuration containing authentication claims and authorized users for a specific region or system client.

docs.title: Fields
docs.text: Field Type Description clientId string Identifier of the client or logical region. Typically used to associate authentication and authorization settings with a specific runtime environment. claims object Defines the mapping between local claim names and JWT or identity provider attributes. claims.opid string The name of the claim containing the operator ID. claims.opclass string The name of the claim containing the operator class or privilege level. claims.username string The name of the claim containing the preferred username or display name. users array List of user definitions associated with this client. Each user can have roles and optional system privileges.

docs.title: User Object
docs.text:
Each element of the users array has the following structure:

Field Type Description userid string Unique user identifier. system boolean (optional) If true, marks this account as a system-level user (e.g., service or administrative account). roles array[string] List of roles or permissions assigned to the user.

Example jwt-authentication-provider.json:

JSON
{
  "clientId": "testcics-region",
  "claims": {
    "opid": "opid",
    "opclass": "opclass",
    "username": "preferred_username"
  },
  "users": [
    {
      "userid": "SYSTEM",
      "system": true,
      "roles": [
        "admin"
      ]
    },
    {
      "userid": "CICSUSER",
      "roles": [
        "guest"
      ]
    }
  ]
}

docs.title: Keycloak Authentication Provider
docs.text: The Keycloak authentication provider (com.nib.supernaut.security.keycloak.KeycloakAuthenticationProvider) enables user authentication and user attribute retrieval (such as roles, opid, and more) from Keycloak.

docs.title: Keycloak Configuration Requirements
docs.text: To integrate with Keycloak, the following setup is required:

docs.title: Realm
docs.text:
A dedicated Keycloak realm should be configured to manage all clients, users, and roles relevant to the LIBER*TP region. Each user in this realm must have the following custom attributes defined:

  • userid
  • opid
  • opclass

docs.title: Application Client
docs.text:
This client is used to authenticate user credentials and retrieve assigned roles.

Requirements:

  • Must have a defined client secret.
  • Direct Access Grants must be enabled.

Example client configuration:

JSON
{
  "clientId": "testcics-region",
  "name": "CICS Region TESTCICS",
  "enabled": true,
  "clientAuthenticatorType": "client-secret",
  "secret": "HR2JD7nHio56v8jGYQYupAvS8FOveaxR",
  "redirectUris": [
    "/*"
  ],
  "webOrigins": [
    "/*"
  ],
  "directAccessGrantsEnabled": true,
  "serviceAccountsEnabled": true,
  "authorizationServicesEnabled": true,
  "publicClient": false,
  "frontchannelLogout": true,
  "protocol": "openid-connect"
}

docs.title: Admin Client
docs.text: An additional client with administrative privileges is required to query user details, roles, and attributes on behalf of the LIBER*TP region.

docs.title: Groups
docs.text: Users should be assigned to one or more groups. Groups help organize users and simplify role management.

docs.title: Roles
docs.text:
Keycloak roles are directly mapped to LIBERTP roles. Every role assigned to a user in Keycloak corresponds to a role recognized by LIBERTP.

To manage role assignments across different regions, roles must be defined at the client (region) level in Keycloak. While it is possible to assign roles directly to users, assigning them to groups is generally considered a best practice.

docs.title: Users
docs.text:
Users in Keycloak are mapped to LIBERTP users. When a user attempts to authenticate through CESN or the LIBERTP API, their credentials are validated against Keycloak.

Upon successful authentication, the provider extracts user attributes from Keycloak and maps them as follows:

LIBER*TP Field Keycloak Source username Username password Password userid Custom attribute userid (if present) role Client role opid Custom attribute opid (if present) opclass Custom attribute opclass (if present)

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: To enable the Keycloak authentication provider, include the nib-supernaut-keycloak library and its dependencies in the LIBER*TP classpath.

docs.title: Maven Dependency
Configuration
docs.text:
Configure the LIBER*TP region to use the Keycloak provider by specifying the class name and configuration file:

YAML
supernaut:
  security:
    authentication:
      provider: "com.nib.supernaut.security.keycloak.KeycloakAuthenticationProvider"
      config: "file:./keycloak.json"

The configuration file must be in JSON format and include the following properties:

Property Description serverUrl Base URL of the Keycloak server realm Name of the realm used for authentication regionClientId ID of the client representing the LIBER*TP region regionClientSecret Secret associated with the region client adminClientId ID of the client with admin privileges adminClientSecret Secret for the admin client (if applicable) adminUsername Admin username adminPassword Admin password

Example keycloak.json:

JSON
{
  "serverUrl": "http://localhost:9010",
  "realm": "master",
  "regionClientId": "testcics-region",
  "regionClientSecret": "HR2JD7nHio56v8jGYQYupAvS8FOveaxR",
  "adminClientId": "admin-cli",
  "adminClientSecret": null,
  "adminUsername": "admin",
  "adminPassword": "admin"
}

docs.title: RDBMS Authentication Provider
docs.text:
The RDBMS authentication provider utilizes a relational database to store user credentials and related configuration data.

It supports both password changes and password expiration policies.

docs.title: Database Tables
docs.text: The provider persists its data across the following database tables.

docs.title: Configuration Table
docs.text:
The RDBMS_AUTHENTICATION_PROVIDER_CONFIG table holds configuration settings for the authentication provider, stored as name/value pairs.

Table structure:

Column Type Description PK NOT NULL PROPERTY_NAME VARCHAR(512) Configuration key ✔ ✔ PROPERTY_VALUE VARCHAR(512) Configuration value ✔

Supported properties:

Property Description Default password.expires Enables password expiration when set to true false password.lifetime Password validity period, expressed in days 90 trust.jwt Trust USERID authenticate by web security in jwt mode false

docs.title: Users Table
docs.text:
The RDBMS_AUTHENTICATION_PROVIDER_USERS table contains user account information, including credentials and associated metadata.

Passwords must be stored as BCrypt-hashed strings.

Table structure:

Column Type Description PK NOT NULL USERID VARCHAR(8) Unique user identifier ✔ ✔ USERNAME VARCHAR(20) User's display name ✔ PASSWD VARCHAR(512) Encrypted password (BCrypt) ✔ ROLES VARCHAR(512) Comma-separated list of assigned roles ✔ OPID CHAR(3) Operational ID OPCLASS CHAR(3) Operational class EXPIRES TIMESTAMP/DATETIME Password expiration timestamp (null if the password does not expire)

docs.title: PostgreSQL Example
docs.text:
Below is an example schema and table creation for PostgreSQL:

SQL
CREATE SCHEMA ONLINE_SECURITY;

SET SCHEMA 'ONLINE_SECURITY';

CREATE TABLE RDBMS_AUTHENTICATION_PROVIDER_USERS
(
    USERID   VARCHAR(8)   NOT NULL PRIMARY KEY,
    USERNAME VARCHAR(20)  NOT NULL,
    PASSWD   VARCHAR(512) NOT NULL,
    ROLES    VARCHAR(512) NOT NULL,
    OPID     CHAR(3),
    OPCLASS  CHAR(3),
    EXPIRES  TIMESTAMP
);

CREATE UNIQUE INDEX RDBMS_AUTHENTICATION_PROVIDER_USERS_IDX1
    ON RDBMS_AUTHENTICATION_PROVIDER_USERS (USERNAME);

CREATE TABLE RDBMS_AUTHENTICATION_PROVIDER_CONFIG
(
    PROPERTY_NAME  VARCHAR(512) NOT NULL PRIMARY KEY,
    PROPERTY_VALUE VARCHAR(512)
);

INSERT INTO RDBMS_AUTHENTICATION_PROVIDER_CONFIG
VALUES ('password.expires', 'true');
INSERT INTO RDBMS_AUTHENTICATION_PROVIDER_CONFIG
VALUES ('password.lifetime', '90');

insert into RDBMS_AUTHENTICATION_PROVIDER_USERS values('ADMIN','Administrator', public.crypt('adminpassowrd', public.gen_salt('bf', 8)), 'admin', 'ADM', 'ABC');
insert into RDBMS_AUTHENTICATION_PROVIDER_USERS values('CICSUSER','CICS Default User', public.crypt('CICSUSER', public.gen_salt('bf', 8)), 'guest', NULL, NULL);
insert into RDBMS_AUTHENTICATION_PROVIDER_USERS values('ZAKK','Zakk Wylde', public.crypt('abcd1234', public.gen_salt('bf', 8)), 'zakk', 'XXX', 'YZU');

docs.title: SQL Server Example
docs.text:
Below is an example schema and table creation for SQL Server:

SQL
CREATE TABLE RDBMS_AUTHENTICATION_PROVIDER_CONFIG
(
    PROPERTY_NAME  VARCHAR(512) NOT NULL PRIMARY KEY,
    PROPERTY_VALUE VARCHAR(512)
);
go

CREATE TABLE RDBMS_AUTHENTICATION_PROVIDER_USERS
(
    USERID   VARCHAR(8)   NOT NULL PRIMARY KEY,
    USERNAME VARCHAR(20)  NOT NULL,
    PASSWD   VARCHAR(512) NOT null,
    ROLES    VARCHAR(512) not null,
    OPID     CHAR(3),
    OPCLASS  CHAR(3),
    EXPIRES  DATETIME
);
go
CREATE UNIQUE INDEX RDBMS_AUTHENTICATION_PROVIDER_USERS_IDX1
    ON RDBMS_AUTHENTICATION_PROVIDER_USERS (USERNAME);

insert into RDBMS_AUTHENTICATION_PROVIDER_CONFIG values ('password.expire', 'true');
go
insert into RDBMS_AUTHENTICATION_PROVIDER_CONFIG values ('password.lifetime', '90');
go

docs.title: Role-Based Authorization Provider
docs.text: The role-based authorization provider leverages a JSON configuration file to define user roles and the resources accessible based on those roles.

docs.title: Authorization Logic
docs.text:
The authorization mechanism functions as follows:

  • Each user is associated with one or more roles.
  • Each role specifies access permissions to a set of resources.
  • Users are only authorized to access resources explicitly allowed by their assigned roles.

docs.title: Resource Assignment Methods
docs.text:
Resources can be associated with roles using one or more of the following criteria:

  • By group: Grants access to all resources within a specified group.
  • By resource type: Grants access to all resources of a given type.
  • By specific resource name: Grants access to individual resources by name.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
The JSON example below illustrates three roles with different access levels:

  • admin: Has unrestricted access to all resource types and instances.

  • guests: Has access to:

    • All resources in the OPS group.
    • The CESN and CESF PCT resources.

    • users: Has access to:

    • All resources in the BILLING, BOOKING, and SYSTEM groups.

    • All resources of type DOCTEMPLATE, DCT, and FCT.
    • The MENU, AC01, and ACXX PCT resources.
JSON
{
  "Roles": {
    "admin": {
      "auths": {
        "*": [
          "*"
        ]
      }
    },
    "guests": {
      "auths": {
        "group": [
          "OPS"
        ],
        "pct": [
          "CESN",
          "CESF"
        ]
      }
    },
    "users": {
      "auths": {
        "group": [
          "BILLING",
          "BOOKING",
          "SYSTEM"
        ],
        "doctemplate": [
          "*"
        ],
        "dct": [
          "*"
        ],
        "fct": [
          "*"
        ],
        "pct": [
          "MENU",
          "AC01",
          "ACXX"
        ]
      }
    }
  }
}

docs.title: Web Security
docs.text:
The Spring web security protects accesses to the LIBER*TP end-points.

There are three modes of Web Security authentication:

  • disabled: no web security control is performed, all API are accessible
  • jwt: end-points are accessible only by API authenticated by valid JWT ( see End-points Protection below)
  • custom: a custom web security module is provided by the user

docs.title: End-point protection
docs.text:
When the web security authentication mode is set to jwt, all the end-point exposed are accessible only by clients providing a valid JWT token.

All end-points (including user defined REST and SOAP web services) require a JWT except the following ones:

  • /tn3270, the end-point for TN3270 Gateways (see tn3270 below).
  • /actuator/**, the Spring actuator end-point
  • /v3/api-docs/**, /swagger-ui.html and /swagger-ui/**, the Swagger end-points.

docs.title: JWT userid trust
docs.text:
When the authentication mode is set to jwt, it is possible to extract the userid from a JWT claim, to be used and trusted by the authorization providers. Optionally, you can indicate which JWT claim contains the value to be used as USERID.

YAML
supernaut:
  security:
    # ....
    web-security:
      auth: jwt
      userid-claim-name: region_userid   # the JWT claim named 'region_userid' contains the USERID

If supported by the configured Authentication provider, the USERID extracted from the JWT, can be trusted and considered authenticated.

docs.title: TN3270
docs.text:
The access to TN3270 request through the /tn3270 is always permitted also when auth is set to jwt. If you want to limit the access to this end-point, you should deploy the TN3270 service and configure ingress so to avoid exposing the end-point publicity.

When you provide your custom web security module, make sure the end-point is accessible to the TN3270 Gateway

docs.title: Custom Web Security
docs.text:
In custom security mode, LIBER*TP uses a custom provided security component, that must define how users are authenticated and authorized.

The custom provided component must be available on LIBER*TP classpath and it package must be included in the component-scan. The component must provide a SecurityFilterChain type bean.

Example configuration

YAML
supernaut:
  component-scan: org.sample.mhplugins
  # ...
  security:
    users:
      default: "CICSUSER"
      system: "SYSTEM"
    web-security:
      auth: custom

Example security component

Java
@Configuration
@EnableWebSecurity
public class CustomWebSecurity {
    private static Logger log = LoggerFactory.getLogger(CustomWebSecurity.class);
    @Autowired
    private SupernautConfig config;

    @Bean
    public SecurityFilterChain securityFilterChain(HttpSecurity http) throws Exception {
        log.debug("Custom web security active");
        return http
                .csrf(csrf -> csrf.disable())
                .authorizeHttpRequests((authorize) -> authorize
                        .anyRequest().permitAll()
                )
                .httpBasic(Customizer.withDefaults())
                .build();

    }
}
